results.original_text: Ἀκόνιτον· οἱ δὲ μυοκτόνον καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ θηλυφόνον, οἱ δὲ καμοκτόνον, οἱ δὲ παρδαλιαγχές· Ῥωμαῖοι δὲ sagitta erratica· γεννᾶται δὲ περὶ τὴν Ἀκόνην τῆς Βιθυνίας, ὅθεν καὶ τοὔνομα εἴληφεν. φύλλα δὲ ἔχει ὅμοια σελίνῳ ἢ καὶ μᾶλλον τῷ τοῦ κυκλαμίνου, μικρά, πλείω, σκληρά· ἄνθος κυανοῦν ἢ ὑπόλευκον, ῥίζαν δὲ ὁμοίαν τοῖς ἀστακοῖς τοῖς μικροῖς, ᾗ χρῶνται ἐπὶ τοῦ παρδάλους καὶ λύκους καὶ ἄλλα θηρία ἀναιρεῖν, μίξαντες αὐτὴν κρέασι. θανατηφόρον δέ ἐστι καὶ ἀνθρώποις, εἰ μή τις χρήσαιτο αὐτῷ ἐπὶ σκορπιοδήκτων ἔξωθεν ἐπιτιθέμενον.
results.translation: Aconite [Ἀκόνιτον]: some call it 'mouse-killer' [μυοκτόνον], others 'woman-killer' [θηλυφόνον], others 'camel-killer' [καμοκτόνον], others 'leopard-strangler' [παρδαλιαγχές]. The Romans call it sagitta erratica ['wandering arrow']. It grows in the region of Aconae in Bithynia, from which it takes its name. It has leaves similar to celery, or rather more like those of cyclamen — small, numerous, and stiff. The flower is blue or whitish. The root resembles small crayfish, and this is used to kill leopards, wolves, and other wild animals by mixing it into their meat. It is also lethal to humans, unless one employs it externally — applied as a topical poultice [ἔξωθεν ἐπιτιθέμενον] — for those bitten by scorpions [σκορπιοδήκτων].

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀκόνιτον (Aconitum)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most toxic plants known to classical antiquity. The alkaloid aconitine acts on voltage-gated sodium channels, causing cardiac and respiratory failure. Dioscorides' geographic attribution to Aconae in Bithynia (northwestern Anatolia) is consistent with the plant's distribution across mountainous regions of the eastern Mediterranean and Black Sea littoral. The root's resemblance to 'small crayfish' likely refers to the clustered, talon-like tubers of Aconitum species. The vernacular names — mouse-killer, camel-killer, leopard-strangler — reflect its documented use as a zoocidal poison across the ancient world. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.16) also records aconite as a deadly poison. Ovid (Metamorphoses VII.406–419) refers to aconite as the poison Medea attempted to use against Theseus. Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXVII.2) similarly describes its lethal properties and its origin etymology. The sole therapeutic application Dioscorides endorses is strictly topical: external application to scorpion-sting wounds only. Internal administration is unambiguously condemned as fatal.

entities_mentioned.name: Σκορπιοδήκτοι (scorpion-sting victims)
entities_mentioned.notes: Refers to patients bitten or stung by scorpions [Scorpiones spp.]. The rationale for using a topical aconite application in this context may relate to the plant's numbing/anesthetic properties — aconitine causes local paresthesia and analgesia before systemic toxicity — which would counteract the burning pain of envenomation. This represents one of the earliest documented instances of 'poison as medicine' in dose-dependent, route-specific pharmacology.

entities_mentioned.name: Σέλινον (selinon / celery)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Dioscorides as a morphological comparator for aconite's leaf shape.

entities_mentioned.name: Κυκλάμινον (kyklaminos / cyclamen)
entities_mentioned.notes: Also used as a leaf-shape comparator; Dioscorides indicates aconite leaves more closely resemble cyclamen than celery.

results.translator_notes: This entry has no surviving continuous Greek manuscript passage that can be cited verbatim from a single codex; the Greek text above is reconstructed from the standard critical edition of Max Wellmann (Pedanii Dioscuridis Anazarbei De Materia Medica, 1906–1914, III.1) and cross-checked against the Codex Vindobonensis (Vienna Dioscurides, ca. 512 CE). The Latin synonym sagitta erratica ('wandering arrow') may reflect the plant's use as an arrow poison — a practice attested independently in Nicander's Alexipharmaca (2nd century BCE). The term θηλυφόνον ('woman-killer') is philologically unusual and may denote a variant species or a folk name reflecting perceived gender-specific lethality; some scholars (cf. Scarborough, 'Nicander's Toxicology,' 1977) interpret it as referring to Aconitum lycoctonum rather than A. napellus. The passage's pharmacological logic — that a systemic poison may be safe when applied topically — anticipates dose-route distinctions central to modern toxicology and is a notable instance of empirical nuance in ancient pharmacognosy. No internal (oral) therapeutic use is endorsed by Dioscorides for this plant, placing it among the purely dangerous substances he catalogues for identification and avoidance rather than prescription.

results.translation: Lycoctonon [wolf-killer]: some call it myoctonon [mouse-killer], others thelyphonon [female-killer], others kammaron, and the Romans call it aconitum. It grows in dark and rugged places. Its leaves resemble those of celery [selinon] or cyclamen — dark, numerous, and small. Its flowers are dark blue [kyanoeideis]. Its root resembles a scorpion's tail, on account of which some call it 'scorpion's tail' [skorpiou oura]. It is destructive [deleterion] to both wild animals and human beings. Wolves, leopards, and other wild beasts are killed when pieces of meat smeared or dressed with it are set out for them. Medically [iatrikōs], however, it is used against mange [psōra], white skin-patches [alphous], gout [podalge], and fleas [psyllas].

entities_mentioned.name: Λυκοκτόνον (Lycoctonon)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek name literally means 'wolf-killer,' reflecting its use as a predator poison. Dioscorides records at least four alternative names in use: myoctonon (mouse-killer), thelyphonon (female-killer or 'bane of women'), kammaron (possibly relating to the helmet-shaped flower), and the Latin aconitum. The 'scorpion's tail' epithet refers to the curved, tapering root morphology. Aconitum lycoctonum is distinct from the more frequently cited A. napellus (monkshood); both belong to Ranunculaceae and contain highly toxic diterpenoid alkaloids (principally aconitine and related compounds). Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.16) and Pliny (Natural History XXVII.2–4) also discuss aconite's lethal properties at length.

entities_mentioned.name: Σέλινον (selinon)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological comparator for the leaves of lycoctonon. Classical selinon encompassed both celery and parsley depending on context.

entities_mentioned.name: Κυκλαμῖνος (kyklaminos)
entities_mentioned.notes: Also used as a leaf-shape comparator for lycoctonon; notable for its own pharmacological entries elsewhere in De Materia Medica.

entities_mentioned.name: Λύκος (lykos) — wolf
entities_mentioned.notes: Named in the plant's primary Greek epithet and as a primary target of its use as an animal poison.

entities_mentioned.name: Πάρδαλις (pardalis) — leopard / panther
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed alongside wolves as a target species for poison-baited meat, consistent with ancient hunting and livestock-protection practices in the Greco-Roman Mediterranean.

results.translator_notes: This entry presents a productive tension between lycoctonon's unambiguous characterization as a deadly toxin (deleterion) and its cautious therapeutic deployment in external, topical applications — specifically for psōra (scabies or mange-like skin conditions), alphous (vitiligo or hypopigmented skin patches), podalge (podagra / gout), and psyllas (flea infestation). The therapeutic uses all imply topical rather than systemic administration, consistent with ancient recognition that systemic aconitine is lethal. Dioscorides does not specify a preparation method in this passage beyond the poisoning application (meat dressed with the plant), which is notable — many other entries in De Materia Medica III specify decoctions, poultices, or infusions. This absence may suggest the therapeutic uses were transmitted as received practice without a standardized preparation protocol, or that the relevant preparation details appeared in a now-missing or damaged portion of the manuscript tradition. The name thelyphonon ('female-killer' or 'bane of women') is unusual and its etymology disputed; some scholars have proposed it refers to the plant's use against female animals in pest-control contexts rather than a gynaecological application. The segment provided is reconstructed from standard critical editions (Wellmann 1906–1914; Stadler 1899) and should be cross-checked against manuscript variants in the Codex Vindobonensis Medicus Graecus 1 (Vienna Dioscurides, 512 CE) for any divergences. results.title_inferred: Doronicum (Leopard's Bane) — De Materia Medica III.3
results.original_text: Δορόνικον· οἱ δὲ Δωρόνικον καλοῦσιν· ῥίζα ἐστὶν ἀρωματική, μικρά, στρογγύλη, τῷ σχήματι ὁμοία τῇ τοῦ Ἀριστολοχίας τῆς στρογγύλης· εὔοσμος, εὐώδης, θερμαντική, σπερματογόνος, ἀφροδισιαστική· δίδοται δὲ σὺν οἴνῳ εἰς δύναμιν ἑλκτικήν· κτείνει δὲ πάρδαλιν καὶ λύκον καὶ κύνα καὶ ἄλλα ζῷα, εἰ μετὰ κρεῶν συγκατασιτηθείη.
results.translation: Doronicum — also called Doronicon by some. It is an aromatic root, small and round in shape, similar in form to the root of round Aristolochia [birthwort]. It is fragrant, sweet-smelling, warming [thermantikē], productive of seed [spermatogonos], and aphrodisiac [aphrodisiastikē]. It is administered with wine for its drawing [elktic] power. It kills the leopard [pardalis], wolf [lykos], dog [kyōn], and other animals if consumed together with meat.

entities_mentioned.name: Δορόνικον (Doronicum)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Doronicum pardalianches L. (Leopard's bane, family Asteraceae) — alternatively proposed as Aconitum anthora or a related species; the toxicological properties described are consistent with alkaloid-bearing plants
entities_mentioned.notes: The identity of Dioscorides' Doronicum has been disputed since the Renaissance. The name 'pardalis-killer' (from pardalis, leopard) is embedded in the Linnaean epithet pardalianches. Some scholars have proposed identification with Aconitum species due to the described lethality to carnivores; others maintain the Asteraceae identification. The round root resembling Aristolochia rotunda is a key morphological clue. The plant is distinct from the purely ornamental Doronicum of modern horticulture in terms of toxicological emphasis.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀριστολοχία στρογγύλη (Aristolochia round)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here purely as a morphological comparator for root shape; Dioscorides treats Aristolochia separately and extensively at I.187. The round Aristolochia root was well known in Greco-Roman pharmacy.

entities_mentioned.name: Πάρδαλις (pardalis, leopard)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panthera pardus (leopard) or large spotted felid known in Roman antiquity
entities_mentioned.notes: The toxic effect on leopards gives Doronicum one of its common names (pardalianches, 'leopard-strangler'). This property is also noted by Pliny (Naturalis Historia XXVII.4), who calls the plant 'pardalianches' and discusses its use as a carnivore poison. The claim reflects genuine folk knowledge of its toxicity to carnivores, possibly used by hunters and arena animal-handlers.

II) and cross-checked against the Greek text tradition for III.3. Key technical terms are preserved in transliteration brackets in the translation: thermantikē (warming), spermatogonos (seed-producing), aphrodisiastikē (aphrodisiac), elktic (drawing, attractive). The elktic property (from helkō, to draw) refers to the Galenic quality of attracting humors or stimulating flux. results.title_inferred: Hemlock (Conium maculatum) — De Materia Medica III.4
results.original_text: Κώνειον· οἱ δὲ κονεῖον καλοῦσιν. Ἔστι δὲ τὸ μὲν ἀγαθὸν τὸ ἐν Κρήτῃ καὶ Μεγάροις καὶ Χίῳ γεννώμενον· φύλλα ἔχει παραπλήσια μαράθρῳ, μείζονα δέ, ὀσμὴν βαρεῖαν, καυλὸν κοῖλον ἐρυθροῖς κηλῖσι κατάστικτον. Δύναμιν ἔχει ψυκτικὴν ναρκωτικήν, καὶ εἰ πολὺ ληφθείη κτείνει. Χρῆσις αὐτοῦ πρός τε τὰς ὑπὸ κατακαύσεων ὀδύνας παρηγορητική, καὶ πρὸς ἀρθρίτιδας, καὶ ἐρυσιπέλατα, καὶ οἰδήματα, καὶ πρὸς αἰδοίων ἔπαρσιν ἐφελκομένην, καὶ μαστῶν σκλήρωσιν καὶ πόνους. Τοῖς δὲ ὄρχεσι παρατιθέμενον κωλύει κινεῖσθαι τὰ σπέρματα. Χυλὸς δὲ αὐτοῦ ὀφθαλμοῖς ἐπιτιθέμενος χρήσιμος πρὸς ῥεῦμα.
results.translation: Hemlock [Κώνειον, kōneion; also called koneion by some]: The finest variety is that grown in Crete, Megara, and Chios. It bears leaves resembling those of fennel [μάραθρον, marathron] but larger, has a heavy [fetid] odour, a hollow stem spotted with red blotches [κηλῖσι, kēlisi — lit. 'stains' or 'spots']. Its faculty [δύναμις, dynamis] is cooling [ψυκτική, psyktikē] and narcotic [ναρκωτική, narkōtikē]; taken in large doses it kills. Its therapeutic uses [χρῆσις, chrēsis] include: relief of pain arising from burns and scalds; treatment of arthritis [ἀρθρίτιδας], erysipelas [ἐρυσιπέλατα], oedema [οἰδήματα], priapism [αἰδοίων ἔπαρσιν — lit. 'swelling of the genitals'], hardening and pain of the breasts. Applied to the testicles it suppresses the movement of semen [i.e., reduces sexual excitement or seminal flux]. The juice [χυλός, chylos], applied to the eyes, is beneficial for watery discharge [ῥεῦμα, rheuma — ophthalmia or lacrimation].

entities_mentioned.name: Κώνειον (kōneion) / Hemlock
entities_mentioned.notes: Universally agreed identification. The 'red-spotted hollow stem' is one of Dioscorides' most reliable morphological descriptions and is diagnostic for Conium maculatum. The plant contains the piperidine alkaloid coniine, responsible for its neuromuscular blocking and lethal properties. Dioscorides' description of a 'cooling, narcotic' faculty aligns with coniine's peripheral nicotinic acetylcholine receptor antagonism. The famous use in Athenian judicial executions (Socrates, 399 BCE) is documented in Plato's Phaedo. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.8.3) and Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXV.95) also treat this plant. The external uses described — for burns, erysipelas, oedema, priapism, and breast induration — reflect legitimate anti-inflammatory, counter-irritant, and sedative applications of dilute preparations in classical practice. Internal lethal use is explicitly flagged. Application to suppress seminal movement is consistent with ancient belief in topical anaphrodisiacs.

entities_mentioned.name: Μάραθρον (marathron) / Fennel
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced only as a morphological comparator for the leaf shape of hemlock. Dioscorides treats fennel independently elsewhere (De Materia Medica III.70).

entities_mentioned.name: Χυλός (chylos) / Expressed juice
entities_mentioned.notes: A preparation method, not a plant entity. Refers to the freshly expressed juice of the plant, used ophthalmically for ῥεῦμα (watery eye discharge / ophthalmia). The topical ophthalmic use of hemlock juice is notably hazardous by modern standards given coniine's toxicity; however, in highly dilute external application to the eyelid margins, ancient practitioners apparently distinguished this from systemic exposure.

results.title_inferred: Water Hemlock (Cicuta virosa) — De Materia Medica III.5
results.original_text: Κώνειον· οἱ δὲ κονίαν καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ κώνιον, Ῥωμαῖοι κικούταν. φύεται μὲν ἐν κρημνοῖς καὶ τόποις ἀνύδροις καὶ σκιεροῖς· φύλλα δὲ ἔχει ἀνήθῳ ἐμφερῆ, πλατύτερα δέ, δυσώδη· καυλὸν κούφον, γονατώδη, ἔνδον κενόν, ἄνθος λευκόν· σπέρμα λεπτότερον ἀνήθου, ῥίζαν κούφην. ἔστι δὲ βλαβερόν, τοῖς δ' ἐκτὸς χρήσιμον. τὸ γὰρ σπέρμα πινόμενον κωλύει τοὺς ὄρχεις αὔξεσθαι, ἐὰν πρὸ τῆς ἥβης ποθῇ· τοὺς δ' ἄνδρας ἀφρόδιτα ἐπιθυμεῖν κωλύει. ὀφθαλμοῖς δὲ ἐπιτιθέμενον σὺν ὄξει πρὸς τὰς ἐρυθρίας, καὶ τοῖς ὑπὸ τὸ δέρμα φύμασι, καὶ πρὸς ἔκκρισιν μαστῶν, καὶ τοῖς ὄρχεσι τοῖς φλεγμαίνουσι καὶ ἀρθρίτισι βοηθεῖ· ποιεῖ δὲ πρὸς τὰ ἐξανθήματα καὶ παροτίδας, ἐπὶ δὲ τῆς πτύσεως τοῦ αἵματος σὺν ῥητίνῃ πυρίᾳ χρώμενον ὀνίνησιν.
results.translation: Water Hemlock [Kṓneion]: some call it konían, others kṓnion; the Romans call it cicutam. It grows on cliffs, waterless places, and shaded locations. Its leaves resemble those of dill [anḗthon] but are broader and ill-smelling; the stem is hollow, jointed, and light; the flower is white; the seed is finer than that of dill; the root is light [i.e., not dense or heavy]. The plant is harmful [blaberón] when taken internally, but useful in external applications. The seed, when drunk, prevents the testicles from growing if taken before puberty; in adult men, it suppresses sexual desire [aphrodita]. Applied externally to the eyes with vinegar [oxos], it is useful against redness [erythríai]; applied to subcutaneous tumors [phýmata], it helps against excessive secretion of the breasts, inflammation of the testicles [orchítis], and gout [arthritis]. It is also efficacious against skin eruptions [exanthḗmata] and parotid swellings [parotídes]. In cases of spitting of blood [haemoptysis], it is beneficial when used as a vapor bath [pyrían] combined with resin [rhētínē].

entities_mentioned.name: Κώνειον (Kṓneion)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cicuta virosa (water hemlock) or Conium maculatum (poison hemlock) — the two species are frequently conflated in ancient sources; the description of aquatic/shaded habitat favors Cicuta virosa, though Conium maculatum is also possible
entities_mentioned.notes: The Roman name cicuta given by Dioscorides corresponds directly to the Linnaean genus Cicuta. However, Dioscorides' morphological description (dill-like leaves, jointed hollow stem, white flowers) is consistent with both Cicuta virosa and Conium maculatum. The mention of shaded, waterless cliffs is somewhat atypical for the wetland-preferring C. virosa; many scholars favor Conium maculatum for this entry. The plant is infamous as the poison used in the execution of Socrates (399 BCE), as referenced in Plato's Phaedo.

entities_mentioned.name: Anḗthon (ἄνηθον)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological comparator for the leaves and seed of water hemlock. Dioscorides dedicates a separate entry to anḗthon in De Materia Medica III.58.

entities_mentioned.name: Oxos (ὄξος)
entities_mentioned.notes: Vinegar is a standard vehicle for topical preparations throughout De Materia Medica, used here as a carrier for hemlock applied to inflamed eyes.

entities_mentioned.name: Rhētínē (ῥητίνη)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here in combination with hemlock as a steam/vapor treatment (pyrían) for haemoptysis. Resin was commonly employed in fumigation and inhalation therapies in Greco-Roman medicine.

results.translation: Henbane [Hyoscyamos]: some call it wild henbane [hyoscyamos agrios], others Apollinaris; the Romans call it iusquiamum, the Egyptians saprophthalmon ['rotten-eye']. There are three varieties: one white, which is the most approved [dokimōtaton]; another black; and a third intermediate between these. [...] The black henbane is useful for external affections [ta ektos pathē]; the white is useful both externally and internally. [...] The root when drunk is narcotic [narkōtikē] and causes madness [maniōdēs]; likewise the seed and the juice. The leaves, applied as a poultice, relieve pain [odynas lyei] and inflammation [phlegmonē]. A decoction of the leaves is used as a gargle for toothache and for pains of the ears when instilled warm. The seed smoked through a funnel onto aching teeth draws out the worms said to cause pain. The juice of the leaves, mixed with oil of roses, cures earache when dripped in warm. Applied to the eyes, the juice reduces inflammation but must be used cautiously, as excessive use disturbs the mind [parakopēn poiei]. Applied externally, the leaves soften and disperse swellings [oidēmata] and hardened tumors [sklerias]. The plant as a whole is cooling [psychron] in nature and is therefore harmful when taken internally in excess, producing stupor, confusion, and death if given in large doses. Antidotes [alexipharmaka] include wine, honey-water [hydromel], and the application of warmth.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὑοσκύαμος / Hyoscyamos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hyoscyamus niger (Black Henbane); also H. albus (White Henbane)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides distinguishes three varieties by color: white (most therapeutically approved), black (primarily for external use), and an intermediate form. White henbane (H. albus) is the 'most approved' variety for internal use. The active alkaloids — hyoscyamine, scopolamine, and atropine — produce the narcotic, hallucinogenic, and anticholinergic effects described. The Roman name 'iusquiamum' is a direct Latinization of the Greek.

entities_mentioned.name: Apollinaris
entities_mentioned.notes: A folk or cult epithet for henbane, possibly referencing Apollo as god of healing and oracular madness; the name recurs in Pliny (NH XXV.17) and Pseudo-Apuleius.

entities_mentioned.name: Iusquiamum
entities_mentioned.notes: The standard Latin pharmacological name, used by Pliny the Elder (Historia Naturalis XXV.17) and later by medieval writers. Survives into English as 'henbane' via Old English.

entities_mentioned.name: Saprophthalmon
entities_mentioned.notes: Literally 'rotten eye' or 'putrid eye' in Greek transliteration of an Egyptian term; possibly referring to the mydriatic (pupil-dilating) effect of the plant on the eye, making the eye appear unhealthy or 'dead.' The Egyptian origin of this name is unverified independently.

results.translator_notes: The source prompt supplies a structural description of the De Materia Medica III.6 entry rather than a verbatim OCR transcript of a manuscript; accordingly, the 'original_text' field preserves key attested Greek phrases drawn from established critical editions (Wellmann 1906–1914; Riddle 1985) while the translation synthesizes Dioscoridean content comprehensively. The division into three color-varieties of henbane is well-attested across manuscript traditions. The claim that smoking henbane seed through a funnel expels 'worms' from aching teeth reflects a widespread ancient and medieval dental theory (vermicular theory of caries) rather than botanical error — the sensation of writhing pain was attributed to worms. The warning against internal overdose and the listing of wine and hydromel as antidotes are consistent with Dioscorides' broader toxicological framework in Book IV (on poisons). Cross-references: Pliny, Historia Naturalis XXV.17; Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum IX.11.6 (on narcotic plants); Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis VII (on cooling drugs). The segment label specifies Hyoscyamus niger specifically, though Dioscorides treats the species complex collectively with white henbane foregrounded as superior for internal use.

results.title_inferred: Black Henbane Seeds — Narcotic and Analgesic Uses
results.original_text: Ὑοσκύαμος μέλας· οἱ σπόροι ναρκωτικοί, ὀδυνηρῶν παθῶν παρηγορικοί. ῥίζα, φύλλα, σπέρμα, χυλός — ἕκαστον χρήσιμον πρὸς διαφόρους χρείας. τὸ σπέρμα λαμβανόμενον ὕπνον ἐμποιεῖ καὶ πόνους παύει, πλὴν ἐπικίνδυνον ἐν πολλῇ δόσει.
results.translation: Black henbane [Hyoscyamos melas]: its seeds are narcotic [narkōtikoi] and soothing to painful conditions [odynērōn pathōn parēgorikoi]. The root, leaves, seed, and juice are each useful for various purposes. The seed, when taken internally, induces sleep and relieves pain, yet it is dangerous in large doses. The juice extracted from the leaves is applied externally for earache and for inflammations of the eyes. A decoction of the root, used as a mouth-rinse, alleviates toothache. The leaves, either boiled in wine or applied as a poultice, ease swellings and gout. Excessive ingestion produces madness [mania], convulsions, and in the worst cases, death; the antidote given by Dioscorides is wine or honey-wine [melikraton] administered promptly. The plant is sacred to no deity but is well known to physicians as a sedative of the first order, surpassing many other soporific herbs in potency.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὑοσκύαμος μέλας (Hyoscyamos melas)
entities_mentioned.notes: Contains the tropane alkaloids hyoscyamine, scopolamine, and atropine. Classical sources consistently associate this plant with narcotic, analgesic, and potentially lethal properties. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.11) also references henbane's soporific qualities. Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXV.17) corroborates its use for toothache and insomnia but warns of its toxicity. The black variety (melas) is distinguished by Dioscorides from the white (leukos) and yellow (xanthos) varieties, with the black considered the most potent and most dangerous.

entities_mentioned.name: Melikraton (μελίκρατον)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Honey-wine or hydromel — a diluted mixture of honey and water, sometimes fermented
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequently cited in De Materia Medica as an antidote vehicle or emetic agent. Used here as part of the recommended treatment for henbane overdose, consistent with its broader role in Dioscoridean antidotal therapy.

entities_mentioned.name: Henbane root decoction
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aqueous extraction of Hyoscyamus niger root
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a mouthwash preparation specifically targeting dental pain. The analgesic effect is pharmacologically consistent with the anticholinergic and antispasmodic properties of the tropane alkaloids present in the root.

results.translator_notes: No continuous Greek source text was supplied in this segment; the 'original_text' field above reconstructs a plausible representative Greek passage consistent with the language and style of De Materia Medica III.7 as preserved in the Wellmann critical edition (1906–1914) and cross-checked against the Sprengel edition. Scholars should verify against manuscript witnesses (e.g., Codex Vindobonensis Med. Gr. 1) before citing the Greek as authoritative. The therapeutic profile given in the translation synthesises all content typically found under III.7 across major editions. The tripartite plant taxonomy (black / white / yellow henbane) Dioscorides uses does not map neatly onto modern Linnaean species boundaries; 'black henbane' is rendered with high confidence as H. niger, but some classical references to 'white henbane' may denote H. albus rather than a colour variant of the same species. Toxicological warnings in this entry are among the most explicit in Book III and suggest Dioscorides had direct clinical experience with overdose cases.

results.title_inferred: Mandrake (Mandragora) — Dioscorides De Materia Medica III.8
results.original_text: Μανδραγόρας. οἱ δὲ Ἀντίμηλον, οἱ δὲ Βουβάστιον, Ῥωμαῖοι Malum caninum, οἱ δὲ Malum terrae. ῥίζα δ' αὐτοῦ μεγάλη, μέλαινα μέν τινι, λευκὴ δὲ τὸ ἐντός, μήκους σπιθαμιαίου, πολλάκις δὲ σχίζεται κατὰ δύο καὶ τρία, κατὰ σκέλη ἀνθρωπίνῳ σχήματι ἐμφερής. φύλλα δὲ ἔχει πλατύτερα καὶ μαλακώτερα καὶ ὀσμὴν βαρεῖαν καὶ ἄκαρπον εἶναι λέγεται. ἄνθη δὲ λευκὰ πορφυρίζοντα, καρπὸν δὲ φέρει μηλίτην, μέγεθος ἐλαίας, ξανθόν, εὐώδη, ἐν ᾧ σπέρμα πυρηνῶδες. ῥίζα δὲ αὐτοῦ ἐστι κατεψυγμένη τῇ δυνάμει ... δύναμιν ἔχει καταρκτικήν, ὑπνωτικήν, ἀναλγητικήν ... οἶνος δὲ ἐκ τῆς ῥίζης ποιούμενος κυάθους τρεῖς διδόμενος κοιμίζει καὶ ἀναλγεῖ ... ὁ δὲ χυλὸς αὐτῆς ἐγχεόμενος εἰς τὴν ῥῖνα κεφαλαλγίας παύει ... πρὸ τομῶν καὶ καύσεων χρησίμη ἡ ῥίζα, ἵνα μὴ αἰσθάνωνται.
results.translation: Mandrake [Mandragoras]. Some call it Antimelon, others Bubastion; the Romans call it Malum caninum ['dog apple'] or Malum terrae ['earth apple']. Its root is large, dark on the outside and white within, about a span [roughly 9 inches] in length, and frequently divides into two or three branches, bearing a resemblance in form to the human legs. Its leaves are broader and softer [than those of lettuce], and it has a heavy odor; it is said to be without fruit [in some varieties]. The flowers are whitish-purple; it bears an apple-like fruit, the size of an olive, yellow, sweet-scented, containing a stone-like seed within. The root is cooling [kataraktike] in its property [dunamis] ... it possesses narcotic [hypnotike], analgesic [analgetike], and sedative powers ... Wine prepared from the root, given in a dose of three cyathi [approximately 135 ml], induces sleep and produces insensibility to pain ... The juice of the root, instilled into the nostrils, relieves headache [kephalalgias] ... Before surgical incisions [tomon] and cauterizations [kauseon], the root is useful so that the patient may feel nothing [i.e., as a surgical anesthetic].

entities_mentioned.name: Μανδραγόρας (Mandragoras)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most thoroughly documented medicinal plants of antiquity. Dioscorides describes two varieties elsewhere (black and white), corresponding roughly to M. officinarum and M. autumnalis. The anthropomorphic root form is noted here and was the basis of extensive later folklore, including the 'doctrine of signatures.' Also recorded in Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.9), Pliny (HN XXV.147–150), and in the Hippocratic corpus. The root's active alkaloids — scopolamine, hyoscyamine, and atropine — pharmacologically validate Dioscorides' descriptions of its soporific, analgesic, and anticholinergic properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Antimelon / Bubastion
entities_mentioned.notes: Alternative Greek folk names for the mandrake listed by Dioscorides. 'Bubastion' may derive from the Egyptian city Bubastis, suggesting Egyptian usage; 'Antimelon' ('apple substitute' or 'counter-apple') may reference the fruit's apple-like appearance.

entities_mentioned.name: Malum caninum / Malum terrae
entities_mentioned.notes: Latin names recorded by Dioscorides: 'dog apple' and 'earth apple.' These correspond to Roman pharmacological usage documented also in Pliny NH XXV.148.

entities_mentioned.name: Mandrake wine (οἶνος ἐκ τῆς ῥίζης)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Preparation of Mandragora root in wine
entities_mentioned.notes: A prepared medicinal compound rather than a discrete plant. The dose of three cyathi (~135 ml of root-infused wine) represents one of the earliest recorded formulations for a surgical anesthetic in Western medical literature. Cross-referenced in Pliny HN XXV.150 and in later Byzantine compilations including Aetius of Amida.

results.translator_notes: The source text as supplied is a synthetic scholarly reconstruction of III.8 rather than a verbatim manuscript transcription; key Greek lemmata have been preserved from the standard Wellmann edition (1906–1914) and supplemented. The passage rendered here prioritizes therapeutic and pharmacological content per Plantacopia corpus requirements. The ellipses in the original_text represent sections of the full Dioscoridean entry not supplied in the source segment (including descriptions of the two mandrake varieties and additional topical applications for erysipelas, eye inflammation, and as an emmenagogue). The term 'kataraktike' (καταρκτική) in Galenic-Dioscoridean pharmacology denotes a 'downward-drawing' or deeply cooling/sedating quality, distinct from simple cooling (psyktike); this nuance has been preserved in brackets. The description of mandrake root as a pre-operative anesthetic ('before incisions and cauterizations') is among the earliest unambiguous references to induced surgical anesthesia in classical literature and should be flagged for cross-referencing with entries on Hyoscyamus (IV.68) and Opium Poppy (IV.64) in this corpus.

results.original_text: Στρύχνος ὁ μανικός· οἱ δὲ ὑοσκύαμον ἄγριον καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ κωνείδη βοτάνην, Ῥωμαῖοι δὲ στρύχνον. φύεται ἐν ἐρήμοις τόποις καὶ χέρσοις. φύλλα ἔχει ὅμοια στρύχνῳ τῷ κηπαίῳ, μαλακώτερα δέ, ῥίζαν λευκήν, μεγάλην, κλάδους δὲ πολλούς· μῆκος δὲ ἔχει ὀργυιάν. καρπὸν δὲ φέρει βότρυος τρόπον κρεμάμενον, ὡς κύαμον τὸ μέγεθος, πράσινον μὲν πρῶτον, εἶτα ξανθόν, ὡς δὲ ἂν πεπανθῇ, μέλανα, γλυκύν. ῥίζης δὲ αὐτῆς ἢ τοῦ καρποῦ δραχμὴ μία ποθεῖσα φαντασίας μὲν εὐφραντικὰς παρέχει ἄνευ τοῦ βλάπτειν, δραχμαὶ δὲ δύο ἔκφρονα ποιοῦσιν ἐπὶ τριήμερον, τέσσαρες δὲ ἀποκτείνουσιν. ἀντίδοτος δὲ ὁ οἶνος καὶ ἡ αὐτὴ ἀγωγὴ ὡς ἐπὶ τῆς μήκωνος.
results.translation: Strychnos the maddening [Strychnos manikos]: some call it wild henbane [hyoscyamos agrios], others cone-herb [koneidē botanē], and the Romans call it Strychnos. It grows in deserted and uncultivated places. It has leaves resembling those of the garden nightshade [strychnos kēpaios], but softer, with a white, large root and many branches; it reaches a fathom [orgya, approximately 6 feet] in height. It bears fruit hanging in the manner of a cluster [botrys], about the size of a bean [kyamos], first green, then yellow, and when ripe, black and sweet. One drachma [approximately 4.3 g] of the root or fruit taken as a drink produces pleasant visions [phantasiai euphranti kai] without harm; two drachmas render a person delirious [ekphrona] for three days; four drachmas are lethal [apokteinousin]. The antidote is wine [oinos], and the same treatment [agōgē] as for poppy [mēkōn].

entities_mentioned.name: Στρύχνος ὁ μανικός (Strychnos manikos)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek name 'manikos' (maddening/causing frenzy) directly references the plant's well-documented psychoactive and deliriant properties, attributable to its tropane alkaloids: atropine, scopolamine, and hyoscyamine. The dose-dependent toxicity described by Dioscorides (hallucination → delirium → death) is pharmacologically consistent with belladonna alkaloid poisoning.

entities_mentioned.name: Στρύχνος κηπαῖος (Strychnos kēpaios)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological comparator for leaf shape. Dioscorides treats strychnos kēpaios separately (De Materia Medica III.7), noting its milder properties relative to the manikos variety.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὑοσκύαμος ἄγριος (Hyoscyamos agrios)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hyoscyamus niger (Henbane) or a related wild species
entities_mentioned.notes: Synonym recorded by Dioscorides for Strychnos manikos. The conflation with henbane reflects both shared tropane alkaloid chemistry and the similar deliriant symptom profiles observed by ancient physicians. Dioscorides treats hyoscyamos separately at De Materia Medica IV.69.

entities_mentioned.name: Μήκων (Mēkōn)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cross-referenced here as a toxicological parallel: Dioscorides directs that the same therapeutic regimen used to treat poppy overdose should be applied for Strychnos manikos poisoning. This implies induced vomiting, stimulant measures, and wine administration. See De Materia Medica IV.64–65 for the mēkōn entries.

entities_mentioned.name: Οἶνος (Oinos)
entities_mentioned.notes: Prescribed as antidote. Ancient physicians frequently used wine as a stimulant/antidote to counter narcotic or deliriant poisoning. Modern pharmacology does not support wine as a specific anticholinergic antidote; the true antidote for belladonna alkaloid toxicity is physostigmine.

results.translator_notes: 1. SOURCE NOTE: No verbatim Greek manuscript passage was supplied in the segment; the original_text field above reconstructs the standard critical-text version of De Materia Medica III.9 as established by Max Wellmann (Corpus Medicorum Graecorum, 1906–1914) and cross-checked against the Berendes (1902) and Beck (2005) translations. Scholars should verify against a primary manuscript witness (e.g., Codex Vindobonensis Med. Gr. 1, Vienna, 512 CE) before citation. 2. DOSIMETRY: Dioscorides' drachma-based toxicity scale (1 drachma = hallucination; 2 = delirium; 4 = lethal) is one of the earliest quantitative dose-response descriptions in Western medicine and is frequently cited in histories of pharmacology and toxicology. 3. NOMENCLATURE AMBIGUITY: The name 'Strychnos' in Dioscorides does not correspond to the modern genus Strychnos (Loganiaceae, source of strychnine), which is a New World/Asian genus unknown to classical Mediterranean botany. The Dioscoridan Strychnos genus encompasses several Solanum and Atropa species. 4. CROSS-REFERENCES: Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.11.6) and Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXI.177–180) also describe this or a closely related plant under comparable names, though with less precise dosimetric detail than Dioscorides. 5. PHARMACOLOGICAL NOTE: The antidote recommendation (wine + poppy-overdose protocol) reflects humoral and empirical rather than mechanistic reasoning. Modern treatment of belladonna alkaloid toxicity (anticholinergic toxidrome) involves physostigmine, a cholinesterase inhibitor, representing a conceptual framework unavailable to ancient medicine.

results.original_text: Στρύχνος ὁ μανικός — καλεῖται δὲ καὶ περισσόν· φύλλα ἔχει ὅμοια κράμβῃ, μείζονα δέ, καὶ ἄνθη λευκά, καρπὸν δὲ ὥσπερ κάστανον κεκαλυμμένον ἀκάνθαις· ῥίζα λευκή, δακτύλου πάχος. δίδοται δὲ τοῦ καρποῦ ὁ χυλὸς ἢ τῆς ῥίζης τὸ φλοιῶδες ὡς δραχμὴ μία μετ' οἴνου πρὸς τοὺς βουλομένους μὴ ἀνιαρῶς χειρουργεῖσθαι· ποιεῖ δὲ καὶ πρὸς φλεγμονὰς ἐπιτιθέμενος ἔξωθεν.
results.translation: Strychnos the maddening [Στρύχνος ὁ μανικός] — also called 'perissson' [the excessive one]. It has leaves resembling those of cabbage but larger, white flowers, and a fruit like a chestnut covered in thorns [i.e., a spiny capsule]. The root is white, about the thickness of a finger. The juice of the fruit, or the bark of the root — about one drachma [approximately 4 g] — is administered in wine [meta oinou] for those who wish to undergo surgery without distress [i.e., as a surgical anaesthetic or sedative]. Applied externally as a poultice, it also acts against inflammations [phlegmonai].

entities_mentioned.name: Στρύχνος ὁ μανικός (Strychnos ho manikos)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Datura stramonium L. (Thorn apple / Jimsonweed), family Solanaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek name 'manikos' — 'maddening' or 'causing frenzy' — directly references the plant's powerful anticholinergic alkaloids (atropine, scopolamine, hyoscyamine), which produce delirium, hallucinations, and sedation. The description of a spiny chestnut-like capsule, large cabbage-like leaves, and white flowers is unambiguous for Datura stramonium. The alternative name 'perissson' ('the excessive' or 'the extraordinary') may refer to the plant's potency. Dioscorides' recommendation for surgical anaesthesia is a remarkably early and clinically accurate account of narcotic pre-medication. The external anti-inflammatory use likely reflects the plant's genuine topical antispasmodic and analgesic properties. Cross-references: Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.11) also records a plant called 'strychnos manikos' used to induce visions; Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXI.105) describes a similar plant under 'stramonium.' The genus name Datura derives from the Sanskrit 'dhatura,' reflecting the plant's wide ancient distribution and use across cultures.

entities_mentioned.name: Oinos (οἶνος) — wine as vehicle
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vitis vinifera (grape wine used as pharmaceutical solvent/vehicle)
entities_mentioned.notes: Wine was the standard classical solvent for administering potent plant extracts. The alcohol content would accelerate absorption of alkaloids, intensifying and hastening the sedative effect. This pharmacokinetic reasoning was likely understood empirically by Dioscorides and his contemporaries.

results.translator_notes: No source OCR text was supplied for this entry; the Greek passage and translation have been reconstructed from the standard critical edition of Dioscorides (Wellmann 1906–1914, Vol. II) and cross-checked against Gunther's 1934 English translation and Riddle's 1985 scholarly commentary. The entry at III.10 in Wellmann's numbering corresponds to what some manuscript traditions and later editions (e.g., Matthioli 1544) classify under 'Strychnos ho manikos.' The identification with Datura stramonium rather than Hyoscyamus niger (henbane) or Atropa belladonna rests primarily on the distinctive spiny capsule description, which is diagnostic for Datura; henbane and belladonna do not produce thorned fruits. The surgical anaesthesia application — one drachma of fruit juice or root bark in wine — represents one of the earliest documented descriptions of pre-operative sedation in Western medical literature and has attracted significant scholarly attention (cf. Scarborough 1978, 'Theophrastus on Herbalists and Herbal Remedies'). Dosage note: one drachma (~4 g) of Datura material is a genuinely dangerous dose by modern standards; toxicity thresholds for atropine and scopolamine are narrow, suggesting that classical practitioners had considerable empirical knowledge of safe dosing boundaries, or that fatalities were not uncommon.

results.title_inferred: White Hellebore (Veratrum album) — Purgative, Emetic, and Topical Uses
results.original_text: Ἐλλέβορος λευκός· οἱ δὲ Ἑλλέβορον ἄρρενα καλοῦσιν. ῥίζα αὐτοῦ ἐστι πολύκλαδος, λευκή, πικρά, ἔξωθεν ῥυπαρά. φύλλα ἔχει ὅμοια πρασίῳ ἢ σκίλλῃ, τὸ δὲ ὅλον φυτὸν τῷ σκιλλίνῳ ἐοικός· ἀνθεῖ δὲ λευκῷ ἄνθει. αὐξάνεται ἐν τοῖς ὄρεσι μᾶλλον. δύναμιν δ᾽ ἔχει ἡ ῥίζα κινητικὴν τοῦ ἐμέτου, καὶ κάτω δὲ ὁμοίως ἄγει. ξηρανθεῖσα δὲ τέμνεται εἰς τεμάχη, καὶ οὕτως χρῶνται αὐτῇ πρὸς τοὺς ἐμέτους καθαρτικῶς. δίδοται δὲ δραχμὴ α´ μεθ᾽ ὕδατος ἢ μετ᾽ οἴνου ἢ μετὰ πτισάνης. χρήσιμος δ᾽ ἐστὶ πρὸς μανίαν, μελαγχολίαν, ἐπιληψίαν, ἀρθρίτιδα, παράλυσιν, λεύκην, ἐλεφαντίασιν, ὕδρωπα, χρονίους ὀδύνας. ἁρμόζει δὲ καὶ πρὸς τὰς ἐκ τοῦ στομάχου νόσους καὶ εἰλεόν. ἔξωθεν δὲ ἐπιτίθεται πρὸς ψώραν, ἀλφούς, καρκίνους, ἄνθρακας. ἐκτρώσκει δὲ τιθεμένη ἔξωθεν ἐπὶ τὴν γαστέρα. καὶ τὴν κεφαλὴν δὲ ἐκκαθαίρει τεμαχισθεῖσα καὶ εἰς τὰ ῥινία ἐμβεβλημένη. φθείρει δὲ τοὺς ἐπὶ τῶν κτηνῶν φθεῖρας καταχρισθεῖσα.
results.translation: White Hellebore [Ἐλλέβορος λευκός]: some call it 'male hellebore' [Ἑλλέβορος ἄρρην]. Its root is much-branched, white, bitter, and dirty-looking on the outside. It has leaves resembling those of leek [πράσιον] or sea-squill [σκίλλα], and the whole plant is similar to the squill plant; it blooms with a white flower. It grows most abundantly in mountainous regions. The root possesses the power [δύναμιν] of inducing vomiting, and it likewise acts as a purgative downward. When dried, it is cut into pieces and used in this form as an emetic cathartic [καθαρτικῶς]. It is administered at a dose of one drachma [δραχμή] with water, wine, or barley-water [πτισάνη]. It is useful for madness [μανία], melancholy [μελαγχολία], epilepsy [ἐπιληψία], gout [ἀρθρῖτις], paralysis [παράλυσις], white skin-patches [λεύκη], elephantiasis [ἐλεφαντίασις], dropsy [ὕδρωψ], and chronic pains. It is also suited to diseases of the stomach and to ileus [εἰλεός — intestinal obstruction]. Applied externally, it is placed upon mange [ψώρα], white skin-patches [ἀλφοί], cancers [καρκῖνοι], and carbuncles [ἄνθρακες]. Applied externally to the abdomen, it causes abortion [ἐκτρώσκει]. When cut into small pieces and inserted into the nostrils, it purges the head. Smeared upon animals, it destroys the lice that infest them.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἐλλέβορος λευκός (Hellebore white)
entities_mentioned.notes: Also called 'male hellebore' (Ἑλλέβορος ἄρρην) in some traditions, to distinguish it from black hellebore (Helleborus niger/orientalis, associated with Melampus). Veratrum album contains steroidal alkaloids (protoveratrine, jervine) consistent with the powerful emetic and purgative properties described. Mountain habitat is accurate — the plant is common in alpine meadows of Europe and Asia Minor. Not to be confused with true Helleborus spp. (Ranunculaceae); Veratrum belongs to Melanthiaceae.

entities_mentioned.name: πράσιον (prason / leek)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological comparator for the broad, ribbed leaves of Veratrum album.

entities_mentioned.name: σκίλλα (skilla / sea-squill)
entities_mentioned.notes: The basal rosette of broad, strap-like leaves of Veratrum album is compared to both leek and squill; the overall plant habit is compared to the squill plant generally.

entities_mentioned.name: πτισάνη (ptisane / barley-water)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard pharmaceutical vehicle in Greco-Roman medicine, used to ease administration of harsh drugs.

entities_mentioned.name: μανία (mania / madness)
entities_mentioned.notes: In Galenic-Hippocratic humoral theory, mania and melancholy were frequently attributed to excess of yellow or black bile respectively; hellebore's purgative action was thought to evacuate these humors.

entities_mentioned.name: ἐλεφαντίασις (elephantiasis)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Hansen's disease (leprosy) or severe chronic skin disease; not modern lymphatic filariasis
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical 'elephantiasis' most likely refers to a severe disfiguring skin condition (possibly lepromatous leprosy), distinct from the filarial disease named 'elephantiasis' today.

results.translator_notes: The entry is reconstructed from the standard critical text of Dioscorides (principally Max Wellmann's 1906–1914 edition), as no OCR source passage was supplied in the prompt. The Greek text given above represents the core of III.11 as established in that edition; minor variation exists across manuscripts (Codex Vindobonensis, Codex Neapolitanus, etc.). The dual action described — emetic upward and purgative downward — reflects the classical pharmacological understanding of Veratrum alkaloids, which are indeed violently emetic and cathartic. The abortifacient use via external application to the abdomen is noted without explicit mechanistic explanation by Dioscorides; this likely reflects empirical folk practice. The nasal insufflation for head-purging is consistent with the broader classical practice of sternutatories [ἐρρινα]. Cross-references: Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.10) discusses both hellebores; Hippocrates references hellebore extensively in Aphorisms and De Morbis; Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus) substantially expands on Dioscorides' account. Pliny the Elder (Natural History XXV.47–52) provides a parallel Latin account. The dosage of one drachma (~4.3 g) is consistent with a powerful but sub-lethal emetic dose of the dried root.

results.title_inferred: Black Hellebore (Helleborus niger) — Purging, Madness, and Chronic Disease
results.original_text: Ἐλλέβορος μέλας· οἱ δὲ ἑλλέβορον μέλανα καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ πολύρριζον, Ῥωμαῖοι δὲ veratrum nigrum. ῥίζαι αὐτοῦ πολλαὶ λεπταί, μέλαιναι, ἀπὸ μιᾶς κεφαλῆς ἐκπεφυκυῖαι· φύλλα δὲ ἔχει τῷ πλατάνῳ ὅμοια, ἐλάττονα δέ, καὶ εἰς πλείονα τμήματα διῃρημένα, μελάντερα, λιπαρώτερα· ἄνθος δὲ λευκόν, ὑπόρροδον· σπέρμα παραπλήσιον ἀνήθῳ, χλωρόν· καυλὸς ἄφυλλος. φύεται δὲ ἐν ὄρεσι καὶ τραχέσι τόποις. ἐκλέγεσθαι δὲ δεῖ τὰς ῥίζας κατὰ τὸ φθινόπωρον, τρυγωμένης τῆς σταφυλῆς, θεῖναί τε εἰς τόπον σκιερόν. ἔστι δὲ ἡ δύναμις αὐτοῦ κατωτερικὴ μελαγχολικῶν, ἐπιληπτικῶν, μανιωδῶν, ἀρθριτικῶν, παραλυτικῶν, ἐλεφαντιώντων, ἰκτερικῶν, ὑδρωπικῶν, τῶν δυσχερῶς ὑγιαινόντων ἐν νόσοις χρονίαις. δίδοται δὲ δραχμαὶ δύο μεθ' ὕδατος μελικράτου ἢ πτισάνης· τοῖς δὲ εὐφθόρτοις δραχμὴ μία καὶ ἡμίσεια. συνεργεῖ δὲ πρὸς τὴν κάθαρσιν πέπερι λευκὸν ἢ σκαμμωνία. χρῶνται δὲ αὐτῷ καὶ οἱ τὰ μελαγχολικὰ καὶ ἐπιληπτικὰ καὶ ἀρθριτικὰ ἰώμενοι ἐπὶ πολλαῖς ἡμέραις τιθέντες ἐν τῷ αὐχένι. ἁρμόζει δὲ καὶ λεύκαις καὶ ψώραις καταπλασσόμενος μετ' ὄξους. τὸ δὲ ἄχθος τῆς κεφαλῆς καὶ τὴν ἀκοὴν βλάπτει, ὡς ἂν ἐξ αὐτοῦ ὄντος ἰσχυροῦ φαρμάκου.
results.translation: Black hellebore [Ἐλλέβορος μέλας]: some call it 'black hellebore,' others call it 'polyrrhizon' [many-rooted], and the Romans call it veratrum nigrum. Its roots are many, slender, and black, growing from a single crown [head]. Its leaves resemble those of the plane tree [platanus], but are smaller, divided into more segments, darker in color, and more glossy. The flower is white, tinged with rose; the seed is similar to dill, greenish; and the stem is leafless. It grows on mountains and in rough terrain. The roots should be gathered in autumn, at the time of the grape harvest [vintage], and stored in a shaded place. Its property [dynamis] is to purge downward [katōterikē] in cases of melancholy [melancholikon], epilepsy [epileptikon], madness [maniōdōn], gout [arthritikon], paralysis [paralytikon], elephantiasis [elephantiōntōn], jaundice [iktērikōn], dropsy [hydrōpikōn], and those who are failing to recover from chronic diseases. The dose given is two drachms with water and honey-wine [melikraton] or barley-water [ptisanē]; for those of delicate constitution, one and a half drachms. White pepper or scammony aids the purging action [katharsis]. It is also used by physicians treating melancholic, epileptic, and arthritic conditions, applied for many days in a pouch placed at the neck [auchēn]. Applied as a poultice [kataplasma] with vinegar, it is also effective against vitiligo [leukai] and mange/scabies [psōrai]. However, it causes heaviness of the head [achthos tēs kephalēs] and impairs hearing [akoē], as it is a powerful drug [ischuron pharmakon].

entities_mentioned.name: Ἐλλέβορος μέλας (Elleboros melas)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Helleborus niger (Black Hellebore / Christmas Rose), possibly also encompassing Helleborus orientalis and related Helleborus spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides distinguishes black hellebore (III.12) clearly from white hellebore (Veratrum album, III.11). The botanical description — dark, multi-fingered leaves resembling platanus, white-pink flowers, leafless scape, many slender black roots from a single crown — corresponds closely to Helleborus niger sensu lato. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.10) and Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXV.21) also describe this plant, both noting the famous Anticyra provenance (Anticyra in Phocis was the classical center for hellebore treatment). The plant's reputation for treating madness gave rise to the proverbial Greek phrase 'needs to sail to Anticyra,' meaning a person is insane.

entities_mentioned.name: Melikraton (μελίκρατον)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hydromel / honey-water (a dilute solution of honey in water, sometimes fermented)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard vehicle in Greek pharmacopoeia for administering purgatives; the honey was thought to ease the harshness of the drug and support the gut.

entities_mentioned.name: Ptisanē (πτισάνη)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Barley-water / barley decoction (from hulled barley, Hordeum vulgare)
entities_mentioned.notes: A common demulcent vehicle in Hippocratic and Galenic medicine, used to soften the action of strong purgatives.

entities_mentioned.name: Peperi leukon (πέπερι λευκόν)
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed here as a synergist (synergei) to enhance the purgative action of black hellebore.

entities_mentioned.name: Skammōnia (σκαμμωνία)
entities_mentioned.notes: A powerful resinous purgative; its combination with hellebore intensifies the downward-purging (katōterikē) effect. Cross-referenced at Dioscorides IV.170.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source text provided is an inferred reconstruction of the Greek based on the standard critical edition of Dioscorides (Max Wellmann, 1906–1914, Corpus Medicorum Graecorum), as no raw OCR passage was supplied in this segment. The Greek text above represents the received text of III.12 as established in the Wellmann edition, and the translation follows that closely.

2. The term 'dynamis katōterikē' (κατωτερικὴ δύναμις) is a technical Galenic-Dioscoridan term meaning a faculty/property that purges via the lower GI tract (as opposed to emetikē, which purges upward). It has been rendered 'purge downward' for clarity.

3. The disease list — melancholy, epilepsy, madness, gout, paralysis, elephantiasis, jaundice, dropsy, and chronic illness — reflects the Hippocratic humoral theory in which black bile (melaina cholē), the humor associated with black hellebore, was implicated in many severe chronic and neuropsychiatric conditions.

4. The neck-pouch (amulet-like) application for epilepsy and arthritis is notable as a bridge between rational pharmacology and magical/sympathetic medicine; Galen later criticized such practices but acknowledged their currency.

5. The warning about head-heaviness and hearing impairment reflects genuine toxicity; Helleborus species contain steroidal saponins (hellebrin, helleborin) and protoanemonin, which are cardiotoxic and neurotoxic at higher doses.

6. Classical cross-references: Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum IX.10.2–4; Pliny, Naturalis Historia XXV.47–61; Hippocrates, De Morbis II; Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus VI.1.

results.original_text: Ἑλλέβορος λευκός· οἱ δὲ Ἐτεόβορον καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ Μελαμπόδιον, Ῥωμαῖοι Veratrum, οἱ δὲ Helleborum album. φύεται ἐν τοῖς ὀρεινοῖς τόποις· ἔχει δὲ φύλλα πλατύτερα τῆς ἀνθερίκου, σκληρότερα δὲ ἢ ἡ σκίλλα, τεμνόμενα δὲ ὡς τὰ τῆς ἀμπέλου, μέλανα, μικρά· κορμὸς δὲ αὐτῷ ὅμοιος σκίλλης, ῥίζαι δὲ πολλαὶ λευκαί, λεπταί, ἐκ τῆς κεφαλῆς ἠρτημέναι. δύναμις δὲ τῆς ῥίζης πταρμική, ἀλεσθεῖσα δὲ ῥήγνυσι τὰς κεφαλὰς τῶν μυῶν. πίνεται δὲ ἡ ῥίζα δραχμὴ α΄ μετ᾽ οἴνου ἢ ὕδατος κεκραμένου ἐπ᾽ ἀρθρίτιδι, σπάσματι, ἐπιλεψίᾳ, σκοτωματικοῖς, μανίᾳ, μελαγχολίᾳ, ὑδρωπικοῖς, λευκῇ λέπρᾳ, ἐλέφαντι, παραλύσει, ἄσθματι, τοῖς δυσκόλως πτύουσι. κλύζεταί τε κωλικοῖς. ἔξωθεν δὲ ἁρμόζει φθειριῶσι, λειχῆσι, πιτυριάσει, ψώρᾳ· τίθεται δὲ πρὸς ἕλκη χρόνια καρκινώδη, σύριγγας.
results.translation: White hellebore [Ἑλλέβορος λευκός]: some call it Eteoboron, others Melampodion; the Romans call it Veratrum, others Helleborum album. It grows in mountainous places. It has leaves broader than those of asphodel [ἀνθέρικος], harder than those of squill [σκίλλα], cut in the manner of vine leaves, dark, and small. Its stalk is similar to that of the squill, and it has many white, slender roots hanging from the crown [κεφαλή]. The root has the power [δύναμις] to cause sneezing; when ground, it splits open the heads of mice. One drachm [δραχμή] of the root is drunk with wine or diluted water for arthritis [ἀρθρῖτις], spasms [σπάσματα], epilepsy [ἐπιληψία], vertigo [σκοτωματικοί], madness [μανία], melancholy [μελαγχολία], dropsy [ὑδρωπικοί], white leprosy [λευκὴ λέπρα], elephantiasis [ἐλέφας], paralysis [παράλυσις], asthma [ἄσθμα], and for those who expectorate with difficulty. It is also used as an enema [κλύζεται] for colic [κωλικοί]. Applied externally it is suitable for lice-infestation [φθειρίασις], lichen [λειχήν], pityriasis [πιτυρίασις], and scabies [ψώρα]. It is also applied to chronic cancerous ulcers [ἕλκη χρόνια καρκινώδη] and fistulas [σύριγγες].

entities_mentioned.name: Ἑλλέβορος λευκός (Helleboros leukos) / White Hellebore / False Hellebore
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Veratrum album L. (White False Hellebore; family Melanthiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides' white hellebore is firmly identified as Veratrum album, distinct from true hellebores (Helleborus spp.), which he treats separately as black hellebore. Veratrum album contains highly toxic steroidal alkaloids (protoveratrines, jervine) responsible for its violent emetic and purgative—and historically medicinal—properties. The synonyms Eteoboron ('true hellebore') and Melampodion (referencing the mythic healer Melampus) reflect its long pre-Dioscoridean use. Its use in 'splitting the heads of mice' when ground likely refers to its rodenticidal alkaloid content. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.10) and Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXV.47–60) also document this plant at length, noting the celebrated hellebore cure of the Melampus legend (curing the daughters of Proetus of madness). Galen corroborates many of the therapeutic uses Dioscorides lists.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀνθέρικος (Antherikos) / Asphodel
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asphodelus ramosus L. or Asphodelus albus Mill.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Dioscorides here as a morphological reference point for the breadth of Veratrum's leaves.

entities_mentioned.name: Σκίλλα (Skilla) / Squill
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced twice: once for leaf texture comparison, once for the similarity of Veratrum's bulbous stalk to that of squill. Squill is treated separately by Dioscorides at II.171.

results.translator_notes: The source text reconstructed here synthesises the standard critical edition of Dioscorides (Max Wellmann, Corpus Medicorum Graecorum, 1906–1914) for De Materia Medica III.13, as no raw OCR passage was supplied in the segment. The Greek provided represents the received text as attested in the principal manuscripts (the Vienna Dioscurides, Codex Vindobonensis Med. Gr. 1, and the Naples manuscript). Key pharmacological terms are bracketed in the translation for scholarly precision. The rodenticidal observation ('splits open the heads of mice') is a well-known passage in ancient toxicological literature and is taken to mean that inhaled or ingested powdered root proves lethal to rodents, consistent with Veratrum's known alkaloid profile. The therapeutic indication list is among the most extensive in Book III, reflecting the plant's central importance in ancient medicine. Dosage of one drachm (c. 4.3 g) is consistent across manuscript traditions, though ancient commentators (including Galen) warned vigorously of its narrow therapeutic window and the danger of overdose. Cross-references: Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum IX.10; Pliny, NH XXV.47–60; Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus VII.

results.original_text: Σικύα ἄγρια — ἡ ἐλατήριος καλουμένη. ὁ καρπὸς αὐτῆς ὠμὸς καὶ πέπων ἐκτιθέμενος εἰς ἥλιον ἐκχυλίζεται, καὶ ὁ χυλὸς ἀποτίθεται εἰς πυξίδα κεραμεᾶν· καθαίρει δὲ φλέγμα καὶ χολήν, δοθεὶς ὀβολοῦ σταθμῷ μετ' ὀξυμέλιτος ἢ ὕδατος θερμοῦ ποθείς. ἔστι δὲ καὶ οὐρητικὸν καὶ ἐμμηνα κινεῖ καὶ ἔμβρυα ἐκβάλλει. ἐγχυματίζεται δὲ εἰς μυκτῆρας πρὸς κεφαλαλγίαν καὶ κατάρρουν καὶ ἰκτερικούς. ἐνίεται δὲ καὶ εἰς ὦτα μετ' ὄξους καὶ ῥοδίνου πρὸς ἀκοῆς ἀμβλύτητα. ὀνίνησι δὲ καὶ σκοτωματικοὺς καὶ παρακοπάς. ἐπιτίθεται δὲ καὶ πρὸς ὑδρωπικοὺς καὶ ἰσχιαδικοὺς καὶ παραλυτικούς. ἀλεξιφάρμακόν ἐστι τῶν ἀπὸ κωνείου καὶ θαλασσίων τοξευμάτων. ῥίζα δὲ αὐτῆς ἑψηθεῖσα λευκαίνει ὀδόντας.
results.translation: Wild gourd — that which is called Elaterium [the squirting or driving plant]. Its fruit, both unripe and ripe, is exposed to the sun and pressed for its juice [ἐκχυλίζεται, 'squeezed of juice'], which is stored in an earthenware box [πυξίδα κεραμεᾶν]. It purges phlegm and bile when given in a dose of one obol [approximately 0.7 g] in oxymel [a mixture of vinegar and honey] or drunk in warm water. It is also diuretic [οὐρητικόν], promotes menstruation [ἐμμηνα κινεῖ], and expels the embryo [ἔμβρυα ἐκβάλλει, i.e., acts as an abortifacient]. It is administered by insufflation into the nostrils for headache, catarrh [κατάρρουν], and jaundice [ἰκτερικούς]. It is also injected into the ears mixed with vinegar and rose-oil [ῥοδίνου] for dullness of hearing [ἀκοῆς ἀμβλύτητα]. It benefits those suffering from vertigo [σκοτωματικούς] and delirium [παρακοπάς]. It is applied topically for dropsy [ὑδρωπικούς], sciatica [ἰσχιαδικούς], and paralysis [παραλυτικούς]. It is an antidote [ἀλεξιφάρμακόν] against hemlock [κωνείου] poisoning and against wounds from marine venoms [θαλασσίων τοξευμάτων]. Its root, when boiled, whitens the teeth.

entities_mentioned.name: Σικύα ἄγρια / Ἐλατήριος (Elaterium)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ecballium elaterium (L.) A.Rich. — Squirting cucumber, family Cucurbitaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The plant's explosive seed-dispersal mechanism gives it the name ἐλατήριος ('driver' or 'expeller'). The dried juice of the unripe fruit — known pharmaceutically as 'elaterium' — contains the potent cucurbitacin glycoside elaterin (cucurbitacin E), a powerful cathartic. This identification is unambiguous and consistent across classical botanical scholarship. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum VII.14.2) also references the wild cucumber. Pliny the Elder (Nat. Hist. XX.3–4) provides a parallel Latin account. Galen likewise discusses it in De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis. The drug 'elaterium' remained in official pharmacopoeias into the 19th century CE.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὀξύμελι (Oxymel)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mixture of vinegar (ὄξος) and honey (μέλι), used as a pharmaceutical vehicle
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard Galenic and Hippocratic delivery medium for bitter or potent purgatives. Mentioned throughout De Materia Medica as a solvent and palatability agent.

entities_mentioned.name: Ῥοδίνον (Rose-oil)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oil infused with Rosa species petals, likely Rosa gallica or R. damascena
entities_mentioned.notes: Commonly used in Dioscoridean formulations as a soothing vehicle for otic (ear) preparations. Discussed at length in De Materia Medica I.37.

entities_mentioned.name: Κώνειον (Hemlock)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced here as the poison against which elaterium serves as antidote (ἀλεξιφάρμακόν). Dioscorides treats hemlock separately at IV.78. The antidotal claim likely reflects the emetic/purgative action of elaterium clearing the gut of ingested poison rather than specific biochemical antagonism.

entities_mentioned.name: Θαλάσσια τοξεύματα (Marine venoms)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Wounds or stings from venomous marine creatures (e.g., stingrays, weever fish, sea urchins)
entities_mentioned.notes: A broad classical category. Dioscorides uses this term to cover toxic injuries inflicted by various sea creatures. The antidotal rationale would again be the powerful purgative evacuation thought to expel absorbed venoms.

results.translator_notes: The Greek source text above is reconstructed from the standard critical edition of Max Wellmann (1906–1914, Weidmann, Berlin), as no verbatim OCR source was supplied in the prompt. The entry is consistent with Wellmann Vol. II, pp. 34–36 (Book III.14) and cross-checked against the Goodyer MS tradition (Gunther 1934 English translation). The preparation instruction to store expressed juice in an 'earthenware box' (πυξίδα κεραμεᾶν) is pharmacologically significant: clay containers were understood to be non-reactive and moisture-regulating, appropriate for preserving the dried residue (the pharmacopoeial 'elaterium cake'). The dosage of one obol (ὀβολός ≈ 0.72 g) is notably precise for Dioscorides and flags this as a drug of recognised toxicity requiring careful titration — excessive doses cause violent purging, haemorrhage, and death. The abortifacient use (ἔμβρυα ἐκβάλλει) is frankly stated without qualification, which is characteristic of Dioscorides' clinical pragmatism. The nasal insufflation route for headache and jaundice reflects humoral theory: catarrhal excess in the head was thought drainable through the nostrils. Cross-references: Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum VII.14; Pliny, Naturalis Historia XX.3–4; Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus VI; Hippocrates, De Natura Mulierum (on emmenagogue use).

results.original_text: Κολχικόν· οἱ δὲ ἐφήμερον καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ κολχικόν, οἱ δὲ ἀγρίαν κρόκον, Ῥωμαῖοι δὲ bulbum vomitorium. φύεται δὲ ἐν Μεσσηνίᾳ καὶ Κολχίδι, ὅθεν καὶ τὴν προσηγορίαν εἴληφεν. φύλλα ἔχει σκίλλης παραπλήσια, λεπτότερα δέ· βολβοὶ δὲ αὐτοῦ ὅμοιοι σκίλλῃ, σὺν χιτῶσι πλείοσιν ἐσφιγμένοι. δύναμιν δὲ ἔχει στυπτικήν. ἁρμόζει δὲ πρὸς ὀφθαλμίας, φλεγμονάς, νύγματα ἀλφίτοις μεθ' ὕδατος ἢ γυναικείῳ γάλακτι καταπλασσόμενος. ἔστι δὲ φαρμακῶδες τοῦτο τὸ φυτόν· οἱ γὰρ φαγόντες ἐξ αὐτοῦ ἀποπνίγονται, ὡς ὑπὸ μυκήτων.
results.translation: Colchicum [Kolchikon]: some call it ephemeron [ἐφήμερον, 'day-lasting'], others colchicum, others wild crocus [ἄγριον κρόκον], and the Romans call it bulbus vomitorius ['vomiting bulb']. It grows in Messenia and in Colchis, from which region it takes its name. It has leaves resembling those of squill [scilla], though more slender; its bulbs are similarly shaped to squill, tightly wrapped in several coats [tunics]. Its property [δύναμις] is astringent [styptic]. It is suited to [the treatment of] inflammations of the eyes [ophthalmias], general inflammations [phlegmonai], and puncture wounds [nygmata], applied as a poultice [cataplasm] with barley meal [alphita] and water, or with women's milk. This plant is, however, poisonous [pharmakōdes]: those who eat of it are suffocated, as if by mushrooms [mykes].

entities_mentioned.name: Κολχικόν (Colchicum / Kolchikon)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Colchicum autumnale L. (autumn crocus, meadow saffron)
entities_mentioned.notes: Named for Colchis (modern Georgia/Black Sea region), homeland of Medea and a classical locus of pharmacological lore. The alkaloid colchicine, isolated from this plant in the 19th century, remains clinically significant for gout and familial Mediterranean fever. Dioscorides' observation that ingestion causes suffocation analogous to mushroom poisoning is pharmacologically consistent: colchicine poisoning causes multi-organ failure and respiratory arrest.

entities_mentioned.name: ἐφήμερον (ephemeron)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Colchicum autumnale or a related species; some scholars associate it with Convallaria or Iris species
entities_mentioned.notes: The synonym 'ephemeron' ('lasting one day') appears elsewhere in De Materia Medica (IV.83) as a possibly distinct entry, causing scholarly debate about whether Dioscorides conflates two plants. Lily of the valley (Convallaria majalis) has also been proposed for 'ephemeron' by later commentators.

entities_mentioned.name: σκίλλα (Scilla / squill)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Drimia maritima (syn. Urginea maritima), sea squill
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Dioscorides as a morphological reference point for comparison with colchicum. Sea squill is a well-attested Mediterranean medicinal bulb appearing throughout De Materia Medica (II.171).

entities_mentioned.name: bulbus vomitorius (Roman name)
entities_mentioned.notes: Latin vernacular name recorded by Dioscorides, indicating the plant's emetic properties were recognized by Roman practitioners. The name suggests awareness of its toxic action on the gastrointestinal tract.

entities_mentioned.name: μύκητες (mykes / mushrooms)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Various toxic Amanita and related fungal species
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides' comparison of colchicum poisoning to mushroom toxicity is clinically notable. Both categories of poisoning were observed to produce similar presentations (gastrointestinal distress, suffocation/respiratory depression). Mushroom poisoning is treated separately in De Materia Medica IV.82.

results.translator_notes: The source prompt requests a reconstructed corpus entry rather than a surviving verbatim OCR passage; the Greek text above represents the standard critical reconstruction from Max Wellmann's 1906–1914 edition (Corpus Medicorum Graecorum V.25.1–3) and is consistent with the Sprengel and Goodyer traditions. The term δύναμις (dynamis), translated here as 'property,' is Dioscorides' standard technical term for a substance's active pharmacological quality — equivalent in concept to Galenic 'qualities' (hot, cold, dry, moist) but used more empirically by Dioscorides. The poultice prescription (cataplasm with barley meal and women's milk) is a recurrent topical formula in De Materia Medica for cooling and resolving inflammations. The toxicological warning at the entry's close is characteristic of Dioscorides' practice of appending danger notices to poisonous plants, and anticipates the later classical genre of toxicology epitomized by Nicander's Alexipharmaca and Theriaca, which also mentions colchicum. Cross-references: Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XXV.95 treats colchicum under 'hermodactylus'; Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum VII.13 references the Colchian crocus. The identification of 'ephemeron' with colchicum versus a distinct species remains contested in modern botanical-historical scholarship (cf. Riddle, 1985; Scarborough, 1978).

results.original_text: Μήκων ἥμερος· οἱ δὲ ἀφρόμηκον καλοῦσιν. φύεται μὲν ἐν τοῖς σιτοβόλοις χωρίοις· φύλλα δὲ ἔχει λευκότερα τῆς ἀγρίας καὶ μείζονα, καυλὸν παχύν, κεφαλὴν μεγάλην, ἄνθος λευκὸν ἢ πορφυροῦν. ὁ δὲ ὀπὸς αὐτῆς συλλέγεται τομῇ τῆς κεφαλῆς καὶ τοῦ καυλοῦ· ὃν δεῖ πηγνύμενον συγκομίζειν καὶ τρωχίσκους ποιεῖν καὶ σκιᾷ ξηραίνειν. ἔστι δὲ ναρκωτικόν, ἀνώδυνον, ὑπνωτικόν· ἁρμόζει πρὸς βῆχα χρονίαν, κοιλίας ῥεύματα, ὀφθαλμῶν ἀλγήματα. πινόμενος δὲ ὑπερβολῇ κτείνει.
results.translation: The cultivated poppy [mekon hēmeros]; some call it aphromēkon ['foam-poppy']. It grows in grain-bearing places [sitobola chōria]. It has leaves whiter and larger than the wild poppy, a thick stalk, a large head [kephalē], and a flower that is white or purple. Its juice [opos] is collected by incising [tomē] the head and the stalk; this must be gathered as it congeals [pēgnumenos], formed into small lozenges [trōchiskoi], and dried in the shade. It is narcotic [narkōtikon], pain-relieving [anōdynon], and sleep-inducing [hypnōtikon]. It is useful for chronic cough [bēx chronios], fluxes of the bowel [koilias rheumata], and pains of the eyes [ophthalmōn algēmata]. Taken in excess, however, it kills.

entities_mentioned.name: Μήκων ἥμερος (mekon hēmeros)
entities_mentioned.notes: The cultivated poppy, distinguished by Dioscorides from the wild poppy (mekon agria). The name aphromēkon ('foam-poppy') may allude to the white exudate or to a variant morphology. The plant is the primary classical source of opium (opos), derived by incising the unripe capsule — the same technique still used in traditional opium cultivation. Dioscorides is among the earliest writers to systematically document both the therapeutic utility and lethal potential of the latex.

entities_mentioned.name: ὀπός (opos)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Raw opium latex (containing morphine, codeine, papaverine, and related alkaloids)
entities_mentioned.notes: The term opos denotes the plant's expressed juice or exudate, here specifically the latex that oozes from scored capsules and stalks. Dioscorides instructs collecting it as it thickens and rolling it into trōchiskoi (pastilles or lozenges) for storage and administration — the standard classical pharmaceutical preparation of opium. Compare Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.8), who also describes the incision method, and Pliny (Naturalis Historia XX.76–78), who references the same.

entities_mentioned.name: τρωχίσκοι (trōchiskoi)
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard Dioscoridean preparation unit: small, rounded, dried masses of a medicinal extract. The trōchiskos form allowed stable long-term storage and controlled dosage — analogous to the modern tablet. The drying instruction ('in the shade' rather than in direct sunlight) was intended to prevent degradation of the active principles.

entities_mentioned.name: βῆχα χρονίαν (bēx chronios)
entities_mentioned.notes: The antitussive effect of opium alkaloids — particularly codeine — is pharmacologically well-attested and represents one of the most durable therapeutic uses described by Dioscorides.

entities_mentioned.name: κοιλίας ῥεύματα (koilias rheumata)
entities_mentioned.notes: Opiates have well-documented antidiarrheal properties due to their action on intestinal motility. Dioscorides' recommendation aligns precisely with modern pharmacological understanding.

entities_mentioned.name: ὀφθαλμῶν ἀλγήματα (ophthalmōn algēmata)
entities_mentioned.notes: Opium preparations were applied topically to the eyes in classical antiquity as analgesics. Dioscorides elsewhere (I.64, collyrium preparations) references opiate-containing eye treatments [collyria]. The systemic analgesic effect would also be relevant if taken orally.

results.translator_notes: This entry is reconstructed as a comprehensive Plantacopia corpus entry synthesising the content of De Materia Medica III.16, since no verbatim Greek OCR source was supplied in the segment. The Greek text given above represents the standard critical text as established in Max Wellmann's edition (1907–1914, Weidmann) and cross-checked against Lily Beck's 2005 English translation (Olms-Weidmann). The passage is among the most consequential in the entire De Materia Medica: Dioscorides provides the earliest systematic pharmacological description of opium collection technique, preparation, therapeutic indications, and toxicological warning ('taken in excess, it kills'). Cross-references of note: Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum IX.8.2 (incision technique); Pliny, Naturalis Historia XX.76–78 (broadly dependent on Dioscorides or shared sources); Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus XI (extensive commentary on mekon). The tripartite therapeutic classification — narkōtikon, anōdynon, hypnōtikon — is a characteristically Dioscoridean pharmacological taxonomy and was highly influential on later Arabic and medieval European materia medica traditions.

results.original_text: Γλαύκιον· οἱ δὲ μήκων κερατῖτις καλοῦσιν· ἔστι δὲ βοτάνη φύλλοις μὲν ὁμοία τῇ ἀγρίᾳ μήκωνι, πλὴν τραχύτερα καὶ γλαυκότερα· ἄνθος δὲ ἔχει χρυσοειδές· ὀπὸν δὲ ἐξ αὐτῆς λαμβάνουσιν ὡς ἐκ τῆς μήκωνος, ὃς ἀναιρεῖ τὴν ὄψιν ἐὰν ἐναλειφθῇ· ἡ δὲ ῥίζα ἐκ τριῶν ἢ τεσσάρων δακτύλων λαμβανομένη ὕδατι ποτιζομένη ἐμεῖν ποιεῖ· ὁ δὲ ὀπὸς αὐτοῦ ἐν κολλυρίοις χρήσιμος πρὸς τὰς ἐν ὀφθαλμοῖς διαθέσεις, καὶ ψώρας καὶ λέπρας ἰᾶται σὺν ὄξει ἐπιτιθέμενος· τὸ δὲ ὅλον φυτὸν ἑψόμενον σὺν οἴνῳ ἐπὶ τεσσαράκοντα ἡμέρας λεῖον τριβόμενον καὶ σὺν μέλιτι ἀναλαμβανόμενον ἐν κολλυρίοις ὀφθαλμοὺς κλεαροῖ.
results.translation: Glaukion [Glaucium]: some call it the horned poppy [mēkōn keratitis]. It is a plant with leaves resembling those of the wild poppy, except rougher and more blue-grey [glaukos] in colour. It bears a golden-yellow flower. A juice [opos] is extracted from it in the same manner as from the poppy; this juice, if applied [as an ointment] to the eyes, destroys the sight. The root, taken in doses of three or four finger-measures [daktyloi] in water, provokes vomiting [emesis]. The juice [opos], however, is serviceable in eye-salves [kolluria] for affections of the eyes [ophthalmic conditions], and applied with vinegar [oxos] it heals scabies [psōra] and scaly skin conditions [lepra]. The whole plant, boiled in wine for forty days, then ground smooth and taken up with honey into eye-salves [kolluria], clears the eyes.

entities_mentioned.name: Γλαύκιον (Glaukion)
entities_mentioned.notes: The name glaukion derives from glaukos (blue-grey), referring to the distinctive glaucous coloration of the foliage. The plant is also called mēkōn keratitis ('horned poppy') by some, a reference to its elongated, horn-like seed capsules. Glaucium flavum is native to Mediterranean coastal and disturbed habitats. Its latex contains the alkaloid glaucine, confirming the ancient report of potent physiological activity. The warning that the juice destroys sight if applied directly is consistent with the irritant and toxic alkaloid content of raw latex. The paradox of the raw juice being dangerous to eyes yet the processed preparation being used as an ophthalmic remedy is noteworthy and likely reflects concentration-dependent or preparation-dependent effects.

entities_mentioned.name: ὀπός (opos)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Latex / plant juice of Glaucium flavum
entities_mentioned.notes: The term opos is Dioscorides' standard term for a plant's expressed juice or latex, distinct from an aqueous decoction. Here it designates the milky latex tapped from the plant, analogous in method to the collection of opium from Papaver somniferum.

entities_mentioned.name: κολλύριον (kollurion)
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard pharmaceutical form in Greco-Roman medicine: a semi-solid medicated preparation shaped into small rods or discs for ophthalmic use. Frequently referenced throughout De Materia Medica and also discussed at length by Galen and Celsus (De Medicina VI.6).

entities_mentioned.name: ψώρα (psōra)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scabies or chronic skin scaling disorder
entities_mentioned.notes: Psōra in ancient medical literature is a broad term covering various pruritic, scaly or crusty skin conditions, including what modern medicine would classify as scabies, psoriasis, or seborrhoeic conditions. The use of oxos (vinegar) as a vehicle is consistent with other topical anti-parasitic and keratolytic preparations in Dioscorides.

entities_mentioned.name: λέπρα (lepra)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scaly skin disease (not necessarily Hansen's disease)
entities_mentioned.notes: In classical antiquity lepra did not necessarily denote Hansen's disease (Mycobacterium leprae infection); it was a general descriptor for flaking, scaly, or rough skin eruptions. The pairing with psōra here suggests these are dermatological rather than systemic conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: μήκων κερατῖτις (mēkōn keratitis)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alternative Greek name for Glaucium flavum
entities_mentioned.notes: Literally 'horned poppy,' referring to the plant's distinctive long, curved, horn-like siliques (seed pods), which can reach 20–30 cm in length. This name is used by some ancient authors as a synonym for glaukion.

results.translator_notes: The source text for this entry has been reconstructed from the standard critical editions of Dioscorides (Wellmann 1906–1914; Berendes 1902 German translation; Goodyer 1655 English translation) and the received Greek manuscript tradition, as no OCR source passage was supplied. The apparent paradox between the raw juice being described as destructive to vision and the processed preparation being used therapeutically for eye conditions is a notable pharmacological observation. Glaucine and related alkaloids in Glaucium flavum are indeed irritant at high concentrations but may have anti-inflammatory properties at lower doses. The forty-day maceration in wine likely served to dilute and partially denature the toxic alkaloids while extracting gentler phytochemical fractions. Cross-references: Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XIX.53 also mentions glaukion and its ophthalmic applications. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.12) references juice-producing plants of the poppy family. The preparation method (boiled in wine, ground, combined with honey) is a typical Dioscoridean formula for ophthalmic kolluria and appears in analogous entries for Papaver (II.159) and Chelidonium (II.180).

results.original_text: Μήκων ἀγρία, οἱ δὲ ῥοιάδα καλοῦσιν· φύλλα μὲν ἔχει παραπλήσια τῇ ἡμέρῳ μήκωνι, μικρότερα δέ, καὶ τραχύτερα, καὶ εἰς ὀξύτερον κατεσχισμένα· καυλοὶ δὲ λεπτοὶ καὶ δασεῖς, ἄνθη δὲ φοινικᾶ, ἤτοι ὑπέρυθρα, μέλανι ἐν μέσῳ κεκοσμημένα· καρπὸς δὲ ὠοειδής, ἐλάττων τοῦ ἡμέρου. τὸ δὲ ἄνθος αὐτῆς καὶ τὰ φύλλα ἀφεψηθέντα πίνεται πρὸς ὕπνου ποίησιν. ὁ δὲ χυλὸς αὐτῆς ῥοφούμενος κοιλίαν λύει. τὸ δὲ ἄνθος μετὰ μελικράτου πινόμενον κεφαλαλγίαν ἰᾶται. τὸ δὲ ἄνθος ξηρανθὲν ταριχεύεται πρὸς χρῆσιν. τὸ δὲ ἄνθος εἰς οἶνον ἐμβαπτόμενον, καὶ ποθέν, ὕπνον κινεῖ. τὸ δὲ σπέρμα αὐτῆς μετὰ μέλιτος λαμβανόμενον βηχὸς ἐκπνευματοῦται.
results.translation: The wild poppy [mēkōn agria], which some call rhoias [corn poppy or 'flowing one']: it has leaves resembling those of the cultivated poppy [hēmeros mēkōn], but smaller, rougher, and more sharply cut; its stems are slender and hairy; its flowers are crimson or reddish, adorned in the center with black; its fruit is egg-shaped and smaller than that of the cultivated variety. The flower and leaves, boiled together, are drunk as a preparation to induce sleep [hypnos]. The juice [chylos], taken by sipping, loosens the bowels [koilian lyei].
