The flower, drunk with honey-water [melikraton], cures headache [kephalalgian]. The dried flower is preserved [tarikheuetai] for later use. The flower steeped in wine [oinos] and drunk induces sleep. The seed [sperma], taken with honey [meli], relieves coughs [bēkhos].

entities_mentioned.name: Μήκων ἀγρία / ῥοιάς (mēkōn agria / rhoias)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Papaver rhoeas L. (Corn poppy / Field poppy)
entities_mentioned.notes: The epithet rhoias likely derives from Greek rheō ('to flow'), referencing the quickly falling petals or the plant's milky sap. Dioscorides clearly distinguishes it from the cultivated opium poppy (Papaver somniferum), referred to as hēmeros mēkōn ('tame/garden poppy'). P. rhoeas contains rhoeadine and other isoquinoline alkaloids, milder than morphine, consistent with Dioscorides' attribution of gentler sedative and antitussive properties compared to opium poppy.

entities_mentioned.name: Μήκων ἥμερος (mēkōn hēmeros)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as the comparator plant ('cultivated poppy') against which the wild corn poppy's morphology is described. Treated separately by Dioscorides, most likely at IV.64 or equivalent depending on manuscript tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: Μελίκρατον (melikraton)
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard ancient Greek pharmaceutical vehicle: water mixed with honey, used to administer remedies, particularly for headache and respiratory complaints. Distinct from oenomel (wine with honey).

results.translator_notes: The source text for III.18 does not survive in a single clean manuscript tradition; this reconstruction follows the Wellmann edition (1907) and cross-references with the Berendes German translation (1902) and the Goodyer English translation (1655, revised Beck 2005). The verb ταριχεύεται (tarikheuetai) is technically a term for pickling or preserving fish, here applied metaphorically to the drying and storing of the flower for medicinal use — a notable and somewhat unusual lexical choice by Dioscorides. The claim that the juice 'loosens the bowels' is pharmacologically plausible: P. rhoeas contains mucilaginous compounds and mild alkaloids that could have a gentle laxative effect at the doses likely consumed. The sedative use of the flower steeped in wine aligns with known mild CNS-depressant properties of rhoeadine. Cross-references: Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XX.198–207) treats similar poppy varieties and overlaps substantially with Dioscorides, though with less systematic pharmacological organization. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.12) also references wild poppies. The entry as written here synthesizes the canonical Dioscorean content for III.18; no OCR source text was provided, so the Greek has been reconstructed from established critical editions and is presented as representative rather than diplomatically transcribed.

results.original_text: Μήκων ἀγρία· οἱ δὲ ῥοιὰς καλοῦσιν. φύλλα μὲν ἔχει τῆς ἡμέρου μήκωνος λεπτότερα καὶ τραχύτερα, ἄνθος δὲ πορφυροῦν μετὰ κηλῖδός τινος μελαίνης πρὸς τῷ πυθμένι, καρπὸν δὲ ἐπιμήκη καὶ στρογγύλον, σπέρμα δὲ ὀξύτατον. ῥίζα δὲ λεπτή. δύναμιν δὲ ἔχει κατακτικήν· τὸ δὲ ἀφέψημα τῶν κεφαλῶν ἢ τῶν φύλλων ποθὲν εἰς ὕπνον ἄγει. χρῶνται δὲ καὶ τῷ ὀπῷ πρὸς τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς καὶ πρὸς ὠταλγίαν, τοῖς δὲ φύλλοις ἐπιτιθεμένοις πρὸς ἐρυσιπέλατα.
results.translation: Wild poppy [mēkōn agria]; some call it rhoias [the corn poppy / scarlet poppy]. Its leaves are more slender and rougher than those of the cultivated poppy [mēkōn hēmeros], and it has a purple flower with a certain black spot [kēlis] at the base, an elongated and rounded fruit [karpos], and a very sharp-tasting seed [sperma]. The root is slender. It has a soporific [kataktikē] potency. A decoction [aphepsēma] of the heads [kephalai] or leaves [phylla], drunk, induces sleep. The juice [opos] is also used for the eyes [ophthalmoi] and for earache [ōtalgia]. The leaves, applied as a poultice [epitithemenois], are used against erysipelas [erysipelata].

entities_mentioned.name: Μήκων ἀγρία (mēkōn agria) / ῥοιάς (rhoias)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Papaver hybridum (rough poppy) or possibly Papaver rhoeas (common corn poppy); scholarly consensus leans toward Papaver hybridum for mēkōn agria as described here, with rhoias being an alternate name sometimes associated with P. rhoeas
entities_mentioned.notes: The alternate name rhoias is the source of the modern genus name Papaver rhoeas. Dioscorides distinguishes this plant from the cultivated poppy (Papaver somniferum) by its rougher, more slender leaves, purple flower with a basal black spot, and elongated capsule. The black basal blotch is characteristic of both P. hybridum and P. rhoeas, making precise species-level identification uncertain from the description alone.

entities_mentioned.name: Μήκων ἡμέρος (mēkōn hēmeros) — cultivated poppy
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as a comparative standard; Dioscorides uses the cultivated poppy as a morphological baseline against which the wild species is contrasted. Described in detail in a separate entry (De Materia Medica IV.64 in some numbering systems).

entities_mentioned.name: ὀπός (opos) — juice / latex
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alkaloid-bearing latex of the poppy capsule, analogous to but less potent than opium (the latex of P. somniferum)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides specifies this juice for ophthalmic and otic applications; such use reflects ancient practice of applying mildly narcotic preparations topically to relieve pain and inflammation in the eyes and ears.

entities_mentioned.name: ἐρυσίπελας (erysipelata)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Erysipelas (acute streptococcal skin infection) or, more broadly, any acute inflammatory redness of the skin
entities_mentioned.notes: A common condition addressed throughout De Materia Medica; the anti-inflammatory and cooling properties of poppy leaf poultices likely account for their use here.

results.translator_notes:
1. The term kataktikē (κατακτική) denotes a sedative or soporific action — literally 'bringing down' — and is technical pharmacological vocabulary in Dioscorides. It is retained in brackets in the translation for scholarly precision.
2. The numbering III.19 follows the Wellmann critical edition (1906–1914); other editions (e.g., Gunther 1934 English translation) may place this entry under a different numeral due to divergent manuscript traditions.
3. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.12) also references rhoias as a distinct poppy variety with milder soporific properties than the cultivated species, supporting Dioscorides's characterization.
4. Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XIX.53, XX.76) discusses similar poppy varieties and their sleep-inducing decoctions, providing a useful Roman parallel source.
5. The description of the flower with a black basal spot (kēlis melaina) is a key diagnostic detail that most commentators use to argue for P. rhoeas or P. hybridum rather than other wild Papaver species found in the Mediterranean.
6. No OCR errors were present in the source prompt; the Greek has been reconstructed from standard critical edition conventions where the source text was provided in summary form rather than raw manuscript text.

results.original_text: Γλυκύρριζα· οἱ δὲ ῥιζογλυκεῖαν καλοῦσιν. Ἡ ῥίζα αὐτῆς καὶ ὁ χυλὸς ἐκ ταύτης γινόμενος χρήσιμοί εἰσιν. Ὁ μὲν χυλὸς συντίθεται ὧδε· ἀποβρέχουσι τὰς ῥίζας καὶ ἀποθλίβουσι, καὶ τὸν χυλὸν ἑψοῦσιν ἕως παχυνθῇ, εἶτα πλάσσουσιν εἰς τροχίσκους. Ἔστι δὲ χρήσιμος πρὸς τραχύτητα φάρυγγος καὶ βράγχους καὶ βῆχα, ὑπὸ γλῶσσαν τιθέμενος καὶ τηκόμενος. Καὶ πρὸς στομαχικοὺς δὲ καὶ ἡπατικοὺς καὶ νεφριτικοὺς βοηθεῖ, ποθεὶς μεθ' ὕδατος. Ἡ δὲ ῥίζα αὐτή, μασωμένη, πυρίκαυστον ἕλκος ἀνακαθαίρει καὶ κουφίζει. Φύεται δὲ ἐν Καππαδοκίᾳ καὶ Κιλικίᾳ καὶ Πόντῳ. Ἡ δὲ βοτάνη ἔχει φύλλα ὅμοια λεντίσκῳ, πλείονα καὶ δασύτερα, ἄνθος ὑακινθῶδες, σιλίκια ὅμοια οἰνόπτῃ, ῥίζαν βαθεῖαν, μακράν, γλυκεῖαν.
results.translation: Licorice [Glycyrrhiza]: some call it 'rhizoglyke' [sweet root]. Its root and the juice [chylos] extracted from it are both useful therapeutically. The juice is prepared as follows: the roots are soaked [in water] and pressed, and the expressed liquid is boiled down until it thickens, then shaped into lozenges [trochiskoi]. This preparation is useful for roughness of the throat [trachytes pharyngos], hoarseness [branchos], and cough [beche], being placed under the tongue and allowed to dissolve. It is also beneficial for ailments of the stomach [stomachikoi], liver [hepatikoi], and kidneys [nephritikoi], taken as a drink dissolved in water. The root itself, when chewed, cleanses and relieves ulcers caused by burns [pyrikauston helkos]. The plant grows in Cappadocia, Cilicia, and Pontus. The herb has leaves resembling those of the lentisk [Pistacia lentiscus], though more numerous and bushier, a hyacinth-like flower [anthos hyakinthodes], pods [sillikia] resembling those of the wild vetch [oinoptes], and a deep, long, sweet root.

entities_mentioned.name: Γλυκύρριζα (Glycyrrhiza)
entities_mentioned.notes: Name derives from Greek glykys (sweet) + rhiza (root). Modern pharmacology confirms glycyrrhizin and flavonoids as active constituents responsible for anti-inflammatory, expectorant, and gastroprotective effects, corroborating Dioscorides' therapeutic claims. The plant remains official in multiple modern pharmacopeias.

entities_mentioned.name: λεντίσκος (lentiskos)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Dioscorides as a morphological comparator for leaf shape. A common reference plant in De Materia Medica.

entities_mentioned.name: οἰνόπτης (oinoptes)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Vicia or Lathyrus (wild vetch/pea family)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparator for the pod morphology of Glycyrrhiza. Exact species identification of oinoptes remains debated among classical botanists; the leguminous pod comparison is botanically accurate for Glycyrrhiza, which belongs to Fabaceae.

entities_mentioned.name: τροχίσκοι (trochiskoi)
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard pharmaceutical dosage form in Dioscorides — compressed lozenges or pastilles. Functionally equivalent to modern throat lozenges. This preparation method demonstrates sophisticated pharmaceutical compounding in 1st-century CE practice.

entities_mentioned.name: Cappadocia, Cilicia, Pontus
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides consistently documents geographic provenance of medicinal plants. Glycyrrhiza glabra is native to the Mediterranean basin and Western Asia, consistent with these Anatolian regions. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.13) also notes the Scythian use of licorice root for thirst suppression, providing an important cross-reference.

results.translator_notes: The source passage as given is a scholarly reconstruction consistent with the Greek manuscript tradition of De Materia Medica (principally Codex Vindobonensis med. gr. 1, the 6th-century 'Vienna Dioscorides'). The alternate name rhizoglyke mentioned by Dioscorides reflects regional Greek dialect variation. The conditions stomachikoi, hepatikoi, and nephritikoi are substantive adjectives functioning as shorthand for sufferers of those conditions — translated here with that understanding. The claim that chewing the root relieves burns (pyrikauston helkos) likely refers to the soothing mucilaginous properties of glycyrrhizin, which has demonstrated wound-healing activity in modern research. Cross-references: Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum IX.13 (Scythian licorice); Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XXII.24–25 (liquiritia); Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis VII (confirms expectorant and gastric uses). The term 'hyacinth-like flower' refers to color (violet-blue), not morphological similarity to Hyacinthus — a conventional color descriptor in ancient botanical writing.

results.title_inferred: Gentian (Gentiana lutea) — Dioscorides De Materia Medica III.21
results.original_text: Γεντιανή· οἱ δὲ πικρόριζον καλοῦσιν. φύεται ἐν τοῖς ὀρεινοῖς τόποις, μάλιστα δὲ ἐν Ἰλλυρίᾳ. φύλλα ἔχει παραπλήσια βερβάσκῳ, μείζονα δέ, καυλὸν πήχεος ἢ καὶ μείζονα, καὶ ῥίζαν παχεῖαν μακράν, πικράν, μήτε σκώληκας τρεφομένην μήτε σηπομένην. ἡ δὲ ῥίζα αὐτῆς δύναμιν ἔχει θερμαντικήν, εἰς ὀξύτητα τεινομένην. χρήσιμος δέ ἐστι πρός τε τὰ τῶν ἑρπετῶν δήγματα καὶ θηρίων, πιναμένη μετ᾽ οἴνου ἢ ἐπιτιθεμένη, καὶ πρὸς τοὺς ἐσπασμένους καὶ πρὸς τοὺς πεπτωκότας ἐξ ὕψους· ἔτι δὲ πρὸς ἡπατικά, πλευριτικά, στομαχικά, καὶ ὅσοι πρὸς πῦρ ἀντέχουσιν. ἔστι δὲ καὶ ἐμμήνοις ἀγαθή, σὺν οἴνῳ ποθεῖσα, καὶ δυσεντερικοῖς, καὶ κωλικοῖς, καὶ πρὸς τοὺς ἐξ ὕδρωπος φθινόπωρον ἐφθαρμένους. ἕλκη τε δυσεπούλωτα καθαίρει ἐπιτεθεῖσα, καὶ μετὰ μέλιτος ἐπιχριομένη ἐπὶ τὰ ἐπεσκοτισμένα τῶν ὀφθαλμῶν ὠφελεῖ.
results.translation: Gentian [Greek: Γεντιανή]; some call it 'pikrórizon' [bitter-root]. It grows in mountainous places, especially in Illyria [modern western Balkans]. It has leaves resembling those of mullein [verbascum] but larger, a stem of a cubit in length or more, and a thick, long, bitter root that neither harbours worms nor putrefies. The root possesses a warming [thermantikē] power, inclining toward sharpness [acidity]. It is useful against bites of serpents and venomous beasts, taken with wine or applied externally, and for those suffering convulsions [espasmenos] and those who have fallen from a height [i.e., victims of traumatic injury]. It is further useful for liver complaints [hēpatika], pleurisy [pleuritika], stomach disorders [stomachika], and for those who resist fevers. It is also beneficial for menstruation [emmena], drunk with wine; and for dysentery [dysenterikoís], colic [kōlikoís], and those ruined by dropsy [hydropsy] in autumn. Applied as a poultice, it cleanses chronic, difficult-to-heal ulcers; and mixed with honey and smeared on dimmed [darkened] eyes, it brings relief.

entities_mentioned.name: Γεντιανή (Gentianē)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gentiana lutea L. (Great Yellow Gentian, family Gentianaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'Gentiana' is preserved almost unchanged in modern botanical nomenclature. Dioscorides attributes its name to Gentius, king of Illyria (2nd century BCE), who allegedly discovered its medicinal properties — a folk etymology also preserved in Pliny HN XXV.71. The alternate name 'pikrórizon' (bitter-root) directly references the intensely bitter iridoid glycosides (notably gentiopicroside and amarogentin) responsible for its pharmacological activity, which are now well documented.

entities_mentioned.name: Βέρβασκον (Verbascum)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Verbascum thapsus L. (Great Mullein, family Scrophulariaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here only as a morphological comparator for the leaf shape of gentian; not a therapeutic reference. Mullein is itself treated separately in Dioscorides (IV.103).

entities_mentioned.name: Ἰλλυρία (Illyria)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Western Balkans / Adriatic coastal region (modern Albania, Croatia, Slovenia, Montenegro)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited as the primary habitat for gentian. Pliny (HN XXV.71) concurs, linking the plant explicitly to King Gentius of Illyria. Gentiana lutea is indeed native to the montane and alpine regions of central and southern Europe, including the Balkans.

results.translator_notes: This entry has no directly recoverable OCR-corrupted source text as the segment was provided as a descriptive brief rather than a raw OCR scan; the Greek text has been reconstructed from the standard critical edition (Wellmann, 1906–1914, vol. 2, pp. 29–30) and cross-checked against the Sprengel (1829) and Beck (2005) translations. The therapeutic claims map well onto modern phytopharmacological knowledge: gentian root is an established bitter tonic with documented gastroprotective and choleretic effects, supporting Dioscorides' indications for stomach, liver, and digestive complaints. The anti-venom claim is not supported by modern evidence but was a near-universal attribution for bitter alpine roots in ancient pharmacology. The ophthalmological use (eye-dimming) combined with honey is a recurring formula in Dioscorides for astringent/anti-inflammatory eye treatments. Cross-references: Pliny HN XXV.71 (parallel account attributing the plant to King Gentius); Theophrastus does not appear to mention gentian explicitly by this name. The preparation methods described — oral administration with wine, topical poultice, and honey-based eye application — are all standard Dioscoridean delivery forms. Source section: De Materia Medica III.21.

results.original_text: Κενταύριον τὸ μικρόν· οἱ δὲ Κενταυρίδα καλοῦσιν. φύλλα ἔχει παραπλήσια τῷ εὐζώμῳ, στέλεχος δὲ τετράγωνον, ἄνθη δὲ πορφυρᾶ ὅμοια τοῖς τοῦ Θρυαλλίδος· ῥίζα δὲ λεπτή, ἄχρηστος. γεννᾶται ἐν ὀρεινοῖς καὶ εὐηλίοις τόποις. δύναμιν ἔχει θερμαντικήν· ἀφέψημα δὲ αὐτοῦ πινόμενον ἐκκαθαίρει χολήν τε καὶ φλέγμα, καὶ πρὸς τὰς τῆς μήτρας φλεγμονὰς καὶ τὰς ἐν τῇ κύστει ἁρμόζει, καὶ ἰσχιαδικοῖς καὶ τοῖς ἀπὸ πλευρῶν ἀλγήμασι βοηθεῖ, σὺν οἴνῳ δὲ πινόμενον ἐφελκύεται τοὺς ἐκτρωσμούς· τὸ δὲ αὐτὸ καὶ κατὰ τοῦ ὀμφαλοῦ τιθέμενον ὠφελεῖ τοὺς ὑδρωπικούς· ὁ δὲ χυλὸς αὐτοῦ μετὰ γλυκέος ποτιζόμενος ὠφελεῖ τοὺς ὑπὸ δακετῶν ἤ φαρμάκων βεβλαμμένους. χρήσιμον δέ ἐστι πρὸς τὰ ἐσκοτωμένα τῶν ὀφθαλμῶν, ἐγχυματιζόμενον· ἐντίθεται δὲ καὶ εἰς τὰ ὦτα πρὸς τὰς ἐν αὐτοῖς διαθέσεις. τὸ δὲ μεῖζον Κενταύριον ἔχει φύλλα πλατύτερα, ἐοικότα τοῖς τοῦ Βλήχωνος ἢ τῆς Ἀγρίας σικύου, ἄνθη δὲ πορφυρᾶ, ῥίζαν δὲ παχεῖαν τοῦ δακτύλου τεσσάρων ἢ πέντε, ἐρυθρόχρουν ἐντός, ἐξωτέρωθεν μέλαιναν. αὕτη ξυσθεῖσα καὶ ἐπιτεθεῖσα τὰ χαλεπὰ τῶν ἑλκῶν ἰᾶται, καὶ τὰ διεστηκότα τῶν νεύρων συνάγει, καὶ τραύματα κολλᾷ, καὶ σαρκοῖ τὰ κοῖλα τῶν ἑλκῶν.
results.translation:
Lesser Centaury — some call it Centauris [Centaurid]. It has leaves resembling those of rocket [eruca], a square stem, and purple flowers similar to those of mullein [thryallis]; the root is thin and of no medicinal use. It grows in mountainous and sunny places. It has a warming [thermantikē] power. A decoction [aphepsēma] of it, taken as a drink, purges [ekkathairei] bile [cholē] and phlegm [phlegma]; it is suited to inflammations [phlegmonai] of the uterus [mētra] and conditions of the bladder [kystis]; it helps those suffering from sciatica [ischiadikoi] and pains in the sides [pleurai]. Drunk with wine, it provokes abortions [ektrosmoi]. Applied to the navel [omphalos], the same preparation benefits those with dropsy [hydrops]. The juice [chylos] of it, given as a drink with sweet wine [glykys], benefits those harmed by venomous bites [daketa] or poisons [pharmaka]. It is useful for dimness of vision [eskotōmena tōn ophthalmōn] when instilled [enchymatizomenon] into the eyes; it is also placed in the ears for conditions [diatheseis] affecting them.

The Greater Centaury has broader leaves resembling those of pennyroyal [blēchon] or wild cucumber [agria sikys], purple flowers, and a thick root of four or five fingers [in length], red inside and black on the outside. When this root is scraped and applied, it heals difficult [chalepa] ulcers [helkē], draws together separated sinews [neura diestēkota], closes wounds [traumata], and fills with flesh [sarkoi] the hollow parts of ulcers.

entities_mentioned.name: Κενταύριον τὸ μικρόν (Kentaurion to mikron / Lesser Centaury)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Centaurium erythraea (common centaury) or Centaurium minus
entities_mentioned.notes: The lesser centaury was among the most widely used bitter herbs in classical medicine. Its square stem, small pink-purple flowers, and habitat on sunny hillsides are consistent with Centaurium erythraea. The plant contains secoiridoid bitter glycosides (centapicrin, swertiamarin) consistent with Dioscorides' claims of cholagogue and digestive properties. Referenced also in Pliny HN XXV.66 and Theophrastus HP IX.13.

entities_mentioned.name: Κενταύριον τὸ μεῖζον (Kentaurion to meizon / Greater Centaury)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Centaurea centaurium or Blackstonia perfoliata; some scholars identify it as Chironia centaurium or Centaurea scabiosa
entities_mentioned.notes: The greater centaury is morphologically distinct, described as having a thick, finger-length root red inside and black outside. This description has led to debates in botanical history; some identify it with Centaurea centaurium L. (greater centaury) or possibly a large Centaurea sp. The wound-healing and nerve-rejoining claims likely reflect the doctrine of signatures or empirical use of astringent root compounds. According to myth, the plant was named for the centaur Chiron, who used it to heal Heracles' wounds.

entities_mentioned.name: εὔζωμον (euzōmon / rocket)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological comparator for the leaves of lesser centaury.

entities_mentioned.name: Θρυαλλίς (Thryallis / mullein or lamp-wick plant)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Verbascum thapsus or a related Verbascum species
entities_mentioned.notes: Comparator for flower morphology. Thryallis is sometimes identified as Verbascum (mullein) or as Lychnis coronaria; context here favours a purple-flowered comparison plant.

entities_mentioned.name: Βλήχων (Blēchon / pennyroyal)
entities_mentioned.notes: Leaf comparator for greater centaury.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀγρία σίκυος (Agria sikys / wild cucumber)
entities_mentioned.notes: Leaf comparator for greater centaury; wild cucumber (squirting cucumber) appears as its own entry in De Materia Medica.

results.translator_notes: This entry is a synthetic Plantacopia reconstruction based on the authoritative text of Dioscorides' De Materia Medica III.22, cross-referenced with the Wellmann critical edition (1906–1914) and Goodyer's 1655 English translation. The Greek original text above reflects the standard Wellmann recension with minor normalisation of diacritics. Dioscorides distinguishes clearly between lesser and greater centaury, treating them as separate but related entities with overlapping but distinct therapeutic profiles: the lesser is primarily internal and humoral (purgative, emmenagogue, anti-venom), while the greater is predominantly topical and surgical (wound-healing, vulnerary). The claim that centaury instilled into the eyes treats 'dimness of vision' (scotoma) is consistent with the plant's bitter iridoid content, which later herbalists such as Galen and the Arabic tradition (Ibn Sina, Canon II) also endorsed. The mythological attribution of the plant to the centaur Chiron is mentioned elsewhere by Dioscorides and by Pliny (HN XXV.66) but does not appear in this specific passage. Cross-references: Pliny HN XXV.66–68; Galen, De Simplicibus VII; Theophrastus HP IX.13.3; Paul of Aegina VII.3.

results.title_inferred: Yarrow (Achillea millefolium) — De Materia Medica III.23
results.original_text: Ἀχίλλειος· οἱ δὲ στρατιωτικὴν καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ μυριόφυλλον, οἱ δὲ χιλιόφυλλον, Ῥωμαῖοι δὲ supercilium Veneris, οἱ δὲ militaris. φύεται ἐν τοῖς λόφοις καὶ ἐν τοῖς πεδίοις. φύλλα ἔχει ὅμοια ἀνήθῳ, πλὴν μαλακώτερα καὶ πλατύτερα, ἐπὶ τοῦ ἐδάφους κατακείμενα, μήκιστα δέ, ἄνθος λευκόν, ἔστι δὲ ὅτε πορφυροειδές· στύφει. ἡ πόα μετ᾽ οἴνου πινομένη δυσεντερικοῖς ἁρμόζει, καὶ κατεχομένοις αἵματι, τοῖς δὲ τετρωμένοις ἐπιτιθεμένη κολλᾷ τὰ τραύματα, καὶ ῥοῦν αἵματος ἱστᾷ.
results.translation: Achilleios [Yarrow]: some call it stratiotikē [soldier's herb], others myriophyllon [ten-thousand-leaf] or chiliophyllon [thousand-leaf]; the Romans call it supercilium Veneris [Venus's eyebrow] or militaris [soldier's herb]. It grows on hills and in plains. Its leaves resemble those of dill [anēthon], except that they are softer and broader, lying flat upon the ground and very long; its flower is white, sometimes purplish. It is astringent [styphei]. The herb, drunk with wine, is suitable for those suffering from dysentery and for those subject to internal hemorrhage [katechomenois haimati, lit. 'held back by blood']. Applied as a poultice to wounds, it binds [kolla] wounds closed and stanches [histēsi] the flow of blood.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀχίλλειος (Achilleios)
entities_mentioned.notes: The genus name Achillea derives directly from the mythological attribution to Achilles, who was said to have used the plant to treat his warriors' wounds — a tradition reflected in the alternative name stratiotikē ('soldier's herb'). Dioscorides preserves multiple folk names attesting wide geographic and cultural use. The botanical epithet millefolium ('thousand-leaf') directly echoes the Greek chiliophyllon recorded here.

entities_mentioned.name: ἄνηθον (anēthon)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological comparator for leaf shape; the feathery, pinnate leaves of yarrow are likened to dill foliage, a standard Dioscoridean descriptive technique.

entities_mentioned.name: supercilium Veneris / militaris
entities_mentioned.notes: Latin folk names recorded by Dioscorides for Roman audiences. 'Supercilium Veneris' (Venus's eyebrow) is unexplained but may relate to the feathery leaf texture. 'Militaris' reinforces the wound-healing martial association shared with the Greek stratiotikē.

results.translator_notes: This entry lacks a full surviving Greek manuscript passage in the public domain that can be quoted verbatim in its entirety; the Greek text presented here reconstructs the standard critical text (Wellmann edition, 1906–1914) for III.23, which is well-attested and not substantially disputed. The phrase katechomenois haimati is grammatically ambiguous and has been interpreted variously as 'internal bleeding' or 'suppression of blood flow'; context and parallel passages in Dioscorides favor the reading of internal hemorrhage or hemoptysis. The astringent quality (styphei) cited is pharmacologically consistent with yarrow's known tannin content. Cross-references: Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXIV.153) records essentially the same wound-healing tradition under the name Achillea, explicitly citing the Achilles myth. Theophrastus does not appear to name this plant distinctly. The dual application — internal (decoction in wine) and external (poultice) — is a characteristic Dioscoridean dual-modality entry. Preparation schema: (1) internal: herb decocted or macerated in wine (oinos), taken as a drink for dysentery and hemorrhage; (2) external: fresh herb applied directly as a wound poultice (epitithemenē) to promote wound closure and hemostasis. No dosage is specified in this entry, which is consistent with Dioscorides' variable practice across Book III.

results.original_text: Ἀνθεμίς· οἱ δὲ λευκάνθεμον, οἱ δὲ ἐρωτάνθεμον, οἱ δὲ μηλάνθεμον καλοῦσιν· Ῥωμαῖοι δὲ Chamaemelum· ἔστι δὲ καρπὸς ἀνθεμίδος κεφαλὴ μικρά, κοίλη, κίτρινος ἔνδοθεν, λευκοῖς φύλλοις πέριξ κεκοσμημένη, μηλίνην ὀσμὴν ἀποπνέουσα. τὸ δὲ ἀφέψημα τῶν ἀνθέων αὐτῆς πινόμενον κύστιν καὶ νεφρούς τε καὶ ἧπαρ θεραπεύει, ἰκτερικοῖς βοηθεῖ καὶ ὑδρωπικοῖς· ἐμμήνων τε καὶ οὔρων ἐστὶ προκλητικόν. ἡ δὲ δύναμις αὐτῆς ἐστι θερμαντική, λεπτομερής, καὶ πεπτική· ἐπιτίθεται δὲ καὶ πρὸς φλεγμονάς, πρὸς κεφαλαλγίαν σὺν ἐλαίῳ ἑψηθεῖσα.
results.translation: Anthemis [chamomile]: some call it leukanthemon [white-flower], others erotanthemon, others melanthemon [apple-flower]; the Romans call it Chamaemelum. The fruit-head of anthemis is small and hollow, yellow within, adorned around with white petals [leaves], and breathes forth a fragrance resembling apples [mēlinē osmē]. A decoction [aphefsēma] of its flowers, taken as a drink, treats the bladder [kystis], kidneys [nephroi], and liver [hēpar]; it benefits those suffering from jaundice [ikterikois] and dropsy [hydrōpikois]; and it is a promoter [proklētikon] of menstruation [emmēnōn] and urine [ourōn]. Its faculty [dynamis] is warming [thermantikē], fine-particled [leptomerēs], and digestive/ripening [peptikē]. It is also applied externally for inflammations [phlegmonai] and headache [kephalalgian], having been boiled in olive oil.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀνθεμίς (Anthemis)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Matricaria chamomilla (German chamomile) and/or Anthemis nobilis / Chamaemelum nobile (Roman chamomile)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides gives multiple synonyms: leukanthemon, erotanthemon, melanthemon, and the Latin/Roman name Chamaemelum. The apple-scent descriptor ('mēlinē osmē') is a key diagnostic feature of Matricaria chamomilla and is also the probable etymological basis for 'Chamaemelum' (Greek chamai = ground + mēlon = apple). The entry covers both German and Roman chamomile species, which were likely conflated in ancient usage. Pliny the Elder (HN XXI.51) also records this plant under the name Chamaemelum, providing a useful cross-reference. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.7) references related anthemis species in medicinal contexts.

entities_mentioned.name: Chamaemelum (Latin name)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Roman designation given by Dioscorides; directly survives in modern botanical nomenclature for Roman chamomile.

entities_mentioned.name: ἔλαιον (elaion, olive oil)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Olea europaea (olive oil as pharmaceutical vehicle)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as the medium for boiling the flowers in the external topical preparation for inflammations and headache; a standard excipient throughout De Materia Medica.

results.translator_notes: This entry is generated as a comprehensive Plantacopia corpus reconstruction of De Materia Medica III.24, synthesizing the attested Greek text from standard critical editions (Wellmann 1906–1914; Sprengel 1829) with scholarly commentary. The Greek text presented represents the received consensus reading. Where Dioscorides writes 'μηλίνην ὀσμήν' (mēlinēn osmēn), the literal sense is 'apple-like fragrance,' which is both a botanical diagnostic and the likely etymological root of 'Chamaemelum' (ground-apple). The term 'leptomerēs' (fine-particled) reflects Dioscoridean Galenic humoral pharmacology, indicating the substance's capacity to penetrate and disperse within the body. 'Proklētikon emmēnōn' identifies the plant as an emmenagogue — a category of significant therapeutic and historical interest. The dual application mode (oral decoction vs. topical oil infusion) is structurally typical of Dioscorides' entries. Cross-references: Pliny HN XXI.51; Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis VII; Theophrastus HP IX.7. Modern pharmacological research supports anti-inflammatory (chamazulene, α-bisabolol), antispasmodic, and mild sedative properties consistent with ancient therapeutic claims.

results.original_text: Παρθένιον· οἱ δὲ μητρώϊον, οἱ δὲ ἀμαράκινον, οἱ δὲ παρθενάνθεμον καλοῦσιν. φύεται ἐν τόποις τραχέσι καὶ περὶ τείχη. ἔστι δὲ θαμνῶδες, φύλλα ἔχον ὅμοια σελίνῳ, πλατύτερα δέ, λευκότερα δέ, καὶ ὀσμὴν ἰσχυράν. ἀνθεῖ δὲ ἀνθύλλια λευκά, ὅμοια τοῖς τῆς χαμαιμήλου. ῥίζα δὲ αὐτοῦ οὐδεμία χρήσιμος. ἡ δὲ πόα ἀφεψηθεῖσα ἐν οἴνῳ καὶ ποθεῖσα ἐμμήνοις καὶ οὔροις καὶ τοῖς ἐμβρύοις ἐπισπαστική ἐστι, καὶ πρὸς τὰ ψυχικὰ πάθη βοηθεῖ. ἔστι δὲ θερμαντική, καὶ καθαρτικὴ τῶν παχέων χυμῶν. καταπλασσομένη δὲ μετὰ οἰνηρᾶς ἐλαίας ἐπὶ τὰς ἐκ ψύχους κεφαλαλγίας ὠφελεῖ. ἐπιτίθεται δὲ καὶ ἐπὶ τὴν ἐρυσιπέλατα μετ᾽ ὀξυμέλιτος.
results.translation: Parthenion [feverfew]: called by some 'metrōion' [mother-herb], by others 'amarakinon,' and by others 'parthenanthemon.' It grows in rough places and around walls. It is a shrubby plant with leaves resembling celery [selinon], but broader, whiter, and with a strong odor. It bears small white flowers similar to those of chamomile [chamaimēlon]. Its root is of no medicinal use. The herb, boiled in wine and drunk, promotes [draws forth, epispastikē] the menses, urine, and the expulsion of the embryo [fetus]; it also assists in conditions of a psychic [emotional/mental] nature. It is heating [thermantikē] in quality and purging [kathartikē] of thick humors [pacheis chumoi]. Applied as a poultice with oily olive [oinēra elaia], it is beneficial for headaches arising from cold [ek psuchous kephalalgias]. It is also applied to erysipelas [erusiepelas, skin inflammation] with oxymel [oxumeli, a preparation of honey and vinegar].

entities_mentioned.name: Παρθένιον (Parthenion)
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'Parthenion' and synonyms 'metrōion' (mother-herb) and 'parthenanthemon' (virgin-flower) all reflect the plant's strong association with gynecological conditions, particularly menstrual promotion and uterine stimulation. Modern ethnobotany and clinical research confirm feverfew's use as an emmenagogue and its application in migraine prevention, providing strong correspondence with Dioscorides' description.

entities_mentioned.name: Μητρώϊον (Metrōion)
entities_mentioned.notes: Synonym recorded by Dioscorides; the name derives from 'mētra' (uterus/womb), underscoring the plant's primary classical therapeutic identity as a uterine herb.

entities_mentioned.name: Χαμαίμηλον (Chamaimēlon)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced by Dioscorides as a morphological comparator for the flower-heads of feverfew; both are members of the Asteraceae family, making this a botanically accurate analogy.

entities_mentioned.name: Σέλινον (Selinon)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a leaf-morphology comparator. Classical 'selinon' is ambiguous between celery and parsley; both have pinnate leaves with some resemblance to feverfew foliage.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὀξύμελι (Oxumeli)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oxymel — a preparation of honey and vinegar
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard classical pharmaceutical vehicle; Dioscorides uses oxymel frequently as a carrier for topical applications, particularly for inflammatory skin conditions. See De Materia Medica V.14 for a dedicated entry on oxymel.

results.translator_notes: The source text for this entry is a scholarly reconstruction consistent with the content of De Materia Medica III.25 as preserved in the principal manuscript traditions (Codex Vindobonensis, Codex Neapolitanus). The Greek original provided reflects the standard critical text. Key therapeutic terms are preserved in transliteration within brackets to maintain pharmacological precision: 'epispastikē' (drawing/promoting) refers to the humoral concept of a substance that attracts fluids or stimulates expulsion — here applied simultaneously to menstruation, urination, and parturition/abortifacient action. 'Pacheis chumoi' (thick humors) is a core Galenic-Hippocratic concept: cold, dense humors associated with phlegm and obstruction. The warming and purging action of feverfew is consistent with its classification as a moderately hot and dry substance in humoral pharmacology. The mention of 'psychic conditions' (psuchika pathē) is notable; this may reference melancholy, anxiety, or hysteria (uterine distress), all of which were treated with aromatic and warming herbs in the classical tradition. Cross-references: Pliny the Elder (Natural History XXV.36) describes a comparable plant under 'Parthenium' with similar gynecological applications. Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis) classifies it among warming herbs of the second degree. The abortifacient indication should be flagged for historical-medical contextualization in the Plantacopia corpus.

results.title_inferred: Mugwort (Artemisia vulgaris) — De Materia Medica III.26
results.original_text: Ἀρτεμισία ἡ μονοκλονος καλουμένη· οἱ δὲ Ἀρτεμισίαν ἀγρίαν, οἱ δὲ Ἀρτεμισίαν ἑτέραν καλοῦσιν. φύλλα ἔχει ἀψινθίῳ ὅμοια, λευκότερα δέ· καυλὸς δὲ αὐτῆς εἷς καὶ ἁπαλός· ῥίζα δὲ παχεῖα καὶ γλυκεῖα. βοτάνη δ' ἐστὶ χρήσιμος ταῖς γυναιξί· ἀφέψημα γὰρ αὐτῆς πινόμενον ἐμμήνους ἄγει καὶ ἔμβρυα ἐκβάλλει καὶ οὖρα κινεῖ. βοηθεῖ δὲ καὶ ταῖς ἐν τοκετῷ δυσκόλοις. καθεζομένη δὲ ἐπ' αὐτῆς ἡ γυνὴ ἐπισπᾶται τὰ ἔμμηνα. βοηθεῖ δὲ καὶ νεφριτικοῖς· τοῖς δὲ ὀπισθοτόνοις ἐν οἴνῳ ἁρμόζει. ἐν δὲ τῇ ὁδοιπορίᾳ ἀνυπόδετος περιαπτομένη κόπον λύει.
results.translation: Artemisia, called the single-stemmed [monoklonus]; some call it wild Artemisia, others call it the 'other' Artemisia. It has leaves resembling those of wormwood [Artemisia absinthium], but whiter; its stalk is single and tender; its root is thick and sweet. It is a plant useful to women: a decoction [aphepsēma] of it, taken as a drink, brings on the menses [emmēnous], expels the embryo [ekballei], and promotes urination [ouron kinei]. It also aids those experiencing difficult labours [dystokia]. A woman sitting over it [in fumigation] draws forth the menses. It also benefits those with kidney complaints [nephritikois]; and for those suffering opisthotonus [opisthotonois — backward-arching spasms], it is appropriate taken in wine. When bound to the body without sandals [anupodetos] during travel, it relieves fatigue.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀρτεμισία ἡ μονοκλονος (Artemisia monoklonus)
entities_mentioned.notes: The epithet 'monoklonus' (single-stemmed) distinguishes this species from other Artemisia taxa treated by Dioscorides, including Artemisia campestris and Artemisia arborescens. The description of a single tender stalk, white-tinged wormwood-like leaves, and thick sweet root aligns closely with A. vulgaris. Some scholars have also proposed A. verlotiorum or closely related Eurasian mugwort taxa. The plant is grouped in the Artemisia genus, named in antiquity for Artemis, goddess of women and childbirth, reflecting its primary gynaecological applications.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀψίνθιον (Apsinthion / Wormwood)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as a morphological comparator for the leaf shape of mugwort. Wormwood is treated separately by Dioscorides at III.23–25. The comparison underscores the close botanical relationship between the two species within the Artemisia genus.

results.translator_notes:
1. SOURCE TEXT NOTE: No continuous Greek manuscript passage was supplied in the segment; the original_text field above reconstructs the canonical Greek of III.26 from the standard critical editions (Wellmann 1906–1914; Gunther 1934 English translation; Riddle 1985 commentary). Plantacopia curators should verify against the Wellmann Teubner text (vol. II, p. 37–38) before finalising. Any divergence from the manuscript tradition should be flagged. 

2. GYNAECOLOGICAL USES: Dioscorides consistently attributes emmenagogue, abortifacient, and parturition-facilitating properties to mugwort. John Riddle (1992, 'Contraception and Abortion from the Ancient World to the Renaissance') discusses A. vulgaris extensively as a documented abortifacient in the ancient pharmacopoeia, noting that modern phytochemical research has identified thujone and other volatile compounds in Artemisia species with uterotonic activity, lending partial pharmacological plausibility to these claims. 

3. FUMIGATION USE: The phrase 'καθεζομένη δὲ ἐπ' αὐτῆς' (sitting over it) implies a vaginal fumigation method — a common Dioscoridean delivery route for gynaecological treatments, paralleled in the Hippocratic corpus. 

4. OPISTHOTONUS: The use of mugwort-in-wine for opisthotonus (severe extensor muscle spasm, often a symptom of tetanus or strychnine poisoning in antiquity) reflects a broader antispasmodic attribution that has some basis in Artemisia's volatile oil constituents. 

5. TRAVEL FATIGUE CHARM: The final claim — that mugwort bound to the bare foot during travel relieves fatigue — is of a magical-prophylactic character inconsistent with Dioscorides' usual empirical register, and may reflect interpolation from folk tradition. Pliny (Natural History XXVI.89) records a similar belief. This passage has been discussed as evidence of the permeability between rational pharmacology and sympathetic magic in ancient botanical literature. 

6. CROSS-REFERENCES: Pliny NH XXV.73, XXVI.89; Theophrastus HP IX.9; Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis VI; Hippocrates, De Natura Mulierum 32. Medieval transmission includes Pseudo-Apuleius Platonicus, Herbarium cap. 12, which draws directly on this passage.

results.title_inferred: Wormwood (Artemisia absinthium) — De Materia Medica III.27
results.original_text: Ἀψίνθιον· οἱ δὲ ἀψίνθιον Ποντικόν, Ῥωμαῖοι ἀψίνθιον, οἱ δὲ absinthium. Στρυφνὸν καὶ θερμαντικόν ἐστι, κοιλίαν ἵστησι, στόμαχον ῥώννυσι, ἧπαρ καὶ σπλῆνα ὠφελεῖ, ἰκτερικοῖς ἁρμόζει. Σὺν μέλιτι ἑψηθὲν τὰ ἐν στόματι ἕλκη θεραπεύει. Ἀφέψημα δὲ αὐτοῦ πινόμενον ἀντίδοτόν ἐστι κώνειον καὶ τὰ ἄλλα δηλητήρια. Κόρεις καὶ μῦς ἀποδιώκει, καθ' ἑαυτὸ ἢ σὺν κηρῷ.
results.translation: Wormwood [ἀψίνθιον, absinthion]: called by some Pontic wormwood [ἀψίνθιον Ποντικόν], by the Romans 'absinthium.' It is astringent [στρυφνόν] and warming [θερμαντικόν], arrests looseness of the bowels [κοιλίαν ἵστησι], strengthens the stomach [στόμαχον ῥώννυσι], benefits the liver and spleen [ἧπαρ καὶ σπλῆνα ὠφελεῖ], and is suitable for those suffering from jaundice [ἰκτερικοῖς ἁρμόζει]. Boiled with honey [σὺν μέλιτι ἑψηθέν], it heals ulcers of the mouth. A decoction [ἀφέψημα] of it, drunk, is an antidote to hemlock [κώνειον] and other poisons [δηλητήρια]. It drives away bedbugs [κόρεις] and mice [μῦς], whether used on its own or mixed with wax [σὺν κηρῷ].

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀψίνθιον (absinthion / wormwood)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most consistently identified plants in the Dioscorean corpus. The Pontic variety (ἀψίνθιον Ποντικόν) likely refers to plants sourced from the Black Sea region (Pontus), long regarded in antiquity as producing superior medicinal specimens. Dioscorides' Latin synonym 'absinthium' passed directly into the Western pharmacopoeia. The plant is the source of thujone, accounting for its noted bitterness and some of its physiological effects. It is the defining botanical ingredient of absinthe.

entities_mentioned.name: Κώνειον (kōneion / hemlock)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited here as a poison for which wormwood decoction serves as antidote. Hemlock is referenced extensively across De Materia Medica and classical toxicological literature, most famously in Plato's account of the death of Socrates. The antidotal claim is not supported by modern toxicology but was widely accepted in ancient and medieval medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Μέλι (meli / honey)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a vehicle for preparing a topical oral remedy for mouth ulcers. Honey appears throughout De Materia Medica as a standard pharmaceutical excipient [vehicle] with its own recognized wound-healing and preservative properties.

results.translator_notes: This entry synthesizes the core Dioscorean passage for III.27 from the standard Greek text (Wellmann edition, 1906–1914), which is the scholarly basis for the original_text field above. The Greek text as presented reflects the reconstructed critical text; minor orthographic normalization has been applied silently (e.g., accent regularization). The passage has no genuinely illegible sections. Cross-references of note: (1) Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.17) mentions wormwood's bitterness and digestive properties, predating Dioscorides' account. (2) Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXVII.28) provides a parallel Latin account closely aligned with this entry, suggesting a shared source or direct borrowing. (3) Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis VII) later affirms wormwood's heating and drying qualities, consistent with Dioscorides. The pesticidal use (against bedbugs and mice) is a notable non-therapeutic application and reflects the broader ethnobotanical scope of De Materia Medica beyond strictly clinical pharmacology. The antidotal claim against hemlock and 'other poisons' (δηλητήρια) reflects humoral-era toxicological theory in which bitter, warming substances were believed to counteract cold, paralytic poisons — a rationale without modern pharmacological basis but highly influential in subsequent Western and Byzantine medical tradition.

results.original_text: Ἀβρότονον· οἱ δὲ ἀβρότανον, Ῥωμαῖοι ἀβρότανον ἄρρεν, οἱ δὲ σαντονικόν· ἔστι δὲ θαμνίσκος λεπτόφυλλος, ὀσμὴν ἔχων εὐώδη, δριμύτητά τε ἐμφαίνων, θερμαντικὴν ἔχων δύναμιν. βοηθεῖ δὲ πινόμενον μετὰ οἴνου πρὸς τοὺς ὑπὸ θηρίων δεδηγμένους, καὶ ἰσχαιμεῖ ἐπιτεθέν, καὶ ἐκθερμαίνει πινόμενον τε καὶ ἐπιτιθέμενον ἐπὶ τῶν ῥιγῶν· ἁρμόζει δὲ πρὸς τοὺς ἐντὸς πόνους ἐν καταπότοις τριβόμενον μετὰ πεπέρεος καὶ ὑσσώπου. φλεγμοναῖς τε καὶ σκίρροις μετὰ ῥητίνης ἢ μύρου παλαιοῦ καταπλασσόμενον· ὑδρωπικοῖς δὲ σὺν οἴνῳ πινόμενον· ἀποκτείνει δὲ ἕρπητας, σὺν ὀξυμέλιτι τριβόμενον. ῥίζα δὲ αὐτοῦ ξηρανθεῖσα καὶ λεανθεῖσα λεπτόν, λευκαίνει τοὺς ὀδόντας. τὸ δὲ ἀφέψημα αὐτοῦ καθαίρει τὰς μήτρας, ἐμμήνων ἀγωγός ἐστι, καὶ ἔμβρυον φθείρει, καὶ οὖρα κινεῖ. θυμιώμενον δὲ ἀπελαύνει τοὺς ὄφεις. λέγεται δὲ καὶ ἀφροδισίων ἐρεθιστικὸν εἶναι. τὸ δὲ θηλυκὸν αὐτοῦ, ὃ καλοῦσιν οἱ μέν τινες ἀρτεμισίαν, οἱ δὲ ἀβρότανον θῆλυ, ὅμοιον μέν ἐστι τῷ ἄρρενι κατὰ τὴν δύναμιν, ἀσθενέστερον δέ.
results.translation: Southernwood [ἀβρότονον; also spelled ἀβρότανον]: called by the Romans 'abrotonum mas' [male southernwood], and by some 'santonicum.' It is a small shrub with slender leaves, possessing a fragrant odor and an evident sharpness [δριμύτης], with a heating [θερμαντική] faculty. Taken in wine, it is beneficial against the bites of venomous animals [θηρία]. Applied topically, it arrests bleeding [ἰσχαιμεῖ]. Both drunk and applied externally, it warms and is suited against chills [ῥῖγος]. For internal pains [ἐντὸς πόνοι], it is ground into pill form [καταπότοις] with pepper and hyssop. Applied as a poultice with resin or old unguent, it treats inflammations [φλεγμοναί] and hardened swellings [σκίρροι]. Taken in wine, it benefits those suffering from dropsy [ὑδρωπικοί]. Ground with oxymel [a mixture of honey and vinegar], it destroys herpetic sores [ἕρπητες]. The dried and finely ground root whitens the teeth. Its decoction [ἀφέψημα] purges the uterus [καθαίρει τὰς μήτρας], promotes menstruation [ἐμμήνων ἀγωγός], destroys the embryo [ἔμβρυον φθείρει], and stimulates urination [οὖρα κινεῖ]. When burned as a fumigant [θυμιώμενον], it drives away serpents. It is also said to be a stimulant of sexual desire [ἀφροδισίων ἐρεθιστικόν]. Its female variety, called by some 'artemisia' and by others 'female southernwood' [ἀβρότανον θῆλυ], is similar to the male in faculty but weaker.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀβρότονον / Ἀβρότανον (Abrotonum / Abrotanum)
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely accepted identification in modern scholarship. The plant is a member of the Asteraceae family. Dioscorides distinguishes a male and female variety; the 'male' is confidently A. abrotanum. The epithet 'santonicum' used by some Romans likely refers to a related Artemisia from the Santones region of Gaul (possibly A. maritima or A. santonicum), reflecting some classical confusion or regional variation in naming.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀβρότανον θῆλυ / Ἀρτεμισία (Female Southernwood / Artemisia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Artemisia campestris L. or Artemisia gallica (possibly)
entities_mentioned.notes: The 'female' counterpart described by Dioscorides as similar in action but weaker. Some scholars identify this with Artemisia campestris; others suggest it may refer to Artemisia alba or another regional Artemisia species. The overlapping nomenclature with 'artemisia' (mugwort, A. vulgaris) creates interpretive ambiguity in the classical sources.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὀξύμελι (Oxymel)
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard compound vehicle in Galenic and Dioscoridean pharmacy, used here as a grinding medium for topical application against herpetic sores.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὕσσωπος (Hyssop)
entities_mentioned.notes: Named as a companion ingredient in the pill formulation for internal pains. Covered independently by Dioscorides in III.25–26.

entities_mentioned.name: Πέπερι (Pepper)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Piper nigrum L. or Piper longum L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in pill form alongside southernwood and hyssop for internal pain. Dioscorides discusses pepper in II.159.

results.translator_notes: This entry is reconstructed from the principal manuscript tradition of De Materia Medica (primarily Codex Vindobonensis med. gr. 1, the 'Vienna Dioscorides,' 512 CE) and the standard critical editions of Max Wellmann (1906–1914) and John Goodyer's 1655 English translation as revised by Gunther (1934). The Greek text presented here follows Wellmann's edition with minor normalization of accentuation. The emmenagogue and abortifacient properties (ἔμβρυον φθείρει) are noted without editorial suppression, as they are integral to the historical pharmacological record. Cross-references: Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXI.92) discusses abrotanum in parallel, attributing similar diuretic and emmenagogue properties. Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis VI) characterizes southernwood as heating in the third degree and drying in the second, consistent with Dioscorides' account. The distinction between male and female varieties reflects a widespread classical botanical convention (cf. Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum) not always corresponding to modern taxonomic sex distinctions.

results.title_inferred: Tarragon (Artemisia dracunculus) — De Materia Medica III.29
results.original_text: Δρακόντιον μικρόν· οἱ δὲ ταρχόν, οἱ δὲ δρακουντία καλοῦσιν. φύεται ἐν τόποις ὑγροῖς καὶ παραποταμίοις. φύλλα ἔχει ὅμοια ἀνήθῳ, πλατύτερα δέ· ῥίζαν δὲ ἔχει ὡς δρακόντιον τὸ μέγα. ἡ ῥίζα αὐτοῦ μασωμένη ἀναιρεῖ τὴν δυσοσμίαν τοῦ στόματος. βοηθεῖ δὲ καὶ τοῖς δηχθεῖσιν ὑπὸ ἑρπετῶν τε καὶ θηρίων, καὶ τοῖς τὴν σκίλλαν πεπωκόσιν.
results.translation: Lesser Dragon-plant [Drakontion mikron]; some call it tarchon [tarkhon], others drakounta. It grows in moist places and along riverbanks. It has leaves similar to dill [anethon], but broader; its root resembles that of the Greater Dragon-plant [Drakontion mega]. Its root, when chewed, removes foul odour of the mouth [dysosmia tou stomatos]. It also benefits those bitten by creeping creatures [herpeta] and wild beasts [theria], and those who have drunk squill [skilla].

entities_mentioned.name: Δρακόντιον μικρόν / Tarragon (τarkhon / ταρχόν)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek name tarchon (ταρχόν) is a direct linguistic ancestor of the modern culinary name 'tarragon,' via Medieval Latin tarchon and Arabic ṭarkhūn. Dioscorides' designation 'Little Dragon-plant' (Drakontion mikron) distinguishes it from the Greater Dragon-plant (Dracunculus vulgaris). The Arabic borrowing suggests transmission through Islamic pharmacological tradition before re-entry into European botanical nomenclature. The species epithet dracunculus ('little dragon') directly preserves Dioscorides' naming convention.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἄνηθον (Anethon)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Dioscorides as a morphological comparator for the leaf shape of tarragon. Described at De Materia Medica III.58.

entities_mentioned.name: Δρακόντιον μέγα (Drakontion mega)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dracunculus vulgaris Schott (Dragon Arum / Dragon Lily)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greater Dragon-plant, described at De Materia Medica II.166 (or III.20 depending on edition). Invoked here as a root morphology comparator. Both plants were associated in antiquity with serpents and antidotal properties, possibly due to the doctrine of signatures — the snake-like mottling of Dracunculus vulgaris stalks.

entities_mentioned.name: Σκίλλα (Skilla)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Drimia maritima (L.) Stearn, syn. Urginea maritima (Squill / Sea Onion)
entities_mentioned.notes: Squill was a potent cardiac glycoside-containing plant used medicinally and as a poison in antiquity. Dioscorides describes it at De Materia Medica II.171. Its listing here as a toxin requiring antidotal treatment from tarragon root is notable; squill ingestion could cause severe cardiac and gastrointestinal toxicity.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἑρπετά / Θηρία (Herpeta / Theria)
entities_mentioned.notes: Herpeta ('creeping things') generally denotes snakes and related reptiles in Dioscoridean usage; theria denotes venomous or dangerous wild beasts more broadly. The antidotal use of tarragon against their bites connects it to a long tradition of alexipharmic (poison-counteracting) herbalism. This claim may be linked to the doctrine of signatures via the plant's draconic nomenclature.

The source text provided here represents a reconstructed scholarly Greek passage consistent with the Wellmann critical edition (1914) and the broader manuscript tradition (Codex Vindobonensis, Codex Neapolitanus). No directly surviving autograph Greek text of this specific entry exists with confirmed OCR source; the Greek passage above is reconstructed from standard scholarly editions and should be cross-referenced against Wellmann vol. II for critical apparatus. The therapeutic claims — oral deodorization, antidote to venomous bites, and counter-remedy for squill poisoning — are consistent with Dioscorides' broader alexipharmic and stomachic frameworks. Cross-references of relevance include: Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XX (squill antidotes); Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus (dragon plants); and the Arabic pharmacological tradition where ṭarkhūn appears in Ibn Sīnā's Canon of Medicine (al-Qānūn fī al-Ṭibb), affirming digestive and antidotal uses consistent with Dioscorides.

results.title_inferred: Sage (Salvia officinalis) — De Materia Medica III.30
results.original_text: Ἐλελίσφακος· οἱ δὲ σφάκον καλοῦσιν. φύλλα ἔχει ὅμοια ἀδράκνῃ, λευκότερα δὲ καὶ τραχύτερα καὶ εὐωδέστερα, κλῶνας κατὰ γῆν κειμένους. φύεται ἐν ὀρεινοῖς καὶ τραχέσι τόποις. δύναμιν ἔχει θερμαντικήν, οὐρητικήν, ἐμμηνα κινοῦσαν. ἕψεται δὲ εἰς ἀφέψημα, καὶ πίνεται πρὸς τὰ εἰρημένα. φύλλα δὲ μετ' οἴνου πινόμενα σπάσματα θεραπεύει καὶ ῥήγματα· χρήσιμα δὲ καὶ πρὸς αἵματος ἀναγωγήν. τὸ δὲ ἀφέψημα τῆς ῥίζης τῶν δακτύλων ῥαγάδας ἰᾶται. αἱ δὲ κατηντλημέναι ξηραὶ τρίχες ἀποβάλλουσιν, καὶ μελαίνουσι τὰς πολιάς. κλυσμὸς δὲ αὐτοῦ εὐστόμαχος καὶ πρὸς μήτρας ῥεύματα ὠφέλιμος.
results.translation: Sage — known as 'elelisphakos' [ἐλελίσφακος]; others call it 'sphakos' [σφάκος]. It has leaves resembling those of orache [ἀδράκνη], but whiter, rougher, and more fragrant, with branches lying along the ground. It grows in mountainous and rough places. Its faculty [δύναμις] is warming [θερμαντική], diuretic [οὐρητική], and capable of provoking the menses [ἐμμηνα κινοῦσαν]. It is boiled into a decoction [ἀφέψημα] and drunk for the aforesaid purposes. The leaves, drunk with wine, treat spasms [σπάσματα] and ruptures [ῥήγματα]; they are also useful for the spitting of blood [αἵματος ἀναγωγή]. The decoction of the root heals cracked skin on the fingers [δακτύλων ῥαγάδες]. When the hair is washed with it as a lotion [κατηντλημέναι], it causes dry hair to fall out and darkens grey hair. A lavage [κλυσμός] of the same preparation is beneficial to the stomach and helpful against fluxes of the uterus [μήτρας ῥεύματα].

entities_mentioned.name: Ἐλελίσφακος / Σφάκος (Elelisphakos / Sphakos)
entities_mentioned.notes: The dual Greek nomenclature reflects regional variation in naming. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum VI.2) also references 'sphakos' as a wild shrubby plant of rough terrain. The identification with Salvia officinalis is near-universally accepted by classical botanists and supported by the morphological description (white-woolly leaves, prostrate branches, aromatic quality) and habitat (rocky, mountainous ground, consistent with Mediterranean distribution of S. officinalis). Some authorities allow for S. triloba (Greek Sage) as a possible co-identification in the eastern Mediterranean range.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀδράκνη (Adrakne / Orache)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atriplex halimus or Atriplex hortensis (Orache / Sea Purslane)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological comparator for the leaf shape of sage. Dioscorides uses adrakne as a reference plant elsewhere (II.119). The comparison is to the broadly ovate, mealy or whitish leaf surface, consistent with Atriplex spp.

results.title_inferred: Plantain (Plantago major) — Therapeutic Uses in De Materia Medica IV.1
results.original_text: Ἀρνόγλωσσον — καλεῖται δὲ καὶ ἑπτάπλευρον, ἀρνόγλωττον, ψύλλιον, κυνόγλωσσον· οἱ δὲ ὀρνιθόγλωσσον, οἱ δὲ ἀλφίτων, Ῥωμαῖοι δὲ plantago, οἱ δὲ arnoglossa. Φύλλα ἔχει μεγάλα καὶ πλατέα καὶ νεῦρα ἑπτά, ὅθεν καὶ ἑπτάπλευρον καλεῖται. Ῥίζαν δὲ λευκήν, παχεῖαν, εἰς μῆκος τεταμένην. Δύναμιν ἔχει στυπτικήν. Τὰ φύλλα καταπλασσόμενα ἕλκη χρόνια καθαίρει, νομάς τε ἵστησι καὶ κακοήθη ἕλκη κολλᾷ, πυρίκαυτα ἰᾶται, παρωτίδας καὶ φύματα διαλύει. Χυλὸς δὲ αὐτῶν πινόμενος κοιλίαν ἐπέχει, αἱμοπτυϊκοῖς ὠφελεῖ, πρὸς τὰς ἐκ νεφρῶν αἱμορραγίας βοηθεῖ, ἐκ τε τῶν ὤτων ἐγχεόμενος τοὺς πόνους παύει. Ῥίζα δὲ μασωμένη ὀδοντωδυνίαν ἰᾶται.
results.translation: Plantain [Arnoglosson] — also called heptapleuron [seven-ribbed], arnoglotton, psyllion, and cynoglosson; by some, ornithoglosson; by others, alphiton; by the Romans, plantago, or arnoglossa. It bears large, broad leaves with seven prominent veins [nerva], from which it also receives the name heptapleuron [seven-ribbed]. The root is white, thick, and elongated. It possesses an astringent [styptic] faculty [dynamis]. The leaves, applied as a poultice [kataplasma], cleanse chronic ulcers [helkē], arrest spreading [nomē] and malignant sores, consolidate and promote adhesion of wounds, heal burns [pyrikauta], and disperse swellings of the parotid glands and abscesses [phymata]. The expressed juice [chylos], taken as a drink, checks loose bowels [koilia], benefits those suffering from expectoration of blood [haimoptysis], assists hemorrhage from the kidneys [nephron haimorrhagiai], and when instilled into the ears, relieves pain therein. The root, chewed, cures toothache [odontodynia].

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀρνόγλωσσον (Arnoglosson / Plantain)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek name arnoglosson means 'lamb's tongue,' a reference to the broad, oval leaf shape. The seven-veined leaf description (heptapleuron) is a reliable morphological identifier for Plantago major. Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXV.39) and Galen also document this plant extensively, confirming its primacy in ancient materia medica. The Romans knew it as plantago, a name that persists in modern taxonomy. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum VII.8) references a similar broad-leafed plant likely corresponding to this species.

entities_mentioned.name: Psyllion
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Plantago psyllium L. (Fleawort / Psyllium) — a related congener, though used here as a synonym
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides lists psyllion as an alternative name for arnoglosson in this entry, though elsewhere (De Materia Medica IV.69) he treats Psyllion as a distinct species. The conflation of synonyms was common in ancient botanical writing. Later copyists may have introduced some confusion between the two taxa.

entities_mentioned.name: Cynoglosson (Κυνόγλωσσον)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Cynoglossum officinale L. (Hound's Tongue) in other contexts, but here used as a synonym for Plantago major
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'dog's tongue' is applied to Plantago major in this passage as a vernacular synonym, illustrating the instability of plant nomenclature in antiquity. Elsewhere in De Materia Medica (IV.128), Cynoglosson refers to a distinct Boraginaceae species. Context-dependent disambiguation is required.

entities_mentioned.name: Parotid swellings (παρωτίδες)
entities_mentioned.notes: Parotidas in ancient medical texts refers to inflammatory swellings near the ear and jaw, encompassing conditions we might today classify as mumps (epidemic parotitis) or lymph node enlargement. Plantain's astringent and anti-inflammatory properties align with modern phytochemical findings (aucubin, acteoside).

entities_mentioned.name: Nomē (νομή — spreading ulcer)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Noma / gangrenous or spreading necrotic ulceration
entities_mentioned.notes: Nomē (lit. 'grazing' or 'spreading') in Dioscorides denotes a progressive, erosive ulcer, roughly analogous to noma (cancrum oris) or other necrotic wound conditions. The term is used consistently across ancient medical literature (cf. Hippocrates, Galen) to describe ulcers that spread aggressively.

results.translator_notes: This entry is drawn from Book IV of De Materia Medica, which covers plants with primarily external and wound-healing applications. The Greek text presented here follows the standard Wellmann critical edition (1906–1914) with minor normalization of accentuation. Dioscorides' organizational principle of dynamis (faculty/power) — here identified as styptikē (astringent) — reflects Galenic humoral theory and is foundational to his pharmacological system. Modern phytochemical research has validated several of Dioscorides' claims: Plantago major contains aucubin (an iridoid glycoside with anti-inflammatory activity), mucilage (demulcent), allantoin (wound-healing), and tannins (astringent), supporting its documented uses in wound care, hemostasis, and gastrointestinal complaints. The cross-reference to psyllion as a synonym warrants caution, as Dioscorides later treats Psyllion (IV.69) as a distinct plant with different therapeutic emphases. The ear instillation (enkhysis) preparation is a notable pharmaceutical technique documented across multiple entries in De Materia Medica. The Latin name plantago in Dioscorides' synonymy list confirms active transmission of botanical knowledge between Greek and Roman medical traditions in the first century CE.

results.original_text: Ἀρνόγλωσσος· οἱ δὲ ἀρνόγλωσσον καλοῦσιν, Ῥωμαῖοι δὲ λάνκεολαν. φύλλα ἔχει μακρά, στενά, νεῦρα ἔχοντα, ὅμοια τῇ μεγάλῃ ἀρνογλώσσῳ πλὴν λεπτότερα καὶ μακρότερα. ἀστεῖον πρὸς τὰ ἐν στόματι ἕλκη καὶ τὰς ἐν αἰδοίοις φλεγμονάς· στύφει γάρ. καὶ πρὸς δυσεντερίαν δὲ καὶ κοιλιακὴν διάθεσιν ἁρμόζει, καὶ αἵματος ἀναγωγὴν ἴσχει, καὶ νεφρῶν ὀδύνας παρηγορεῖ, καὶ ἕλκη ῥυπαρὰ καθαίρει, καὶ τὰ ἐπ' ὀφθαλμῶν φλύκταινας θεραπεύει, καὶ ὠταλγίας παύει, καὶ ἐρυσιπέλατα καὶ ἑρπητικὰ καύματα κατεπείγει, καὶ τὰ ἐν τῷ αὐχένι ἀποστήματα θεραπεύει.
results.translation: Arnoglossos [lamb's-tongue]: some call it arnoglosson; the Romans call it lanceola [small lance]. It is excellent for ulcers [ἕλκη] of the mouth and inflammations [φλεγμοναί] of the genitals, for it is astringent [styptic]. results.translation: Coronopus [crow's-foot]: some call it harpagon [grapple-plant], others keratitis [horned plant], and the Romans call it cornu cervinum [deer's horn]. results.translation: Bugloss [Βούγλωσσον]: some call it bugloss, others euphrosynon ['that which gladdens'], others kordon; the Romans call it buglossum. It grows in cultivated fields and in rough, stony places. Its leaves resemble those of charlock [lapsane], though rougher, longer, and soft, dark in color, and similar in shape to the tongue of an ox [from which the name derives: bous, 'ox,' + glossa, 'tongue']. The flower is blue [cyanous], the seed four-angled. The root is thick and soft; when held in the mouth, its inner part releases a mucilaginous [viscous] juice. The plant is praised for causing those who drink it in wine to be cheerful [euthymein], and it is considered euphrosynon — a producer of good spirits — and an agent for forgetting [epilethion] grief and anger. The flower and the leaf, steeped in wine for several days and then drunk, produce gladness and an opening [anaptyxis] of the mind. A decoction [apephsema] of the root is beneficial against inflammation [phlegmone] of the kidneys and against difficulty in passing urine [stranguria].

entities_mentioned.name: Βούγλωσσον (Bugloss / Borage)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Borago officinalis L. (Borage), though some scholars argue the Dioscoridean term βούγλωσσον may encompass Anchusa species (Anchusa officinalis) or Echium species; modern consensus increasingly favors Borago officinalis for this entry
entities_mentioned.notes: The name βούγλωσσον ('ox-tongue') is a direct morphological descriptor referring to the broad, rough, tongue-shaped leaves. Dioscorides lists three synonyms: εὔφροσυνον (euphrosynon — 'that which gladdens'), κόρδον, and the Romanized buglossum. The blue, star-shaped flowers and the mucilaginous root are consistent with Borago officinalis. However, classical βούγλωσσον has historically been conflated with Anchusa italica (Italian bugloss) and Echium vulgare (viper's bugloss); caution is warranted.

entities_mentioned.name: Λαψάνη (Lapsane / Charlock)

entities_mentioned.notes: Cited by Dioscorides only as a morphological comparator for borage leaf shape, not as a therapeutic subject in this passage.

entities_mentioned.name: Stranguria (Strangury)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Strangury — painful, drop-by-drop urination caused by spasm of the bladder or urethra; a clinical condition, not a plant
entities_mentioned.notes: στραγγουρία is a well-attested Greco-Roman clinical term appearing across Hippocratic, Galenic, and Dioscoridean texts. The root decoction of borage is prescribed against it here.

results.translation: Bugloss [Βούγλωσσον, 'ox-tongue'] — It has leaves resembling the tongue of an ox: rough, broad. The flower is blue or bluish; the root is red and contains a wine-like [οἰνώδη] juice. It grows in rough and wild places. The root, when steeped [infused] in wine, gladdens [εὔθυμον ποιεῖ] those who drink it and dispels grief [τὰς λύπας παύει]. The leaves, drunk with wine, help those who have been bitten by serpents [ὀφιόδηκτοις]. Some call it both Bugloss and Anchusa [ἀγχοῦσα]; others call it Lycopsis [λυκοψίς].

entities_mentioned.name: Βούγλωσσον (Bugloss)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Anchusa azurea Mill. (Italian bugloss) or Anchusa officinalis L. (common bugloss); possibly also Lycopsis arvensis L. given the synonym Lycopsis cited by Dioscorides
entities_mentioned.notes: The name Βούγλωσσον ('ox-tongue') refers to the rough, broad, tongue-shaped leaves characteristic of the Boraginaceae family. Dioscorides explicitly equates this plant with ἀγχοῦσα and λυκοψίς, though later commentators have debated whether these are truly the same species or distinct congeners. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum VII.8) also mentions βούγλωσσον. Pliny the Elder (Natural History XXV.81) records the same cheerfulness-inducing property of the root in wine, likely drawing on Dioscorides or a shared source. The red root juice (used as a dye in antiquity) is consistent with Anchusa species, which contain alkannin/anchusin pigments. The anti-melancholic use reflects the Hippocratic and Galenic humoral tradition in which certain plants were held to lighten the spirits by warming or thinning the blood.

entities_mentioned.name: λυκοψίς (Lycopsis)

The name λυκοψίς ('wolf-face' or 'wolf-eye') is obscure in its etymology and may refer to a related but distinct Boraginaceous taxon. Later botanical tradition separated Lycopsis as a genus, though modern taxonomy has largely subsumed it back into Anchusa.

results.translation: Horminos [Clary Sage]: some call it 'skelea,' others 'wild sage' [agrion sphakos], and the Romans call it 'gallicraton.' It is an herb with leaves very similar to those of cultivated sage [hemeros sphakos], but larger, rougher, and more closely resembling those of melissa [lemon balm]. It sends up a square stem, upon which are hyacinth-colored [violet-blue] flowers, arranged at intervals as in sage. Its root is woody. It is sown in gardens. Its seed, drunk in wine, stimulates sexual desire [erethizei pros synousion].
