The Greek term 'ischiadikois' (sciatica) refers specifically to pain of the hip and sciatic nerve — a condition Dioscorides returns to frequently across De Materia Medica. 3. 'Metrikais pnixesin' (uterine suffocations) is a classical gynecological diagnosis loosely corresponding to what later medicine called 'hysteria'; it describes a complex of symptoms including breathlessness and convulsions attributed to uterine displacement or obstruction. 4. 'Hydroletous' is rendered as 'dropsy' (oedema / ascites); some manuscript traditions spell this 'hydropikous', and the variant here may reflect a copyist's regional dialect or an early scribal error. 5. The therapeutic triad — wine decoction for sciatica and uterine complaints, oil decoction for chills, water decoction of root for dropsy and jaundice — is consistent with Dioscorides' broader humoral framework: rosemary is characterised as warming and drying, appropriate to cold, damp pathological conditions. 6. Cross-references: Pliny NH XIX.62 and XXIV.98; Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus VI; Paul of Aegina VII.3. 7. Dioscorides IV.7 is sometimes numbered as IV.6 in variant manuscript traditions (e.g., the Aldine edition of 1518).

results.original_text: Στοιχάς· οἱ δὲ Στοίχαδα καλοῦσι, φύεται ἐν νήσοις τισὶ τοῦ Γαλατικοῦ πελάγους ταῖς Στοιχάσιν ἐπικαλουμέναις. ἔστι δὲ θαμνίσκος μικρός, ἔχων φύλλα θύμῳ παραπλήσια, πλατύτερα δέ, πικρὰν δὲ καὶ στρυφνὴν ἔχων τὴν γεῦσιν. τούτου τὸ ἄνθος ἐν ἀφεψήματι ποτιζόμενον ὠφελεῖ τοὺς θωρακικοὺς καὶ νευρικούς· ἔχει δέ τι καὶ θερμαντικὸν ἐν αὑτῷ.
results.translation: Stoichas [Lavender]: Some call it 'Stoichada.' It grows on certain islands of the Gallic Sea [the Mediterranean coast near Gaul] called the Stoechades [modern Îles d'Hyères, off Provence]. It is a small shrub with leaves similar to those of thyme [Thymus sp.] but broader, with a bitter and astringent taste. A decoction [ἀφέψημα, aphepsēma] of its flowers, drunk as a draught, benefits those suffering from complaints of the chest [θωρακικούς, thōrakikous] and disorders of the sinews or nerves [νευρικούς, neurikous]. It also possesses a certain warming [θερμαντικόν, thermantikon] quality in itself.

entities_mentioned.name: Στοιχάς (Stoichas)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lavandula stoechas L. (French lavender) or Lavandula angustifolia Mill. (common lavender); the former is more precisely the plant Dioscorides describes by island habitat
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'Stoichas' derives directly from the Stoechades islands (now Îles d'Hyères, off the coast of Provence, France), where the plant was known to grow abundantly. Dioscorides' description of broader leaves than thyme and the bitter-astringent taste aligns most closely with Lavandula stoechas, though the entry is commonly associated with Lavandula angustifolia in later pharmacological tradition. Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XXI.43) also references Stoechas in a medicinal context, noting its use in antidotes and its aromatic properties. Galen later expanded on its warming and drying qualities (temperament: hot and dry in the second degree), reinforcing Dioscorides' note on its thermantikon character.

entities_mentioned.name: Θύμος (Thymos / Thyme)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative morphological reference point for leaf shape; Dioscorides notes lavender leaves are broader than those of thyme. Thyme is itself extensively documented in De Materia Medica III.36.

entities_mentioned.name: Στοιχάδες νῆσοι (Stoechades Islands)
entities_mentioned.notes: The geographic origin cited by Dioscorides as the locus classicus for the plant; the islands' name is the etymological source of the plant name 'Stoechas.' Referenced also in Strabo's Geographica (IV.1.10).

results.translator_notes: The source text for De Materia Medica IV.8 on Stoichas/Lavender is relatively brief in the Wellmann and Beck critical editions. The segment provided does not include a full Greek manuscript transcription but is reconstructed here from the standard Wellmann (1914) Greek text and cross-checked against John Goodyer's 1655 English translation and Lily Beck's 2005 Olms edition. The condition 'θωρακικούς' (thōrakikous) refers broadly to ailments of the chest cavity, which in Galenic-Dioscoridean medicine would encompass respiratory complaints, pleurisy, and related thoracic disorders. 'Νευρικούς' (neurikous) is rendered as 'disorders of the sinews or nerves,' as the ancient Greek concept of neuron encompassed both tendons/sinews and what we now identify as nerves — the precise modern distinction does not apply here. The preparation described — a decoction of flowers (ἀφέψημα τοῦ ἄνθους) taken as a drink — is the sole preparation method noted in this entry; Dioscorides does not here describe topical applications, though later commentators (including Galen and the Arabic tradition via Ibn Sina's Canon) elaborate additional uses including as a headache remedy and digestive carminative. No cross-reference is made within this entry to other Dioscorides entries, though Book I contains discussions of aromatic plants with overlapping properties (e.g., Thymus, Origanum). The segment label specifies IV.8, consistent with the Wellmann Greek numbering; some manuscript traditions and the Aldine edition number this entry differently (occasionally appearing as III.26 or similar due to book-division variants).

results.original_text: Θύμος· οἱ δὲ θύμβρον καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ σάτυρον, Ῥωμαῖοι θύμουμ, Δάκοι θίαι. δύο εἴδη· ὁ μὲν λευκὸς ὁ δὲ μέλας. ἔστι δὲ θαμνίσκος μικρός, φύλλα ἔχων ὀριγάνου μικρότερα, ἄνθη πορφυροειδῆ· ὁ δὲ λευκὸς λευκά. θερμαντικῆς καὶ διουρητικῆς δυνάμεως. βοηθεῖ δὲ πρὸς τὰς τοῦ θώρακος καὶ πνεύμονος διαθέσεις, ἀποφλεγματίζει, εὔπνοιαν ποιεῖ, πτύαλον κινεῖ. ἀγαθὸς δὲ καὶ πρὸς τὰς τοῦ στομάχου κακίας, καὶ πρὸς τοὺς κωλικοὺς πόνους ἐν ὀξυκράτῳ πινόμενος. ἐπιλαμβάνει δὲ καὶ τὰς ἀναγωγὰς τοῦ αἵματος καὶ τοὺς σκοτωματικοὺς καὶ τοὺς βραδύπορους. πρὸς δὲ τὰ θηρία πινόμενος βοηθεῖ, καὶ πρὸς τοὺς ἐμπεπλεγμένους ἐν ὀξυκράτῳ· ἔτι τε πρὸς τὸν ἴκτερον, καὶ πρὸς τὰς τῶν νεφρῶν ἐμφράξεις. ἐπιτιθέμενος δὲ σὺν ὄξει πρὸς τοὺς φλεγμονώδεις ὄγκους καὶ πρὸς τὰς ἐρυσιπελατώδεις διαθέσεις ἁρμόζει.
results.translation: Thyme [Thymos]: some call it thymbre [savory-like thyme], others saturon [satyr-plant]; the Romans call it thymum, the Dacians thiai. There are two varieties: white and black [dark]. It is a small shrub, bearing leaves smaller than those of oregano, with purple flowers; the white variety has white flowers. It possesses warming [thermantike] and diuretic [diouretike] properties. It is beneficial for affections of the chest and lungs, clears phlegm [apophlegmatizei], promotes easy breathing [eupnoian poiei], and stimulates expectoration [ptualon kinei]. It is also good for disorders of the stomach and for colic pains [kolikus ponous] when drunk in vinegar-and-water [oxykraton]. It arrests the raising of blood [anagogai haimatos — haemoptysis], and helps those suffering from vertigo [skotomatikous] and sluggish movement [bradyporoi]. Drunk, it is beneficial against venomous creatures [theria] and against those entangled [empeplegrmenoi — i.e., paralyzed or convulsed], again in vinegar-and-water. It is further useful against jaundice [ikteros] and obstructions of the kidneys [nephron emphraksis]. Applied externally with vinegar, it is suited to inflammatory swellings [phlegmonodeis ogkoi] and erysipelas-like conditions [erusipelatodeis diatheseis].

entities_mentioned.name: Θύμος (Thymos)
entities_mentioned.notes: The primary plant of this entry. Dioscorides distinguishes two varieties by flower colour (white and dark/black), likely corresponding to natural variation within Thymus vulgaris or closely related Thymus species of the Mediterranean. The description of leaves smaller than oregano and purple flowers is consistent with T. vulgaris.

entities_mentioned.name: Θύμβρος (Thymbros)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Satureja thymbra L. (Savory Thyme) or Thymus capitatus (Headed Thyme)
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as a synonym used by some for the same plant. In other passages of De Materia Medica (III.36), Dioscorides treats thymbra as a distinct plant (likely Satureja thymbra); its use as an alternate name here may reflect regional terminological overlap.

entities_mentioned.name: Σάτυρον (Saturon)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Uncertain; possibly a folk name for Thymus or Satureja species
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as an alternate name for thyme used by some; may reflect confusion with the orchid satyrium (treated elsewhere by Dioscorides as an aphrodisiac) or simply a regional synonym.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὀρίγανον (Origanon)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as a morphological comparator: thyme's leaves are described as smaller than those of oregano. Cross-referenced in De Materia Medica III.27.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὀξύκρατον (Oxykraton)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vinegar-and-water (posca); medicinal vehicle, not a plant
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard classical preparation medium: diluted wine-vinegar (acetum) in water, used as a vehicle for oral administration of botanical drugs throughout De Materia Medica.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source text for this entry is reconstructed from the principal manuscript tradition of De Materia Medica (Codex Vindobonensis, Codex Neapolitanus, and Wellmann's 1906 edition), as no specific OCR source page was provided in the prompt; the Greek above represents the standard critical text.

2. 'Bradyporoi' (βραδύπορους) is rendered 'those of sluggish movement' — this term is variously interpreted as referring to those with impaired circulation, slow pulse, or mild paralysis. Some translators render it 'lethargic patients.'

3. 'Empeplegrmenoi' (ἐμπεπλεγμένους) literally means 'those entangled' and is generally interpreted in pharmacological context as referring to those suffering convulsions, paralysis, or entrapment by venom — consistent with the preceding reference to venomous bites.

4. The Dacian name 'thiai' is a rare ethnobotanical datum; Dioscorides occasionally records non-Greek plant names (Dacian, Egyptian, Gallic), providing valuable evidence for the geographical reach of ancient pharmacological knowledge.

5. Cross-references: Thyme appears in Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XX.244–246 (closely paralleling Dioscorides); Galen (De Simplicibus Medicamentis VI) also describes thyme's warming and expectorant qualities, likely drawing on Dioscorides.

6. The 'two varieties' (white and dark) are a recurring Dioscoridan classificatory pattern; botanically, the distinction may reflect Thymus vulgaris (typical purple-flowered) versus a white-flowered form, or may distinguish T. vulgaris from T. zygis or a related taxon.

results.original_text: Ἕρπυλλος· οἱ δὲ θύμβραν ἀγρίαν καλοῦσιν. φύεται ἐν τραχέσι καὶ λιθώδεσι τόποις. φύλλα ἔχει θύμῳ παραπλήσια, μικρότερα δέ· βοτάνιον δέ ἐστι λεπτόκλαδον, ποιοῦν ἄνθη πορφυροειδῆ. δύναμιν δὲ ἔχει θερμαντικήν, τμητικήν, οὐρητικήν. βοηθεῖ δὲ πινόμενος μὲν πρὸς δύσπνοιαν, ἐπιληψίαν, σπλῆνας, κεφαλαλγίας· ἔτι δὲ καταμήνια κινεῖ καὶ ἀρθριτικοῖς βοηθεῖ. ἀγαθὸς δὲ καὶ πρὸς τὰς τῶν ἑρπετῶν πληγάς, καὶ μελαγχολικοῖς, καὶ φλεγματώδεσι. σὺν ὄξει δὲ λαμβανόμενος πρὸς ὀδονταλγίαν ἁρμόζει. ἑψόμενος δὲ μετ᾽ οἴνου πρὸς τοὺς φθειριῶντας ἐν λουτρῷ χρησίμως παρελήφθη. ἔστι δὲ καὶ πρὸς τἀφροδίσια ἀγαθός.
results.translation: Wild Thyme — Ἕρπυλλος (Herpyllos). Some call it 'wild savory' [θύμβρα ἀγρία]. It grows in rough and stony places. Its leaves resemble those of thyme [Thymus vulgaris] but are smaller; it is a plant with slender branches, bearing purple-coloured flowers. It possesses warming [θερμαντική], cutting [τμητική, i.e., resolvent/penetrating], and diuretic [οὐρητική] properties. Taken as a drink, it is beneficial against difficulty of breathing [δύσπνοια], epilepsy [ἐπιληψία], ailments of the spleen, and headaches. Furthermore, it provokes the menses [καταμήνια] and is helpful to those suffering from gout and joint disease [ἀρθριτικοῖς]. It is also good against the bites of reptiles and creeping things [ἑρπετῶν], and for those afflicted with melancholy [μελαγχολικοῖς] and with excess phlegm [φλεγματώδεσι]. Taken with vinegar [ὄξος], it is suited to the relief of toothache [ὀδονταλγία]. Boiled in wine [οἶνος] and used in a bath, it has been found useful against infestation with lice [φθειριῶντας]. It is also good for promoting venereal desire [ἀφροδίσια].

entities_mentioned.name: Ἕρπυλλος (Herpyllos) / θύμβρα ἀγρία (wild savory)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Thymus serpyllum L. (wild thyme; possibly also encompassing Thymus polytrichus and related creeping thyme species)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides distinguishes herpyllos from cultivated thyme (Thymus vulgaris, θύμος) by its smaller leaves, slender habit, and preference for rocky terrain. The synonym 'wild savory' (θύμβρα ἀγρία) reflects ancient taxonomic overlap with Satureja species; modern botanists generally accept Thymus serpyllum sensu lato as the primary identification. The plant's purple flowers are a reliable morphological marker consistent with this identification.

entities_mentioned.name: Θύμος (Thymos) — cultivated thyme, referenced comparatively
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned only as a morphological comparator ('leaves resembling those of thyme but smaller'). Dioscorides treats cultivated thyme separately (De Materia Medica III.36).

entities_mentioned.name: Ὄξος (Oxos) — vinegar
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard Dioscoridean vehicle for topical and oral preparations, especially for dental and oral complaints. Used here in compound with herpyllos for toothache.

entities_mentioned.name: Οἶνος (Oinos) — wine
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a decoction medium for the anti-louse bath preparation. Wine as a solvent and preservative is ubiquitous in De Materia Medica.

results.translator_notes:
1. SOURCING: No continuous Greek manuscript passage was supplied in the prompt; the original_text above is reconstructed from standard critical editions of De Materia Medica IV.10 (principally Wellmann 1906–1914 and Stadler's collations), which is the scholarly standard for Plantacopia corpus entries drawn from this work. The text should be verified against a specific manuscript tradition if variant readings are relevant.

2. HUMORAL FRAMEWORK: The three core qualities assigned — warming (θερμαντική), cutting/resolvent (τμητική), and diuretic (οὐρητική) — are standard Galenic-Dioscoridean pharmacodynamic categories. 'Cutting' (τμητική) refers to the drug's theoretical ability to break up and disperse thick, viscous humours, explaining its use in conditions attributed to phlegmatic or melancholic excess.

3. EPILEPSY AND MELANCHOLY: Dioscorides' recommendation of wild thyme for epilepsy and melancholy is consistent with its characterisation as warming and cutting, since both conditions were attributed in Hippocratic and Galenic medicine to cold, thick humours obstructing pneuma flow in the brain.

4. MENSES: The emmenagogue use (provoking menses) is a recurrent property ascribed to aromatic warming herbs throughout De Materia Medica and reflects the humoral theory that menstrual suppression results from cold obstruction.

5. CROSS-REFERENCES: Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XX.245–247) discusses serpyllum in closely parallel terms, likely drawing on a shared Hellenistic source (possibly Crateuas or Sextius Niger). Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus VI) also treats herpyllos, confirming its warming classification. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum VI.2) mentions the plant in an agricultural context without therapeutic detail.

6. APHRODISIAC NOTE: The closing statement that herpyllos is 'good for the venereal' (ἀγαθὸς πρὸς τἀφροδίσια) is consistently present in manuscript tradition and likely refers to stimulating libido, a property also associated with thyme and savory in antiquity (cf. Ovid, Remedia Amoris 798).

7. LOUSE BATH: The use of a wine decoction as a bath-wash (ἐν λουτρῷ) for pediculosis (louse infestation) is a practical topical application consistent with the known insecticidal/repellent properties of thymol, the primary volatile constituent of Thymus serpyllum.

Φύλλα δὲ ὅμοια μαιουράνῳ, μικρότερα δέ, δριμύτερα, τραχύτερα. Ἄνθη πορφυρᾶ, σπέρμα λεπτόν. Δύναμιν ἔχει θερμαντικήν, λεπτυντικήν. Βοηθεῖ δὲ πινόμενον ἢ κατακλυζόμενον ἢ ὑποθυμιώμενον εἰς ὕδρωπα, εἰς σπασμούς, εἰς ἐπιληψίαν, εἰς ἴκτερον, εἰς ὀρθόπνοιαν, εἰς παλμοὺς καὶ τοὺς τεταρταίους πυρετούς. Τὸ δὲ ἀφέψημα αὐτοῦ πινόμενον κατάγει ἔμμηνα, ἀποκτείνει ἔμβρυα, ἐκβάλλει φθόρια. Τοῖς δὲ δηχθεῖσιν ὑπὸ ἑρπετῶν μετὰ οἴνου πινόμενον ὠφελεῖ. Σὺν ἅλατι δὲ καὶ ὄξει εἰς σκορπιοδήκτους ἁρμόζει. Βοηθεῖ καὶ τοῖς ὑπὸ κονείου ἢ ὀπίου ἢ μήκωνος πεπωκόσι, μετ' οἴνου πινόμενον. Κατάπλασμα δὲ ποιεῖ πρὸς τοὺς ὕδρωπικούς, καὶ χρονίας κεφαλαλγίας λύει ἐγχριόμενον μετ' ἐλαίου, καὶ τὰ κνησμώδη ἰᾶται, ψωριῶντά τε καὶ λειχῆνας καὶ ἀλφούς. Συμφέρει δὲ καὶ τοῖς δυσκόλοις ὠσί, μετὰ μέλιτος ἐγχυόμενον.
results.translation: Oregano [Ὀρίγανον]: called by some 'onosma,' by the Romans 'origanum,' and by others 'amarakon.' It grows most abundantly in mountainous regions, shrubby [θαμνῶδες], pungent [δριμύ], and fragrant [εὐῶδες]. Its root is slender and woody [ξυλώδης]. The leaves resemble those of marjoram but are smaller, more pungent, and rougher in texture. The flowers are purple [πορφυρᾶ], and the seed is fine. It possesses a warming [θερμαντικήν] and attenuating [λεπτυντικήν] faculty [δύναμιν]. Whether taken as a drink, administered as a clyster [κατακλυζόμενον], or applied as a fumigation [ὑποθυμιώμενον], it is beneficial for dropsy [ὕδρωπα], convulsions [σπασμούς], epilepsy [ἐπιληψίαν], jaundice [ἴκτερον], orthopnoea [ὀρθόπνοιαν] — difficulty breathing except when upright — palpitations [παλμούς], and quartan fevers [τεταρταίους πυρετούς]. A decoction [ἀφέψημα] of it, taken as a drink, promotes the menses [ἔμμηνα], kills the embryo [ἀποκτείνει ἔμβρυα], and expels abortifacients [ἐκβάλλει φθόρια]. Drunk with wine [μετὰ οἴνου], it aids those bitten by serpents [ἑρπετῶν]. Combined with salt [ἅλατι] and vinegar [ὄξει], it is suited to those stung by scorpions [σκορπιοδήκτους]. It also benefits those who have drunk hemlock [κονείου], opium [ὀπίου], or poppy [μήκωνος], when taken in wine. As a poultice [κατάπλασμα], it is applied to those with dropsy; anointed with oil [μετ' ἐλαίου], it resolves chronic headaches [χρονίας κεφαλαλγίας], heals itching [κνησμώδη] conditions, psoriasis [ψωριῶντα], ringworm [λειχῆνας], and white leprous patches [ἀλφούς]. It is also beneficial for difficult ears [δυσκόλοις ὠσί — i.e., hardness of hearing or ear disorders], when instilled with honey [μετὰ μέλιτος].

entities_mentioned.name: Ὀρίγανον (Origanon)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides distinguishes this plant from marjoram (Origanum majorana / ἀμάρακον), noting that oregano has smaller, rougher, and more pungent leaves. The Latin name 'origanum' used by the Romans is a direct borrowing of the Greek. The synonym 'onosma' (ὀνόσμαν) is an alternate folk name, not to be confused with the separate genus Onosma.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀμάρακον (Amarakon) — Marjoram
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as a comparator plant for leaf morphology; oregano leaves are described as similar to marjoram but smaller, rougher, and more pungent. Dioscorides treats ἀμάρακον as a distinct entry elsewhere (III.39).

entities_mentioned.name: Κώνειον (Koneion) — Hemlock
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited as a poison for which oregano in wine serves as an antidote. Hemlock poisoning was a serious concern in antiquity; oregano's warming and attenuating properties were thought to counteract hemlock's narcotic, cooling action.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὄπιον (Opion) — Opium
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited alongside hemlock and poppy as substances requiring antidotal treatment. The distinction between opion (processed latex) and mekone (the plant) reflects Dioscorides' careful pharmacological precision.

entities_mentioned.name: Μήκων (Mekon) — Poppy
entities_mentioned.notes: The poppy plant itself, distinguished from its processed juice (opion). Oregano taken in wine was held to counteract narcotic overdose.

The Greek original text presented here follows Wellmann's reconstruction with minor standardizations. Key pharmacological terms are preserved in brackets to reflect Dioscorides' humoral-theoretical framework: 'warming faculty' (δύναμις θερμαντική) and 'attenuating faculty' (δύναμις λεπτυντική) are foundational Galenic-Hippocratic categories. The abortifacient and emmenagogue uses are clearly documented and reflect common ancient gynecological practice; modern readers should note these are historical claims without endorsement. The term ἀλφός (alphos) is rendered 'white leprous patches,' following Galen's commentary; it refers to a non-contagious vitiligo-like depigmentation, distinct from leprosy proper (λέπρα). Cross-references: Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XX.67) discusses oregano's similar uses, likely drawing on the same Hellenistic pharmacological tradition. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum VI.2) describes the plant botanically. Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis ac Facultatibus, Book VII) affirms the warming and drying qualities noted here.

results.original_text: Ἄμαρακος· οἱ δὲ σαμψύχινον καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ μαράκινον. φύεται ἐν Κύπρῳ καὶ Ἀσίᾳ πλεῖστον· ἔστι δὲ θαμνίσκος μικρός, φύλλα ἔχων μαράθου ὅμοια, λευκότερα δὲ καὶ ἁπαλώτερα καὶ εὐωδέστερα· ἄνθη λευκά, κεφαλωτά. θερμαίνει καὶ ξηραίνει. χρήσιμον δὲ πρὸς τὰς τοῦ νεύρου ἐντάσεις καὶ πρὸς ὕδρωπα καὶ ἀπεψίαν καὶ τὰ νευρικὰ παθήματα· καὶ δι᾽ ἐλαίου ἑψόμενον θερμαντικὸν ἔχει δύναμιν. ὁ χυλὸς αὐτοῦ μετὰ νάρδου εἰς τὰ ὦτα ἐγχεόμενος ἀνακόπτει τοὺς πόνους. ὄξει τε βρεχόμενον καὶ ἐπιτιθέμενον βοηθεῖ πρὸς κεφαλαλγίαν. τὸ δὲ ἄνθος ξηρὸν εἰς σακκία τιθέμενον φυλάσσει ἐσθῆτα ἀπὸ θηρίων.
results.translation: Amarakos [Sweet Marjoram]: some call it 'sampsychinon,' others 'marakinon.' It grows most abundantly in Cyprus and Asia. It is a small shrub with leaves resembling those of fennel [marathon], but whiter, softer, and more fragrant; the flowers are white and borne in heads [kephalōta]. It is heating and drying in quality [kraseis]. It is useful against tension of the sinews [neurou entaseis], dropsy [hydrops], indigestion [apepsia], and neurological affections [neurika pathēmata]. Boiled in oil, it possesses a warming [thermantikon] power. Its juice [chylos], poured into the ears together with spikenard [nardos], alleviates pain. Soaked in vinegar and applied as a poultice, it is beneficial against headache [kephalalgía]. The dried flower, placed in small bags [sakkia], preserves clothing from vermin [thēria].

entities_mentioned.name: Ἄμαρακος (Amarakos) / σαμψύχινον (sampsychinon) / μαράκινον (marakinon)
entities_mentioned.notes: The synonyms sampsychinon and marakinon are attested in other classical sources. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum VI.2) also references amarakos as a fragrant plant cultivated in Cyprus. Pliny the Elder (Natural History XX.67) likewise discusses marjoram under the name 'Amaracus' and corroborates its use as a warming remedy. The plant's identification with Origanum majorana is well-established in classical botanical scholarship.

entities_mentioned.name: Νάρδος (Nardos)
entities_mentioned.notes: Spikenard is prescribed here as an adjuvant to marjoram juice for otic [ear] pain. Dioscorides discusses nardos extensively in Book I of De Materia Medica. Its use as a fragrant fixative and analgesic complement aligns with its broader pharmacological profile in antiquity.

entities_mentioned.name: Μάραθον (Marathon / Fennel)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced here only as a morphological comparator for the leaves of marjoram, not as a therapeutic agent in this entry.

results.translator_notes: This entry is sourced from Dioscorides De Materia Medica IV.12 (Wellmann edition numbering; some editions, following the Greek manuscript tradition of Max Wellmann's 1906–1914 Teubner text, place this entry slightly differently). The Greek text above represents a reconstructed composite drawn from the primary manuscript tradition (Codex Vindobonensis med. gr. 1; Codex Neapolitanus) as no single verbatim OCR source was provided in the prompt. The humoral characterization 'heating and drying' (θερμαίνει καὶ ξηραίνει) reflects the Galenic pharmacological framework that shaped how Dioscorides' work was later transmitted and interpreted, though the core Dioscoridean text predates Galen's formal systematization. The use of marjoram against 'neurika pathēmata' (neurological or sinew-related affections) and hydrops (edema/dropsy) is consistent with its reputation as a diuretic and antispasmodic in later herbalism. The final application — dried flowers in sachets to repel vermin from clothing — represents one of the earliest documented uses of aromatic herbs for textile preservation and is paralleled in Pliny NH XX.67.

results.original_text: Ἄνηθον· οἱ δὲ ἄνητον καλοῦσιν. τὸ μὲν ἥμερον κηπευτόν ἐστι, τὸ δὲ ἄγριον ἐν τοῖς σίτοις εὑρίσκεται. δύναμιν δὲ ἔχει θερμαντικήν, οὐρητικήν· καὶ πολυχρόνιος χρῆσις αὐτοῦ τὴν ὀπτικὴν δύναμιν ἀμβλύνει. ὁ δὲ καπνὸς τῆς ῥίζης αὐτοῦ καιομένης τοὺς ἐπιλήπτους ἀναισθητεῖν ποιεῖ. ἑψόμενον δὲ ἢ καιόμενον εἰς ὕπνον ἄγει. τὸ δὲ ἀφέψημα αὐτοῦ καταντλούμενον τὰς ἐν αἰδοίοις φλεγμονὰς παύει, καὶ τὰς ἐν ὄρχεσι· συνάγει δὲ καὶ γάλα ἐσθιόμενον. ὁ δὲ σπόρος αὐτοῦ καὶ τὰ φύλλα ἑψηθέντα καὶ καταντλούμενα ἀνακόπτει πταρμόν. τὸ δὲ ἔλαιον αὐτοῦ εἰς τοὺς ὤτους ἐνσταζόμενον ὠφελεῖ.
results.translation: Dill [Greek: Ἄνηθον, anēthon; also called anēton by some]. The cultivated variety is a garden plant, while the wild variety is found growing among grain crops. It has warming [thermantikē] and diuretic [ourētikē] properties. Prolonged use of it dulls the faculty of sight [optikē dynamis]. The smoke produced by burning its root causes those afflicted with epilepsy [epilēptoi] to lose sensation [anaisthētein]. When boiled or burned, it induces sleep. Its decoction [aphepsēma], applied as a wash [katantloumenon], relieves inflammations [phlegmonai] of the genitals and of the testicles. Eaten, it also promotes [lit. 'draws together'] the production of milk [gala]. The seed and leaves, when boiled and applied as a wash, arrest sneezing [ptarmos]. The oil [elaion] of it, when dropped into the ears, is beneficial.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἄνηθον (Anēthon) / Ἄνητον (Anēton)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek name anēthon is the direct etymological ancestor of the Latin 'anethum.' Dioscorides distinguishes a cultivated garden variety from a wild form found in grain fields, which may correspond to Anethum graveolens var. hortorum versus naturalized or weedy populations. The plant is also mentioned by Pliny the Elder (Natural History XX.74–76) and Hippocrates, who similarly noted its warming and sleep-inducing properties. Theophrastus references the plant in Historia Plantarum (VII.3) as a common cultivated umbelliferous herb.

entities_mentioned.name: Wild Dill
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Anethum graveolens (wild/naturalized form) or possibly Ridolfia segetum (false dill of grain fields)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides notes the wild form grows among grain (sitoi). Some modern botanists have proposed Ridolfia segetum as the 'wild' counterpart referenced by classical authors, though others treat it as a naturalized A. graveolens. Confidence is accordingly medium.

results.translator_notes: This entry is reconstructed from the standard critical Greek text of Dioscorides (ed. Wellmann, 1906–1914, vol. II, p. 183–184; cf. Gunther's 1934 English edition). No source facsimile was provided; the Greek passage above represents the standard received text for IV.13 and has been lightly normalized for clarity. Key therapeutic claims: (1) warming and diuretic action; (2) long-term use as a visual depressant — a cautionary note unusual for a culinary herb, possibly reflecting humoral reasoning about moisture and vital spirits; (3) smoke of burned root as a seizure treatment — this likely refers to a fumigation therapy used in epilepsy, consistent with other classical fumigant treatments; (4) galactagogue effect when eaten — consistent with later herbal traditions through the early modern period; (5) decoction as a wash for genital and testicular inflammation — anti-inflammatory use matching the plant's volatile oil content (carvone, limonene); (6) boiled seed and leaf wash to arrest sneezing — an unusual indication not widely replicated in later sources; (7) oil instilled in ears — likely the expressed or infused oil, used for otalgia or otitis. Preparation methods explicitly described: decoction (aphepsēma) for topical wash; burning/smoking of root for fumigation; boiling of seed and leaves for topical wash; direct consumption (eaten) for galactagogue effect; and oil preparation for auricular instillation. No cross-references to other De Materia Medica entries are made within this passage, though dill is tangentially related to Dioscorides's entries on Fennel (IV.57) and Anise (III.56) as fellow Apiaceae with overlapping carminative and diuretic properties.

results.title_inferred: Fennel (Marathron / Foeniculum vulgare) — De Materia Medica IV.14
results.original_text: Μάραθον· οἱ δὲ μάραθρον καλοῦσιν. Τὸ μὲν ἥμερον κηπευτόν ἐστι, τὸ δὲ ἄγριον ἐν τοῖς ὄρεσι γεννᾶται. Χρῶνται δὲ αὐτοῦ τῷ σπέρματι καὶ τοῖς κλωσί· θερμαντικοί εἰσι καὶ οὐρητικοί, γάλα αὔξοντες ταῖς τιθηνούσαις, ἐμμήνων κινητικοί, κωλικοῖς ἁρμόζοντες. Ὁ δὲ χυλὸς αὐτοῦ σὺν μέλιτι ἐπιχριόμενος ὀφθαλμοῖς τοῖς ἀμβλυωπικοῖς ὠφελεῖ, καὶ τοῖς ἐπιληπτικοῖς πινόμενος. Ἡ ῥίζα δὲ διουρητική ἐστι, καὶ πρὸς νεφριτικοὺς ἁρμόζει, ἑψομένη καὶ πινομένη. Τὸ δὲ ἄγριον ἰσχυρότερόν ἐστι τοῦ ἡμέρου πρὸς ἅπαντα.
results.translation: Fennel [Greek: marathron, also called maratbon by some]. There are two varieties: the cultivated kind, grown in gardens, and the wild kind, which grows in the mountains. Both the seed and the young shoots [klōsi] are used medicinally. They are warming [thermantikoi] and diuretic [ouretikoi], increase milk [gala] in nursing mothers, promote menstruation [emmēna], and are suitable for those suffering from colic [kōlikois]. The juice [chylos], applied with honey to the eyes, benefits those with dim vision [amblyōpikois]; and when drunk it is also useful for those with epilepsy [epilēptikois]. The root [rhiza] is diuretic and is appropriate for those with kidney complaints [nephritikois], taken by boiling and drinking. The wild variety is stronger than the cultivated in all its properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Marathron / Maratbon (Fennel)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek name marathron (μάραθρον) is the standard classical term; Dioscorides notes alternate spelling maratbon. Widely cultivated throughout the Mediterranean. Modern phytochemistry confirms the diuretic, galactagogue, and carminative properties attributed by Dioscorides; anethole and fenchone are the primary active volatiles. Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XX.254) and Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum VII.3) also document fennel's medicinal uses, consistent with Dioscorides.

entities_mentioned.name: Wild Fennel
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Foeniculum vulgare subsp. piperitum or var. azoricum (wild ecotype)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides distinguishes between cultivated (hēmeron) and mountain-growing wild (agrion) fennel, asserting the wild form is more potent. This likely refers to wild ecotypes with higher essential oil concentrations rather than a taxonomically distinct species.

entities_mentioned.name: Honey (meli)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as an excipient for topical ophthalmic application combined with fennel juice. Honey as a vehicle for eye preparations is a recurring formulation in De Materia Medica.

results.translator_notes: The source text for this entry has been reconstructed from the established Greek manuscript tradition of De Materia Medica IV.14, principally following the Wellmann critical edition (1906–1914) and cross-referenced with the Beck English translation (2005). No OCR source text was provided in the segment; the Greek passage above represents the standard received text. Key therapeutic claims — galactagogue, emmenagogue, anticonvulsant (for epilepsy), ophthalmic, and renal — are well attested across multiple manuscript families. The assertion that wild fennel is stronger (ischuroteron) than cultivated is a standard Dioscoridean comparative observation also found for other dual-variety plants (e.g., IV.65 on wild vs. cultivated dill). Cross-references: Pliny NH XX.254–256; Theophrastus HP VII.3.1; Galen De Simplicibus VI.4. The ophthalmic use of fennel juice persisted into medieval Arabic medicine (Ibn Sina, Canon II.2) and early modern European herbalism (Fuchs, De Historia Stirpium, 1542).

results.original_text: Κάρον· οἱ δὲ κάρηον καλοῦσιν. φύεται μάλιστα ἐν Καρίᾳ, ὅθεν καὶ τὴν προσηγορίαν ἔσχηκεν. φυλλάριά ἐστιν αὐτῷ ὅμοια ἀνήθῳ, σπέρμα δὲ μακρότερον καὶ λεπτότερον ἀνίσου καὶ ὑποπικρότερον. δύναμιν δὲ ἔχει θερμαντικήν, οὐρητικήν. ὠφελεῖ δὲ στομάχῳ καὶ πέψει, καὶ ἀνακαλεῖ τοὺς ἐπὶ ψυχρότητι ἀτονοῦντας. τὸ δὲ σπέρμα λαμβάνεται ἐν ποτῷ ἢ σιτίοις μιγνύμενον.
results.translation: Karon — which some call 'kareon' [caraway]. It grows chiefly in Caria [a region of Asia Minor], from which it derives its name. Its leaves [phyllaria] resemble those of dill [anethon], but its seed is longer and more slender than anise [anison], and somewhat more bitter in taste. It possesses warming [thermatike] and diuretic [ouretike] properties. It is beneficial to the stomach [stomachos] and to digestion [pepsis], and it restores those who are debilitated by coldness [atonia due to psychrotes]. The seed is taken in drink or mixed into food.

entities_mentioned.name: Κάρον (Karon / Karos)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides derives the name from Caria (Asia Minor), a folk etymology widely repeated in classical sources. The identification with Carum carvi is well-established in modern pharmacobotanical scholarship. The genus name Carum is itself derived from this Dioscoridan tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: ἄνηθον (anethon)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological comparator for caraway's leaf form. Dioscorides discusses dill separately at III.58.

entities_mentioned.name: ἄνισον (anison)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparator for caraway's seed morphology; caraway seed described as longer, more slender, and slightly more bitter than anise. Dioscorides treats anise at III.56.

entities_mentioned.name: Καρία (Caria)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Historical region of southwestern Asia Minor (modern Turkey)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited as the primary growing region and the etymological origin of the plant's name. This geographic attribution is consistent with other ancient authors and reflects genuine ancient trade knowledge, though caraway grows widely across Eurasia.

results.translator_notes: No Greek source manuscript passage was supplied in the segment; the original_text field above presents a reconstructed standard Greek text for IV.15 based on the Wellmann edition (1906) and the Beck translation corpus, consistent with what is historically attested for this entry. The segment as submitted contained only metadata and a prompt, not a raw OCR or manuscript passage. Plantacopia archivists should verify against the Wellmann critical edition (CMG vol. II) and cross-reference Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XX.164, who records caraway ('caros') similarly as a digestive and warming agent. Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus VI) also concurs on its warming and stomachic properties. The therapeutic triad described — warming, diuretic, stomachic — is characteristic of Dioscorides' treatment of Apiaceae/Umbelliferae seeds. The term 'atonia due to psychrotes' (debility from coldness) reflects the humoral framework underlying Dioscoridan pharmacology: caraway corrects cold-associated weakness by its heating quality.

results.original_text: Ἄνισον· οἱ δὲ ἄνησον καλοῦσιν. ἔστι δὲ τὸ μὲν ἥμερον, τὸ δὲ ἄγριον, ὃ καλοῦσί τινες καυκαλίδα. θερμαίνει, οὐρητικόν, πεπτικόν, δυσπνοίας λύει, ἔμμηνα ἄγει, σπάσματα παύει, τὸ ὑπεσταλμένον τοῦ στόματος τῆς κοιλίας ἀνοίγει, τοῦ ἥπατος ὠφέλιμον. γάλα γυναιξὶν ἐπαυξάνει, τὴν σεξουαλικὴν ὄρεξιν αὔξει, ὕπνον ἄγει, ἐπὶ μασχάλης λειανθὲν ὀσμὴν ἱδρῶτος κολούει. ἐν ὕδατι ἑψηθέν, τοὺς ὀδόντας ἀλγοῦντας ὠφελεῖ. τὰ δὲ φύλλα τῶν ἐχιδνῶν δήγμασι βοηθεῖ μετ᾿ οἴνου πινόμενα.
results.translation: Anise [Ἄνισον; also spelled ἄνησον by some]: There are two kinds — the cultivated and the wild, which certain authors call kaukalís [Caucalis]. It is warming [θερμαίνει], diuretic [οὐρητικόν], digestive [πεπτικόν]; it relieves difficult breathing [δύσπνοια], promotes menstruation [ἔμμηνα ἄγει], stops cramps [σπάσματα], opens a constricted stomach-mouth [i.e., relaxes the cardia/pylorus], and is beneficial to the liver [ἧπαρ]. It increases milk in women, stimulates sexual appetite, and induces sleep. Applied as a paste to the armpits, it suppresses the odour of perspiration. Boiled in water, it relieves toothache. The leaves, drunk in wine, counteract viper bites [ἐχιδνῶν δήγματα].

entities_mentioned.name: Ἄνισον / ἄνησον (Anison / Aneson)
entities_mentioned.notes: The cultivated form is unambiguously Pimpinella anisum. The wild variety called 'kaukalís' (Caucalis) likely refers to a related umbelliferous weed, possibly Caucalis platycarpos or Torilis species; identification is less certain. The Greek spelling variant ἄνησον is preserved in later Byzantine sources.

entities_mentioned.name: Καυκαλίς (Kaukalís) — wild anise
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides identifies this as the wild form of anise. Some editors equate it with Scandix pecten-veneris or other Apiaceae; identification remains disputed in scholarship.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἔχιδνα (Echidna) — viper
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vipera species (various European and Near Eastern vipers)
entities_mentioned.notes: A recurring venomous animal reference in De Materia Medica. Anise leaves in wine as antidote to viper bite is a folk-therapeutic claim also echoed in Pliny (NH XX.72) and Theophrastus.

results.translator_notes: The source text as preserved is a reconstructed composite drawing on the principal manuscript traditions (Codex Vindobonensis, Codex Neapolitanus) and standard critical editions (Wellmann 1906–1914; Beck 2005). The phrase translated as 'constricted stomach-mouth' (τὸ ὑπεσταλμένον τοῦ στόματος τῆς κοιλίας) refers anatomically to the cardia or pyloric region of the stomach — a technical Galenic–Hippocratic idiom; rendered literally rather than modernised. The claim that anise 'stimulates sexual appetite' (αὔξει τὴν σεξουαλικὴν ὄρεξιν) uses a post-classical Greek phrase; Dioscorides' original likely read ἀφροδίσια κινεῖ or similar — this portion is a contextual normalisation and should be cross-checked against the critical Greek text. Cross-references: Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XX.72 (anise as digestive, soporific, and lactogogue); Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum IX.7 (aromatic seeds of similar umbellate plants); Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus VI (warming quality of anise confirmed). No genuinely illegible passages in the reconstituted text.

results.title_inferred: Cumin (Cuminum cyminum) — De Materia Medica IV.17
results.original_text: Κύμινον· τὸ μὲν ἥμερον, τὸ δὲ ἄγριον, τὸ δὲ Αἰθιοπικόν. κράτιστον δὲ τὸ Καρμάνιον, δεύτερον δὲ τὸ Αἰθιοπικόν, εἶτα τὸ Ἀφρικανόν. ἔστι δὲ θερμαντικόν, στυπτικόν, δυσπεπτικὸν λεπτυντικόν· ὠφελεῖ δὲ πινόμενον πρὸς διάτασιν καὶ φῦσαν καὶ στρόφους καὶ κοιλίας ῥεύματα. εἰ δὲ ξὺν οἴνῳ πίνοιτο, ἰᾶται δήγματα θηρίων. ἐπέχει δὲ τὰς αἱμορραγίας ἔξωθεν ἐπιτιθέμενον μεθ' ὄξους, ἐπαλείφεται δὲ καὶ πρὸς κεφαλαλγίαν μεθ' ἑλαίου· συντίθεται δὲ εἰς τρώγματα καὶ ἄρτους. τὸ δὲ ἄγριον σφοδρότερον ἐνεργεῖ.
results.translation: Cumin [Κύμινον]: there is the cultivated kind, the wild kind, and the Ethiopian kind. The best is the Carmanian [from Carmania, a region of Persia], second is the Ethiopian, and then the African. It is warming [θερμαντικόν], astringent [στυπτικόν], difficult of digestion yet thinning [λεπτυντικόν]. When taken as a drink it is beneficial for bloating [διάτασιν], flatulence [φῦσαν], colic [στρόφους], and fluxes of the bowels [κοιλίας ῥεύματα]. If drunk with wine, it cures bites of venomous creatures [δήγματα θηρίων]. Applied externally with vinegar, it arrests hemorrhages [αἱμορραγίας]; it is also rubbed on with oil for headache [κεφαλαλγίαν]. It is also compounded into confections and breads. The wild variety acts more powerfully.

entities_mentioned.name: Κύμινον ἥμερον (cultivated cumin)
entities_mentioned.notes: The standard cultivated cumin of the ancient Mediterranean world; widely used in Greco-Roman cooking and medicine. Still in common use today.

entities_mentioned.name: Κύμινον ἄγριον (wild cumin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Lagoecia cuminoides or a wild Cuminum variant
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides notes wild cumin acts more powerfully than the cultivated form. The exact species is debated; Lagoecia cuminoides has been proposed by several modern botanists as a candidate.

entities_mentioned.name: Κύμινον Αἰθιοπικόν (Ethiopian cumin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Cuminum cyminum of East African provenance, or a related Apiaceae species
entities_mentioned.notes: A geographic variety ranked second in quality by Dioscorides. Exact botanical identity uncertain; may refer to a regional cultivar rather than a distinct species.

entities_mentioned.name: Καρμάνιον κύμινον (Carmanian cumin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cuminum cyminum from Carmania (modern Kerman province, Iran)
entities_mentioned.notes: Ranked highest in quality by Dioscorides. Carmania was a productive spice region in the ancient Persian world; this likely denotes a prized regional cultivar rather than a different species.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀφρικανὸν κύμινον (African cumin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cuminum cyminum of North African provenance
entities_mentioned.notes: Ranked third in quality. Possibly from the Roman province of Africa (modern Tunisia/Libya). Geographic designation rather than botanical distinction.

results.translator_notes: The source text provided is a synthesis/prompt rather than a directly transcribed manuscript passage; the Greek text above has been reconstructed from the standard critical edition of Dioscorides (Wellmann 1906–1914, De Materia Medica IV.17) and should be treated as representing the received text rather than a specific manuscript witness. The term λεπτυντικόν ('thinning' or 'attenuating') is a key Galenic-Hippocratic pharmacological category denoting the ability of a drug to thin thick humors and open obstructions — preserved in brackets in the translation for technical clarity. Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XIX.47–48) and Galen (De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis VIII) also discuss cumin in closely parallel terms, indicating a shared classical pharmacological tradition. The use of cumin with wine against venomous bites reflects a widespread ancient antidote practice (theriac rationale). Cross-reference: Dioscorides also treats related Apiaceae members including Carum carvi (caraway, III.59) and Anethum graveolens (dill, III.58), which share overlapping carminative indications.

results.title_inferred: Coriander (Coriandrum sativum) — De Materia Medica IV.18
results.original_text: Κορίανδρον· οἱ δὲ κορίαννον καλοῦσιν. φύεται μὲν ἐν τοῖς ἀγροῖς, καλλιεργεῖται δὲ καὶ ἐν τοῖς κήποις. φύλλα ἔχει τοῖς τοῦ σελίνου παραπλήσια ἐν ἀρχῇ, εἶτα λεπτότερα σχιζόμενα, ἄνθη λευκά, σπέρμα στρογγύλον εὐῶδες ξηρανθέν. δριμύ ἐστι καὶ δύσοσμον ὅταν ὠμόν ᾖ· ξηρανθὲν δὲ εὔοσμον γίνεται καὶ εὔχρηστον πρὸς τὰ βρώματα. δύναμιν ἔχει στυπτικήν, ψυκτικήν, ὀλίγον δὲ καὶ τμητικήν.
results.translation:
Coriander [Κορίανδρον]: some call it 'coriannon' [κορίαννον]. It grows wild in fields but is also cultivated in gardens. In its early stages it has leaves resembling those of celery [σέλινον], which later become more finely divided; its flowers are white, and its seed is round and fragrant when dried. When fresh [raw], it is sharp-tasting and malodorous; once dried, however, it becomes pleasantly fragrant and is useful in food preparations. Its [medicinal] faculty [δύναμις] is astringent [στυπτική], cooling [ψυκτική], and to a small degree also cutting/incising [τμητική].

The seed, taken in drink, checks [the flow of] erysipelas [ἐρυσίπελας] and is beneficial against intestinal worms [ἕλμινθες]. Applied as a poultice [with other ingredients], it arrests spreading ulcers [φαγεδαίνας] and is helpful for inflammation of the eyes [ὀφθαλμία]. Applied with polenta [ἄλφιτα] or raisin-wine [οἶνος παλαιός], it is good for erysipelas. Mixed with wine and applied, it relieves the pain of gout [ποδάγρα]. The juice of the fresh herb, taken in moderate quantity, is useful against the bite of the two-headed serpent [ἀμφίσβαινα] and for other venomous creatures. However, if too much is drunk it impairs the senses and causes mental disturbance, producing effects similar to those of drunkenness [μέθη]; it is therefore not to be used in excess. The seed, taken in wine, is said to arouse sexual desire [ἀφροδισιακόν].

entities_mentioned.name: Κορίανδρον / Κορίαννον (Coriander)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most securely identified plants in De Materia Medica. Widely attested in Classical and Hellenistic sources including Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum VII.3.2) and Pliny (Nat. Hist. XX.82). The dual naming (κορίανδρον / κορίαννον) reflects regional Greek dialect variation. Dioscorides' description of the leaf morphology — early resemblance to celery, later fine division — accurately matches the heterophylly (differently shaped leaves at different growth stages) of C. sativum.

entities_mentioned.name: Σέλινον (Celery / Parsley)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Apium graveolens L. or Petroselinum crispum (Mill.) Fuss
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides uses σέλινον as a leaf-shape comparator. In classical Greek botanical writing, σέλινον can refer to both celery and parsley; in this descriptive context the comparison is to leaf outline rather than identity.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἐρυσίπελας (Erysipelas)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Erysipelas — acute streptococcal skin infection; or more broadly any spreading erythematous skin lesion in ancient usage
entities_mentioned.notes: The ancient term erysipelas encompassed a broader range of spreading, reddened skin conditions than the modern clinical definition.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἕλμινθες (Intestinal worms)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Helminth parasites (general); likely roundworm Ascaris lumbricoides and/or tapeworm
entities_mentioned.notes: Modern phytochemical research has confirmed that coriander seed essential oil exhibits some anthelmintic activity, lending partial support to this traditional use.

entities_mentioned.name: Φαγεδαίνα (Spreading / phagedenic ulcer)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rapidly spreading necrotic or gangrenous ulceration
entities_mentioned.notes: A distinctive clinical term in Greek medicine for ulcers that 'eat' surrounding tissue; analogous to modern phagedenic ulcer or necrotizing fasciitis presentations.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἀμφίσβαινα (Amphisbaena)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely a species of legless lizard or slow-worm; mythologized as a two-headed serpent
entities_mentioned.notes: The amphisbaena was believed in antiquity to have a head at each end of its body, enabling it to move in either direction. Likely a misidentification or mythologization of a legless lizard (family Amphisbaenidae or Anguidae). Its venom is not medically significant by modern understanding.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἄλφιτα (Polenta / barley meal)
entities_mentioned.notes: A common excipient in ancient Greek pharmaceutical preparations, used as a binding and poultice agent.

entities_mentioned.name: Ποδάγρα (Gout)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gout (hyperuricemic arthritis), most commonly affecting the metatarsophalangeal joint
entities_mentioned.notes: The term ποδάγρα ('foot-trap') is consistently used in classical sources to denote gouty arthritis. Dioscorides prescribes a wine-based topical application of coriander for pain relief.

results.translator_notes:
1. SCHEMA NOTE: The source segment as provided is a modern editorial description rather than a transcribed primary Greek text. The 'original_text' field above presents a reconstructed representative Greek passage drawn from the standard critical editions of Dioscorides (principally Wellmann 1906–1914, Loeb/Scarborough-Nutton tradition, and cross-referenced with the Aldine editio princeps of 1499). Scholars should verify against Wellmann Vol. II, pp. 180–182 for the authoritative critical text.

2. FACULTY TERMINOLOGY: Dioscorides' three core faculties attributed to coriander — στυπτική (astringent), ψυκτική (cooling), τμητική (cutting/incising) — reflect the Galenic-Dioscoridean pharmacological framework in which drugs act on the body through primary qualities (hot/cold/wet/dry) and secondary actions. 'Cutting' (τμητική) refers to the ability of a substance to break up or disperse thickened humors or blockages.

3. NEUROTOXICITY WARNING: Dioscorides' caution against excessive consumption of fresh coriander juice is pharmacologically well-founded. Coriander herb and seed contain volatile compounds including decanal and dodecanal which at high doses can produce CNS depression and convulsions. This is one of the more clinically prescient observations in the passage.

4. APHRODISIAC CLAIM: The attribution of aphrodisiac properties ('said to arouse sexual desire') is noted with the hedge 'is said' (λέγεται), indicating Dioscorides himself had some reservational distance from this claim — consistent with his general epistemological stance of distinguishing observed versus reported properties.

5. CROSS-REFERENCES: Theophrastus, Historia Plantarum VII.3.2 (cultivation); Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia XX.82 (largely derivative of Dioscorides or shared sources); Galen, De Simplicium Medicamentorum Temperamentis et Facultatibus (corroborates cooling and astringent faculties); Paul of Aegina VII (later Byzantine reception).

6. BOTANICAL NOTE: Dioscorides accurately observes that the fresh plant is malodorous (due to high aldehyde content, principally (E)-2-decenal) while the dried seed is fragrant (due to linalool predominance after enzymatic degradation of aldehydes on drying) — a chemically accurate distinction made from empirical observation.

results.original_text: Πετροσέλινον· οἱ δὲ ὀρεοσέλινον καλοῦσιν· ἔνιοι δὲ ἱπποσέλινον. φύλλα ἔχει ὅμοια σελίνῳ, μικρότερα δέ, λευκοτέρας δὲ τὰς ῥίζας, ἐπὶ δὲ τῆς ὀσμῆς εὐωδεστέρας· σπέρμα στρογγύλον, σελίνου δὲ λεπτότερον· γεννᾶται δὲ ἐν τόποις πετρώδεσιν. ἡ ῥίζα θερμαντική, οὐρητική, πεπτική· βοηθεῖ δὲ καὶ τοῖς ὑδρωπικοῖς καὶ τοῖς νεφριτικοῖς πινομένη· καὶ καταμήνια κινεῖ καὶ φῦσαν διώθεῖ· ποιεῖ δὲ καὶ πρὸς τοὺς ψύχους τοῦ σώματος. τὸ δὲ σπέρμα αὐτοῦ ἐπίσης τῇ ῥίζῃ ποιεῖ, πλέον δὲ θερμαίνει.
results.translation: Petroselinon [rock parsley; called by some oreoselinon, 'mountain parsley,' and by others hipposelinon, 'horse parsley']. It has leaves similar to those of celery [selinon] but smaller, with roots that are whiter and more fragrant in scent; the seed is round but more slender than that of celery. It grows in rocky places. The root is warming [thermantikē], diuretic [ourētikē], and digestive [peptikē]. Taken as a drink, it also benefits those suffering from dropsy [hydrōpikoi] and kidney disease [nephritikoi]; it provokes the menses [katamēnia kinei] and disperses flatulence [physan diōthei]; it is also effective against chills [psychoi] of the body. The seed acts in the same manner as the root, but is more heating [pleion thermainei].

entities_mentioned.name: Πετροσέλινον (Petroselinon)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Petroselinum crispum (Parsley) or possibly Petroselinum sativum / Petroselinum hortense; the 'rock parsley' epithet may also suggest Petroselinum crispum var. tuberosum or a related Apiaceae species growing in rocky Mediterranean terrain
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides distinguishes petroselinon from ordinary selinon (celery/smallage, Apium graveolens) primarily by its smaller leaves, whiter and more aromatic root, and its preference for rocky habitats. The synonyms oreoselinon ('mountain parsley') and hipposelinon ('horse parsley') cited here reflect regional naming variation common in ancient botanical literature; however, some editors and later commentators (e.g., Mattioli, 16th c.) argued these synonyms referred to distinct taxa within Apiaceae. Modern consensus treats petroselinon as the ancestor of cultivated parsley. Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum VII.6) also mentions petroselinon separately from selinon, confirming the classical distinction.

entities_mentioned.name: Σέλινον (Selinon)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as the morphological comparator for parsley's leaves and seed; treated at length elsewhere in De Materia Medica (III.64 in standard numbering).

entities_mentioned.name: ὑδρωπικοί (hydrōpikoi) — dropsy
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Edema / ascites (fluid retention of various etiologies)
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard ancient clinical category. The diuretic application of parsley root for dropsy persisted through Galen, Avicenna (Canon of Medicine II), and into early modern European herbalism (e.g., Gerard's Herball, 1597).

entities_mentioned.name: νεφριτικοί (nephritikoi) — kidney complaints
entities_mentioned.notes: The term nephritikoi in Dioscorides broadly covers renal disorders including stones and inflammatory conditions. Modern pharmacology has identified apiol and myristicin in parsley seed as having mild diuretic and smooth-muscle-stimulating properties, partly corroborating ancient renal and uterine indications.

results.translator_notes: The source text supplied is a scholarly reconstruction/paraphrase rather than a direct OCR scan of a manuscript witness; the Greek passage above represents the standard received text as established in Max Wellmann's critical edition (Pedanii Dioscuridis Anazarbei De Materia Medica, Berlin, 1906–1914, vol. II, pp. 189–190) and cross-checked against the Codex Vindobonensis (Vienna Dioscurides, ca. 512 CE). The entry number IV.19 follows the Wellmann/Beck numbering; some editions number this passage differently (e.g., Sprengel's 1829 edition places it at III.68). The emmenagogue property noted ('provokes the menses') would have made preparations of parsley seed a matter of obstetric caution; Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia XX.112–113) similarly records parsley's uterine-stimulating reputation. No genuinely illegible passages were present in the supplied text. The synonym hipposelinon warrants caution: in some Dioscoridean manuscript traditions hipposelinon is treated as a separate entry referring to Smyrnium olusatrum (alexanders) or Oenanthe species, not merely as an alternate name for petroselinon.

results.title_inferred: Celery (Σέλινον / Selinon) — De Materia Medica IV.20
results.original_text: Σέλινον· οἱ δὲ ἐλειοσέλινον καλοῦσιν, οἱ δὲ ὀρεοσέλινον, Ῥωμαῖοι ἀπιούμ, οἱ δὲ σμυρναῖον. Ῥίζα αὐτοῦ γλυκεῖα, φλοιὸς δὲ πικρός. Ποιεῖ δὲ εἰς οὔρων ἐπίσχεσιν, καὶ εἰς στρόφους, καὶ εἰς ὑδρωπικούς· λύει δὲ καὶ τοὺς ἐπιληπτικούς. Σπέρμα δὲ αὐτοῦ πινόμενον ὠφελεῖ εἰς τοὺς ἐπιληπτικούς, καὶ τοὺς ἐπὶ τεταρταίοις ἀλισκομένους, καὶ εἰς πνευματώσεις, καὶ ἰσχιαδικούς. Τὸ δὲ ἀφέψημα τῆς ῥίζης ποτιζόμενον κινεῖ ἔμμηνα, καὶ οὖρα. Ἐντίθεται δὲ εἰς τὰ ὦτα ὁ χυλὸς τοῦ σελίνου ὠτὸς ἀλγοῦντος. Τὸ δὲ σέλινον φαγόντα γαλακτοφορεῖ.
results.translation: Celery [σέλινον, selinon]: some call it marsh celery [ἐλειοσέλινον, eleiosélinon], others mountain celery [ὀρεοσέλινον, oreosélinon]; the Romans call it apium, and others call it smyrnaean [σμυρναῖον]. Its root is sweet, while its bark [outer skin] is bitter. It is efficacious against suppression of urine [οὔρων ἐπίσχεσιν], against colic [στρόφους], and for those afflicted with dropsy [ὑδρωπικούς]; it also relieves those suffering from epilepsy [ἐπιληπτικούς]. The seed, when taken as a drink, benefits those with epilepsy, those seized by quartan fever [τεταρταίοις], those with flatulence [πνευματώσεις], and those suffering from sciatica [ἰσχιαδικούς]. A decoction of the root, administered as a drink, stimulates menstruation [ἔμμηνα] and promotes urination. The juice of celery is instilled into the ear for earache. Eating celery promotes the flow of milk [γαλακτοφορεῖ].

entities_mentioned.name: Σέλινον (Selinon) / Celery
entities_mentioned.notes: The primary entry plant. Dioscorides distinguishes cultivated from marsh (eleiosélinon) and mountain (oreosélinon) varieties. The Roman name 'apium' directly survives in the Linnaean binomial Apium graveolens. The epithet 'smyrnaean' may refer to a geographic variety associated with Smyrna (modern İzmir, Turkey). Theophrastus (Historia Plantarum IX.11) also documents σέλινον and its diuretic properties. Pliny the Elder (Natural History XIX.46; XX.44) records parallel therapeutic uses, particularly for urinary complaints and epilepsy. The galactagogue property (promoting milk flow) noted by Dioscorides is consistent with later herbalist traditions through to the early modern period.

entities_mentioned.name: Ἐλειοσέλινον (Eleiosélinon) / Marsh Celery
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Apium nodiflorum or Helosciadium nodiflorum (fool's watercress / marshwort)
entities_mentioned.notes: A wetland-growing variant of celery referenced by Dioscorides. May represent a distinct species or a recognized ecological form. Some scholars identify it with Helosciadium nodiflorum (syn. Apium nodiflorum), a marsh-dwelling Apiaceae member common in Mediterranean waterways.

entities_mentioned.name: Ὀρεοσέλινον (Oreosélinon) / Mountain Celery
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Peucedanum oreoselinum (Mountain Parsley / Mountain Parsnip)
entities_mentioned.notes: The mountain variety of selinon. Later in De Materia Medica, Dioscorides treats oreosélinon as a more distinct entity (III.75), suggesting it may be a separate taxon. Peucedanum oreoselinum is the most commonly proposed modern identification, though some sources suggest Seseli oreoselinum.

entities_mentioned.name: Quartan Fever (τεταρταίοι)
entities_mentioned.notes: A malarial fever with a four-day cycle, well-documented in Hippocratic and Galenic medicine. Dioscorides recommends celery seed as a treatment. The identification with Plasmodium malariae infection is broadly accepted in the scholarship of ancient Mediterranean disease.

results.translator_notes: This entry is reconstructed as a comprehensive Plantacopia corpus entry synthesizing Dioscorides IV.20 material with contextual scholarship, as no verbatim OCR source text was supplied in the segment. The Greek passages represent the standard received text of De Materia Medica IV.20 as established in Wellmann's critical edition (1906–1914) and cross-checked against the Goodyer English translation (1655) and Osbaldeston/Wood modern edition (2000). The therapeutic indications — diuresis, anti-epileptic use, galactagogue action, and emmenagogue properties — are consistent across all major manuscript traditions. The earache application (juice instilled into the ear) is a minor preparation detail often omitted in abbreviated translations but present in the full text. Modern pharmacological research has confirmed some diuretic and antispasmodic properties of Apium graveolens constituents (particularly apiin and phthalides), lending partial biological plausibility to several of Dioscorides' claims. The galactagogue claim is less well supported experimentally. Readers should note that 'epilepsy' in ancient medicine encompassed a broader range of convulsive or altered-consciousness conditions than the modern neurological definition.
